question: A . . . . . instrument rating is issued for all aeroplanes where the flight test was conducted in a single engine aeroplane.
explanation: Standard 421 – Flight Crew Permits, Licences and Ratings An instrument rating is issued for aircraft in one of the following groups:

question: A low level air route is classified as
answers: Class E Airspace and extends upwards from the surface of the earth.
Class E Airspace and extends upward from 2,200 ft AGL up to, but not including, 18,000 ft ASL.
Class G Airspace and extends upwards from the surface of the earth.
Class G Airspace and extends upward from 2,200 ft AGL up to, but not including, 18,000 ft ASL.
explanation: Designated Airspace Handbook Low-level air routes are contained within Class G airspace. They are quite similar to low-level airways except air routes extend upwards from the surface of the earth, are not controlled, and ATC separation is not provided to IFR or VFR aircraft. Their lateral dimensions are identical to those of a low-level airway.

question: ATC provided speed adjustments should be maintained within
explanation: AIM RAC 9.7.3 Speed Adjustment – Radar-Controlled Aircraft To assist with radar vectoring, it is sometimes necessary to issue speed adjustments. While ATC will take every precaution not to request speeds beyond the capability of the aircraft, it is the pilot’s responsibility to ensure that the aircraft is not operated at an unsafe speed. If ATC issues a speed reduction that is inconsistent with safe operation, the pilot must inform ATC when unable to comply. Speed adjustment will be expressed in units of 10 KIAS or multiples of 10 KIAS. Pilots complying with a speed adjustment are expected to maintain a speed within 10 KIAS of the specified speed.

question: Where ATC has not assigned a transponder code, IFR flight in high level controlled airspace shall have a transponder code of
explanation: AIM COM 8.2 Transponder Requirements Where ATC has not assigned a transponder code, IFR flight in low-level controlled or uncontrolled airspace (below 18,000 feet) shall have a transponder code of 1000 to reply on Mode A and on Mode C if available. Where ATC has not assigned a transponder code, IFR flight in high-level controlled or uncontrolled airspace (18,000 feet and above) shall have a transponder code of 2000 to reply on Mode A and on Mode C if available.

question: No holder of a Canadian pilot licence endorsed with an instrument rating shall exercise the privileges of the instrument rating unless, within six months before the flight, the holder has acquired . . . . . of instrument time.
explanation: CAR 401.05 Recency Requirements No holder of a Canadian pilot licence endorsed with an instrument rating shall exercise the privileges of the instrument rating unless, within six months before the flight, the holder has: acquired six hours of instrument time; and completed six instrument approaches in an aircraft in actual or simulated instrument meteorological conditions, or an approved flight training device configured for the same category as the aircraft.

question: A pilot operating in the standard pressure region, shall set the altimeter to . . . . . immediately before commencing a descent for the purpose of landing.
answers: the altimeter setting of the destination aerodrome
the altimeter setting of the nearest aerodrome
explanation: CAR 602.35, 602.36 & 602.37 Altimeter-setting and Operating Procedures The standard pressure region encompasses all airspace over Canada at or above 18,000 feet ASL (high-level airspace) and all low-level airspace outside the lateral limits of the altimeter setting region. Regardless of whether an aircraft is operating in the altimeter setting region or the standard pressure region, the altimeter should be set to the aerodrome’s altimeter setting, if available, immediately before commencing descent for landing. Hatched and shaded areas depicting the horizontal and vertical extent of the Standard Pressure Region

question: A pilot transitioning from the altimeter setting region to the standard pressure region shall set their altimeter to 29.92 in. Hg
answers: prior to entering the standard pressure region.
five minutes before entering the standard pressure region.
after entering the standard pressure region.
after receiving authorization to do so from ATC.
explanation: CAR 602.35, 602.36 & 602.37 Altimeter-setting and Operating Procedures The altimeter setting region is an airspace of defined dimensions below 18,000 feet ASL. The standard pressure region encompasses all airspace over Canada at or above 18,000 feet ASL (high-level airspace) and all low-level airspace outside the lateral limits of the altimeter setting region. Whenever transitioning between the altimeter setting and standard pressure regions, make changes to the altimeter while in the standard pressure region. For example, if descending from the standard pressure region into the altimeter setting region make the change to the local altimeter setting just before entering the altimeter setting region.

question: An instrument proficiency check can be completed every . . . . . in order to maintain IFR currency.
explanation: Advisory Circular (AC) No. 401-005 In order to maintain recency, within 24 months preceding the flight a pilot must have completed an instrument rating flight test or an instrument proficiency check.

question: The pilot of an aircraft departing an aerodrome within the Standard Pressure Region shall set the aircraft altimeter to 29.92 in. Hg
answers: prior to reaching the intended flight level.
explanation: CAR 602.35, 602.36 & 602.37 Altimeter-setting and Operating Procedures In the Standard Pressure Region, before reaching the flight level at which the flight is to be conducted, set the altimeter to 29.92 inches of mercury or 1,013.2 millibars.

question: A pilot transitioning from the standard pressure region to the altimeter setting region shall set the altimeter to the nearest station
answers: within five minutes of entering the altimeter setting region.
after entering the altimeter setting region.
before entering the altimeter setting region.
explanation: CAR 602.35, 602.36 & 602.37 Altimeter-Setting and Operating Procedures Whenever transitioning between the altimeter setting and standard pressure regions, make changes to the altimeter while in the standard pressure region. For example, if descending from the standard pressure region into the altimeter setting region make the change to the local altimeter setting just prior to entering the altimeter setting region.

question: The airspace associated with T-routes extends upward from
explanation: AIM RAC 11.4.4 Fixed RNAV Routes T-routes are low-level controlled fixed RNAV routes depicted on En-Route Low Altitude charts and require GNSS RNAV systems for use. The airspace associated with T-routes extends upward from 2,200 feet AGL , 10 NM on either side of the centreline, and does not splay. The MOCA provides obstacle protection for only 6 NM on either side of the track centreline and does not splay.

question: A . . . . . instrument rating is issued for all aeroplanes where the flight test was conducted in a multi-engine aeroplane.
explanation: Standard 421 – Flight Crew Permits, Licences and Ratings An instrument rating is issued for aircraft in one of the following groups:

question: Aircraft operating IFR that are unable to set the current altimeter setting when it is greater than 31.00 in. Hg
answers: are restricted to VFR weather conditions.
may only be authorized to conduct visual and contact approaches.
shall set the altimeter to 31.00 in. Hg and a correction factor shall be applied to DH or MDA.
shall set the altimeter to 31.00 in. Hg and the DH or MDA will be deemed to have been reached when the published altitude is displayed on the altimeter.
explanation: AIP ENR 1.7 Altimeter-Setting Procedures Cold dry air masses can produce barometric pressures in excess of 31.00 in. Hg. Because barometric readings of 31.00 in. Hg or higher rarely occur, most standard altimeters do not permit the setting of barometric pressures above that level and are not calibrated to indicate accurate aircraft altitude above 31.00 in. Hg. As a result, most altimeters cannot be set to provide accurate altitude readouts to the pilot in these situations. ATC will issue actual altimeter settings and will confirm with the pilot that 31.00 in. Hg is set on the pilot’s altimeters for enroute operations below 18,000 feet ASL in the affected areas. Aerodromes that are unable to accurately measure barometric pressures above 31.00 in. Hg will report the barometric pressure as “in excess of 31.00 in. Hg”. Flight operations to and from those aerodromes are restricted to VFR weather conditions. When the barometric pressure exceeds 31.00 in. Hg, the following procedures take effect: Altimeters of all IFR, controlled VFR flight (CVFR) and VFR aircraft are to be set to 31.00 in. Hg for enroute operations below 18,000 feet ASL. At the beginning of the final approach segment, the current altimeter setting will be set by those aircraft capable of such a setting. Aircraft that are unable to set altimeter settings above 31.00 in. Hg will retain a 31.00 in. Hg setting throughout the entire approach. Aircraft on departure or missed approach will set 31.00 in. Hg prior to reaching any mandatory or fix crossing altitude, or 1,500 feet above ground level (AGL), whichever is lower. For aircraft operating IFR that are unable to set the current altimeter setting, the following restrictions apply: To determine the suitability of departure alternate aerodromes, destination aerodromes and destination alternate aerodromes, increase the ceiling requirements by 100 feet and visibility requirements by 1/4 statute mile (SM) for each 1/10 inch of mercury, or any portion thereof, over 31.00 in. Hg. Example: Destination altimeter setting is 31.28 inches, instrument landing system (ILS) decision height (DH) is 250 feet (200-1/2). When flight planning, add 300-3/4 to the weather requirements, which would now become 500-1 1/4. During the instrument approach, 31.00 in. Hg will remain set. DH or Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA) will be deemed to have been reached when the published altitude is displayed on the altimeter.

question: Prior to taxiing for departure from an aerodrome with an elevation of 550 feet, an aircraft’s altimeters indicate 580 feet and 645 feet. Is it possible for a flight to be conducted under IFR below RVSM airspace?
answers: Yes, since only one of the two altimeters need to be serviceable.
No, since both altimeters must be within the prescribed tolerance.
Yes, but only if a correction factor is applied to the incorrect indication.
Yes, since both altimeters are within acceptable limits.
explanation: AIM AIR 1.5 Pressure Altimeter An aircraft altimeter which has the current altimeter setting applied to the subscale should not have an error of more than ±50 feet when compared on the ground against a known aerodrome or runway elevation. If the error is more than ±50 feet, the altimeter should be checked by maintenance. However, since the aircraft is operating below RVSM airspace, only one of the two altimeters must be serviceable to fly under IFR.

question: The holder of a pilot licence endorsed with a Group 2 instrument rating may, under IFR act as a PIC of
answers: any aircraft certified for flight with two crew members.
explanation: Standard 421 – Flight Crew Permits, Licences and Ratings – Canadian Aviation Regulations (CARs) An instrument rating is issued for aircraft in one of the following groups: Group 1 for all aeroplanes where the flight test was conducted in a multi-engine aeroplane; Group 2 for multi-engine centerline thrust and single-engine aeroplanes where the flight test was conducted in a multi-engine centerline thrust aeroplane; Group 3 for single-engine aeroplanes where the flight test was conducted in a single-engine aeroplane; and Group 4 for helicopters where the flight test was conducted in a helicopter.

question: Which of the following statements is true regarding aircraft operating in the altimeter setting region?
answers: When climbing from the altimeter setting region into the standard pressure region, set the altimeter to standard pressure prior to entering the standard pressure region.
Before reaching the flight level at which the flight is to be conducted, set the altimeter to 29.92 in. Hg.
Immediately prior to reaching the altitude at which flight is to be conducted, the altimeter shall be set to standard pressure.
Before conducting a takeoff, set the altimeter to the elevation of the aerodrome if the altimeter setting is not available.
explanation: CAR 602.35, 602.36 & 602.37 Altimeter-setting and Operating Procedures The altimeter setting region is an airspace of defined dimensions below 18,000 feet ASL. The standard pressure region encompasses all airspace over Canada at or above 18,000 feet ASL (high-level airspace) and all low-level airspace outside the lateral limits of the altimeter setting region. Prior to departure in the altimeter setting region and the standard pressure region, pilots must set the aircraft altimeter to the current altimeter setting of that aerodrome or, if that altimeter setting is not available, it must be set to the elevation of the aerodrome.

question: . . . . . are low-level uncontrolled fixed RNAV routes.
explanation: AIM RAC 11.4.4 Fixed RNAV Routes L-routes are low-level uncontrolled fixed RNAV routes depicted on En-Route Low Altitude charts using green dashed lines and require GNSS RNAV systems for use. The MOCA provides obstacle protection for only 6 NM either side of the track centreline and does not splay.

question: Pilots operating under IFR and approaching an MF shall broadcast their intentions on . . . . . prior to switching to the MF.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.13 IFR Procedures at an Uncontrolled Aerodrome in Uncontrolled Airspace Pilots operating under IFR in uncontrolled airspace should, whenever practical, monitor 126.7 MHz and broadcast their intentions on this frequency immediately prior to changing altitude or commencing an approach. Therefore, when arriving at an aerodrome where another frequency is designated as the MF, descent and approach intentions should be broadcast on 126.7 MHz before changing to the MF. If conflicting IFR traffic becomes evident, this change should be delayed until the conflict is resolved.

question: The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft destined to an airport which underlies controlled low-level airspace and for which there is not a published instrument approach procedure
answers: will be cleared to descend out of controlled airspace and informed of the appropriate minimum IFR altitude.
must advise ATC of the pilot's intended route and landing runway.
must remain within controlled airspace unless cancelling IFR.
will be provided with a visual approach clearance within 15 NM of the destination aerodrome.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.3 Approach Clearance Aircraft destined to airports which underlie controlled low-level airspace and for which there is not a published instrument approach procedure will be cleared to descend out of controlled airspace and informed of the appropriate minimum IFR altitude.

question: When the weather conditions at an uncontrolled aerodrome could permit VFR circuit operations, pilots operating aircraft under IFR
answers: are expected to approach and land on the active runway that may be established by the aircraft operating in the VFR circuit.
have priority over all VFR traffic.
are prohibited from attempting an instrument approach procedure.
must execute a circling manoeuvre after completing an instrument approach procedure.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.12 IFR Reporting Procedures at Uncontrolled Aerodromes When the weather conditions at an uncontrolled aerodrome could permit VFR circuit operations, pilots operating aircraft under IFR are expected to approach and land on the active runway that may be established by the aircraft operating in the VFR circuit. Pilots operating aircraft under IFR at an uncontrolled aerodrome do not establish any priority over aircraft operating under VFR at that aerodrome. Should it be necessary for the IFR aircraft to approach and/or land on a runway contrary to the established VFR operation, it is expected that appropriate communications, between pilots or between pilots and the air-ground facility, will be effected in order to ensure there is no traffic conflict.

question: An IFR flight in low level uncontrolled airspace without an assigned transponder code, should have a transponder code of
explanation: AIM COM 8.2 Transponder Requirements Where ATC has not assigned a transponder code, IFR flight in low level controlled or uncontrolled airspace (below 18,000 feet) shall have a transponder code of 1000 to reply on Mode A and on Mode C if available. Where ATC has not assigned a transponder code, IFR flight in high level controlled or uncontrolled airspace (18,000 feet and above) shall have a transponder code of 2000 to reply on Mode A and on Mode C if available.

question: A route extending upwards from 2,200 feet above the surface of the earth and for which ATC service is provided is known as a
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Airways A low level airway is a route extending upwards from 2,200 feet above the surface of the earth and for which ATC service is provided.

question: When a clearance is to a higher altitude is received and acknowledged, the pilot-in-command
answers: is expected to initiate a climb promptly at any climb rate.
must initiate a climb at a rate of no less than 500 fpm.
is expected to initiate a climb promptly at an optimum rate.
may initiate the climb at their discretion.
explanation: AIM RAC 8.4 Climb or Descent When an altitude clearance is issued, the pilot should begin the climb or descent promptly on acknowledgement of the clearance. The climb or descent should be made at an optimum rate consistent with the operating characteristics of the aircraft. If the above is not the case, or if it becomes necessary to stop the climb or descent, the pilot should advise ATC of the interruption or the delay in vacating an altitude.

question: . . . . . are the basis of the LF/MF airway and air route system.
explanation: AIM COM 4.6 Non Directional Beacon (NDB) Non-directional beacons (NDBs) combine a transmitter with an antenna system providing a non-directional radiation pattern within the low frequency (LF) and medium frequency (MF) bands of 190–415kHz and 510–535kHz. NDBs are the basis of the LF/MF airway and air route system. In addition, they function as marker beacons for instrument landing system (ILS) as well as non-precision approach (NPA) aids for NDB instrument approaches.

question: Once accepted, a Mach number shall be adhered to within . . . . . unless ATC approval is obtained to make a change.
explanation: AIM RAC 8.2.1 Mach Number Clearances to turbojet aircraft equipped with a Machmeter may include an appropriate Mach number. If the Mach number cannot be adhered to, ATC is to be so informed when the clearance is issued. Once accepted, the Mach number shall be adhered to within 0.01 Mach, unless ATC approval is obtained to make a change. If an immediate temporary change in Mach number is necessary (e.g. because of turbulence), ATC must be notified as soon as possible. When a Mach number is included in a clearance, the flight concerned should transmit its current Mach number with each position report.

question: When flying IFR in airspace under the jurisdiction of Canadian ATC, use of the phrase “Cancelling IFR” results in
answers: the opening of a VFR flight plan.
the automatic filing of an arrival report.
the closure of a flight plan or itinerary.
ATC discontinuing the provision of IFR separation.
explanation: AIM RAC 3.11.2 Closing of a Flight Plan or Flight Itinerary Before landing, a pilot who conducts a flight in respect of which a flight plan or flight itinerary has been filed with an ATC unit, FIC, FSS, or CARS, has the option of closing the flight plan or flight itinerary with any of these agencies prior to landing. The closing of a flight plan or flight itinerary prior to landing is considered as filing an arrival report, and as such, it will result in the termination of all alerting services with respect to SAR notification. When flying IFR in airspace under the jurisdiction of Canadian ATC, use of the phrase “Cancelling IFR” results in ATC discontinuing the provision of IFR separation, but it does not automatically close the flight plan or itinerary. Therefore, alerting service with regard to SAR notification is still active and is based on the information submitted in the original flight plan or itinerary. Because the pilot is now flying in accordance with VFR, the flight plan or itinerary must either be closed prior to landing, or an arrival report filed after landing, with an ATC unit, an FIC, an FSS or a CARS. When flying IFR in the USA. or landing at a Canadian airport that underlies airspace delegated to the control of the FAA, use of the phrase “Cancelling IFR” results in ATC discontinuing the provision of IFR separation and also closes the flight plan or itinerary. Therefore, alerting service with regard to SAR notification is also terminated, unless the pilot files and activates a VFR flight plan.

question: Select the correct statement regarding the use of the phrase “when ready” in relation to an altitude clearance or instruction.
answers: Temporarily levelling off at an intermediate altitude is permissible.
Published altitude crossing restrictions will be automatically cancelled.
If radar identified, the PIC is not expected to advise ATC when the altitude change is initiated.
The change of altitude should begin promptly upon receipt of the clearance.
explanation: AIM RAC 8.4 Climb or Descent If the phrase “when ready” is used in conjunction with an altitude clearance or instruction, the change of altitude may be initiated whenever the pilot wishes. The climb or descent should be made at an optimum rate consistent with the operating characteristics of the aircraft. When not informed that the aircraft has been IDENTIFIED, pilots are expected to advise ATC when the altitude change is initiated. Compliance with assigned or published altitude crossing restrictions and speeds is mandatory unless specifically cancelled by ATC.

question: In addition to the equipment for a day or night flight, which of the following equipment is required for flight under IFR? A) A second altimeter. B) Two independent radios. C) An alternate static pressure source for the flight instruments. D) An outside air temperature gauge. E) Approved de-icing equipment.
explanation: CAR 605.18 Power-driven Aircraft – IFR The CARs do not require two independent altimeters for IFR flight, nor do they require two independent modes of radio communication. They do however stipulate that an IFR aircraft must have sufficient radiocommunication equipment to permit the pilot to conduct two-way communications on the appropriate frequency. Neither de-icing nor anti-icing equipment is required, however, an aircraft must be equipped with a means of preventing malfunction caused by icing for each airspeed indicating system. Of the options listed in the question, only C and D are correct.

question: Flight through active Class F airspace with the designator CYR
answers: is permitted only in accordance with permission issued by the user agency.
may be undertaken only by aircraft equipped with two-way radio communication equipment and a transponder.
will be approved only for IFR aircraft under positive radar control.
may be permitted provided that approval from ATC is obtained by the pilot in command.
explanation: AIM RAC 2.8.6.4 Restricted Airspace A restricted area is airspace of defined dimensions above the land areas or territorial waters within which the flight of aircraft is restricted in accordance with certain specified conditions. Restricted airspace is designated for safety purposes when the level or type of aerial activity, surface activity, or the protection of a ground installation requires the application of restrictions within that airspace. No person may conduct aerial activities within active Class F restricted airspace unless permission has been obtained from the user agency. In some instances, the user agency may delegate a controlling agency the authority to approve access. In most cases, the controlling agency will be an ATC unit or an air navigation service provider (ANSP). The user agency is the civil or military agency or organization responsible for the activity for which the Class F airspace has been provided. It has the jurisdiction to authorize access to the airspace when it is classified as restricted.

question: If further clearance has not been received by the end of a closed STAR, the pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft should
answers: continue on the charted lateral track until further clearance has been received.
maintain the last assigned altitude and intercept the final approach course.
climb to the highest minimum IFR altitude and request further clearance.
intercept the final approach course and commence an approach.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.2.3.10 Closed Standard Terminal Arrival (STAR) Procedures A closed STAR procedure provides a continuous path from the en route structure and automatically joins up with the final approach course. A closed STAR terminates at the FACF. On a closed STAR, when an approach clearance is received, the pilot will continue to comply with all published altitude and speed restrictions, fly the charted track to the FACF, intercept the final approach course, and fly the straight-in approach. A closed STAR procedure is normally used when the inbound track is within plus or minus 90˚ of the final approach course to the runway. ATC always strives to issue approach clearances before aircraft reach the end of closed STARs, but in very rare cases (such as a distress call in progress on the frequency, frequency congestion, or high ATC workload), this may not always be possible. In order to assure obstacle clearance throughout the STAR and the approach lateral tracks, if an aircraft were to reach the end of a closed STAR prior to the issuance of an approach clearance, the pilot would be expected to safely intercept the final approach course and fly inbound maintaining the last assigned altitude. In the extremely remote case in which the aircraft reaches the end of the final approach track and further clearance has still not been obtained, the pilot would be expected to track the lateral position of the missed approach procedure for what would have been the anticipated approach, and maintain the last assigned altitude or climb to the anticipated missed approach altitude if the missed approach altitude is higher.

question: If an aircraft is experiencing lost communications, ATC will protect the airspace in the immediate vicinity of the aerodrome of first intended landing for . . . . . from the time at which the aircraft is expected to commence approach.
explanation: Communication Failure Procedures ATC will protect the airspace in the immediate vicinity of the aerodrome of first intended landing for 30 minutes from the time at which the aircraft is expected to commence an approach.

question: Refer to the Appendices: CAP CYHZ Halifax 4 Departure (1/2) CAP CYHZ Halifax 4 Departure (2/2) Time of Departure: 0945Z Flight Planned Altitude: 11,000 feet Safe Altitude 100 NM: 2,900 feet After departing CYHZ on the HALIFAX FOUR DEP, an aircraft has been instructed to turn left to a heading of 050° to intercept an airway and climb to 4,000 feet. If a radio communication failure occurs at 0951Z, the aircraft should climb to . . . . . and then continue the climb to the flight planned altitude at . . . . .
explanation: Two-Way Communications Failure Based on the chart provided, following the recognition of a failure the aircraft must maintain the last assigned altitude (4,000 feet) and begin a climb to the flight-planned altitude ten minutes after departure (0955Z).

question: An aircraft is holding over its clearance limit. The EFC is 1030Z, and the ETA reported by the PIC in the most recent position report was 1045Z. At 1025Z the aircraft experiences a communications failure in IMC, assuming that the clearance limit is also the IAF for an approach to the destination, the PIC should
answers: continue to hold as directed and commence an instrument approach at 1045Z.
continue to hold as directed and commence and instrument approach at the ETA estimated by adding the filed enroute time to the take-off time.
continue to hold as directed and commence an instrument approach at 1030Z.
exit the hold and commence an instrument approach at 1025Z.
explanation: AIM RAC 6.3.2.2 Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Flight Plan If a radiocommunication failure occurs after a pilot has received and acknowledged a holding instruction, hold as directed and commence an instrument approach at the expected approach time (EAT) or expected further clearance time (EFC), whichever has been issued.

question: Refer to the Appendices: CAP CYHU ST HUBERT SEVEN DEP (1/2) CAP CYHU ST HUBERT SEVEN DEP (2/2) If the PIC of an aircraft departing from runway 28 on the St. Hubert Seven Departure experiences a communication failure, after selecting a transponder code of 7600, the PIC is expected to
answers: climb to the flight planned altitude five minutes after selecting code 7600 and proceed on course 5 minutes after take-off.
climb on a heading of 220° and maintain 3,000 feet.
climb to the flight planned altitude five minutes after take-off and proceed on course 5 minutes after selecting code 7600.
follow the published route and climb to the flight planned altitude 5 minutes after take-off.
explanation: AIM RAC 6.3.2 Two-Way Communications Failure It is impossible to provide regulations and procedures applicable to all possible situations associated with a two-way communications failure. During a communications failure, when confronted by a situation not covered in the regulations, pilots are expected to exercise good judgment in whatever action they elect to take. While there are standard communications failure procedures, they may be superseded by specific procedures that take precedence. For example, some missed approach and SID procedures may have specific published communications failure procedures. The St. Hubert Seven Departure has a specific procedure that requires pilots to climb to the flight planned altitude 5 minutes after take-off and proceed on crouse 5 minutes after selecting 7600. AIM RAC 6.3.2 Two-Way Communications Failure It is impossible to provide regulations and procedures applicable to all possible situations associated with a two-way communications failure. During a communications failure, when confronted by a situation not covered in the regulations, pilots are expected to exercise good judgment in whatever action they elect to take. While there are standard communications failure procedures, they may be superseded by specific procedures that take precedence. For example, some missed approach and SID procedures may have specific published communications failure procedures. The St. Hubert Seven Departure has a specific procedure that requires pilots to climb to the flight planned altitude 5 minutes after take-off and proceed on crouse 5 minutes after selecting 7600.

question: Refer to the Appendices: CAP CYKA KEBRA ONE DEP (1/2) CAP CYKA KEBRA ONE DEP (2/2) When departing on the KEBRA ONE departure, after recognition of a communications failure 5 minutes after take-off and in IFR weather conditions, a pilot should maintain the last assigned altitude until . . . . . minutes after take-off.
explanation: Lost Communications The procedures to follow are specific to the instrument departure procedure and can be read directly from the chart as shown below.

question: If an IFR aircraft experiences a radio communication failure in VMC the pilot-in-command shall continue the flight under
answers: IFR and land as soon as practicable.
VFR and land as soon as practicable.
IFR and navigatie along the route assigned in the last ATC clearance received and acknowledged.
explanation: AIM RAC 6.3.2.2 Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Flight Plan If a failure occurs in VMC, or if VMC is encountered after the failure, the pilot-in-command shall continue the flight under VFR and land as soon as practicable. This procedure applies in any class of airspace. The primary purpose is to preclude extended IFR operation in controlled airspace in VMC. However, it is not intended that the requirement to “land as soon as practicable” be construed to mean “land as soon as possible.” Pilots retain the prerogative of exercising their best judgment and are not required to land at an unauthorized airport, at an airport unsuitable for the type of aircraft flown, or to land only minutes short of the destination.

question: If an IFR aircraft experiences a radio communication failure in VMC while in Class B airspace, the pilot should
answers: continue the flight under VFR and land as soon as practicable.
immediately descend out of controlled airspace and squawk 7600.
continue the flight under VFR and land as soon as possible.
immediately descend out of controlled airspace and squawk 1200.
explanation: AIM RAC 6.3.2 Two-Way Communications Failure If the failure occurs in VMC, or if VMC is encountered after the failure, the pilot-in-command shall continue the flight under VFR and land as soon as practicable. This procedure applies in any class of airspace. The primary purpose is to preclude extended IFR operation in controlled airspace in VMC. However, it is not intended that the requirement to “land as soon as practicable” be construed to mean “land as soon as possible.” The pilot retains the prerogative of exercising his/her best judgment and is not required to land at an unauthorized airport, at an airport unsuitable for the type of aircraft flown, or to land only minutes short of the destination.

question: The PIC of an IFR aircraft cleared to Victoria International Airport (CYYJ) is being radar vectored and informed by ATC to expect direct MINIK V368 YZP. Soon after, the aircraft experiences a radio communication failure, the PIC shall
answers: continue along the route last assigned by ATC, then fly direct to the clearance limit and commence a descent to land at the filed ETA or at the EAA, whichever occurs first.
fly direct to MINIK, join V368 until YZP and then continue along the filed flight plan route.
continue along the filed flight plan route.
fly direct to CYYJ at the flight planned altitude and commence a descent for landing at the filed ETA.
explanation: AIM RAC 6.3.2.2 Lost Comms – Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Flight Plan If a radio communication failure occurs in IMC, or if the flight cannot be continued under VMC, the pilot-in-command shall fly a route according to the following: By the route assigned in the last ATC clearance received and acknowledged. If being radar-vectored, by the direct route from the point of communications failure to the fix, route, or airway specified in the vector clearance. In the absence of an assigned route, by the route that ATC has advised may be expected in a further clearance. In the absence of an assigned route or route that ATC has advised may be expected in a further clearance, by the route filed in the flight plan.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CZVL AERODROME CHART Select the correct statements regarding an aircraft departing from runway 34? A) Minimum takeoff visibility is 1/2 SM. B) Minimum climb gradient is 200 ft/min. C) Minimum climb gradient is 200 ft/NM. D) Minimum takeoff visibility is 1 SM.
explanation: CAP GEN – IFR Takeoffs The takeoff minimum for RWY 34 is 1/2 SM as shown in the image below. Since there is no SPEC VIS or supplemental procedures to follow, IFR departures from RWY 34 will be assured of obstacle clearance in any direction if the aircraft complies with the following: The aircraft will cross at least 35 ft above the departure end of the runway. The aircraft will climb straight ahead to 400 ft above aerodrome elevation (AAE) before turning. The aircraft will maintain a climb gradient of at least 200 ft/NM throughout the climb to a minimum IFR altitude for en-route operations.

question: If an IFR departure has not been assessed for obstacles
answers: The pilot must execute a shuttle climb overhead the airport until reaching the MSA before proceeding on course.
The pilot may depart if the reported visibility is greater than the specified minimum take-off visibility.
The PIC is responsible for determining minimum climb gradients and routings for obstacle and terrain avoidance.
The PIC may not depart under IFR.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.7 Obstacle and Terrain Clearance If an IFR departure has not been assessed, it implies that the pilot-in-command is responsible for determining minimum climb gradients and/or routings for obstacle and terrain avoidance. In the absence of a published visibility for a particular runway, a pilot may depart IFR only if take-off visibility will allow avoidance of obstacles and terrain on departure. In no case should the take-off visibility be less than 1/2 SM.

question: Select the correct statement regarding IFR departure procedures.
answers: ATC should be informed if an aircraft is unable to depart IFR from an uncontrolled aerodrome within 15 minutes of the proposed departure time.
ODPs are established to improve traffic flow.
If a departure has not been assessed for obstacles, pilots are responsible for determining minimum climb gradients and routings for obstacle and terrain avoidance.
SIDs are pilot initiated and ATC will provide navigational guidance to an assigned route or to a fix depicted on the chart.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.7 Obstacle and Terrain Clearance Aerodromes that have an instrument approach procedure (IAP) published in the Canada Air Pilot (CAP) also have an instrument flight rules (IFR) departure procedure. There are two types of IFR departure procedures: the standard instrument departure (SID) and the obstacle departure procedure (ODP). SIDs are developed to establish a traffic flow while ODPs are pilot initiated. Both types meet obstacle and terrain clearance requirements. If an IFR departure has not been assessed for obstacles, pilots are responsible for determining minimum climb gradients and/or routings for obstacle and terrain avoidance.

question: While taxiing to a specific runway, the visibility drops below the minimum aerodrome operating visibility and the operator-specific takeoff visibility. However, another suitable runway where the reported visibility is above the minimum take-off visibility but below the minimum aerodrome operating visibility is available. Can the aircraft taxi to the second runway?
answers: No, the aircraft cannot continue taxiing.
Yes, however a take-off will not be authorized even if the operating visibility is at or above the published level of service for that runway.
Yes, the aircraft can continue taxiing only if the operator has written approval from the Minister.
Yes, and a take-off may be authorized once the operating visibility is at or above the published level of service for that runway.
explanation: CAP GEN – Operating Minima (Taxiing) Since the visibility deteriorated below the published aerodrome operating visibility after the aircraft commenced taxi for departure the taxi operations are not deemed to be occurring below the published aerodrome operating visibility. Therefore, the aircraft can taxi to a more suitable runway and legally take-off if the operating visibility is at or above the published level of service for that runway.

question: If a flight originating from an aerodrome with no control tower will not commence within 60 minutes of the proposed departure time stipulated in an IFR flight plan
answers: the pilot-in-command must amend the ETA after becoming airborne.
the search and rescue process will be activated.
a CADOR will be filed by ATC.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.9 IFR Departures From Uncontrolled Airports It is recommended that pilots inform ATC if a flight will not commence within 60 minutes of the proposed departure time stipulated in an IFR flight plan. Failure to do so will result in activating the SAR process.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CNP3 Aerodrome Chart No RVR or METAR is reported, however, the pilot-determined visibility is approximately 1 1/4 miles. Select the correct statement regarding a departure from RWY 10 using a climb gradient of 300 ft/NM.
answers: The pilot may depart with visibility greater than or equal to 1/2 SM regardless of the ceiling.
The ceiling must be high enough to allow for a visual climb up to 1,200 feet overhead CNP3 before proceeding on course.
The pilot may depart if visibility is at least 1/2 SM and the ceiling is greater than or equal to 600 feet.
The pilot may depart since the visibility is greater than or equal to 1/4 SM.
explanation: CAP GEN IFR Take-Offs Based on the departure procedure for RWY 10, if an aircraft is unable to maintain a climb rate of 310 ft/NM it must adhere to the SPEC VIS (Specified Takeoff Minimum Visibility). In this particular example, a visual climb up to 1,200 feet overhead the airport is required before proceeding on course (BPOC).

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP AERODROME CHART CZVL What is the minimum visibility required for an aircraft to depart from runway 34 under IFR?
explanation: CAP GEN – IFR Take-Offs Notwithstanding, and unless otherwise authorized in accordance with CAR 602, IFR take-offs for all aircraft are prohibited when the visibility is below the applicable minimum visibility published in the Canada Air Pilot (CAP), or the level of service published in the CFS and CAP for the runway being used. The minimum visibility required to depart from runway 34 is 1/2 SM.

question: Unless a full readback is requested by ATC, when clearance is received on the ground before departure from a controlled aerodrome and a standard instrument departure (SID) is included in the clearance, the pilot only needs to acknowledge receipt of the clearance by repeating
answers: the aircraft call sign, the name of the SID, initial flight altitude and the transponder code that was assigned.
the aircraft call sign, the name of the SID and the transponder code that was assigned.
the aircraft call sign and the transponder code that was assigned.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.4 IFR Clearances According to CAR 602.31, clearance received by a pilot must be read back to the controller, except in certain circumstances. When clearance is received on the ground before departure from a controlled aerodrome and a standard instrument departure (SID) is included in the clearance, the pilot only needs to acknowledge receipt of the clearance by repeating the aircraft call sign and the transponder code that was assigned. If there is an amendment to the altitude contained in the SID, that altitude shall also be read back. Whenever the controller requests a full readback, the pilot shall comply. Also, the pilot may, at any time, read back a clearance in full to seek clarification.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYET AERODROME CHART METAR CYET 181000Z AUTO 26002KT 1/4SM OVC07 M10/M11 A2944 RMK SLP028= METAR CYET 180900Z AUTO VRB02KT 2SM BKN013 M11/M11 A2942 RMK SLP023= METAR CYET 180800Z AUTO 26003KT 220V290 3SM BKN013 M10/M11 A2939 RMK SLP012= A pilot plans to depart CYET under IFR. Given the weather reports provided above, if the pilot-determined visibility is 1/2 SM, what is the current aerodrome operating visibility?
answers: 1/2 SM and a departure is not authorized.
1/4 SM and a departure is authorized.
1/4 SM and a departure is not authorized.
1/2 SM and a departure is authorized.
explanation: CAP GEN Aerodrome Operating Restrictions – Visibility The minimum visibility for take-off shall be determined by the pilot-in-command consistent with runway level of service as published in the CFS and CAP, runway requirements for RVR 1200 (1/4 SM) or 600 OPS SPEC, aircraft performance, navigation equipment limitations and the requirement for the pilot to ensure obstacle clearance. Notwithstanding, and unless otherwise authorized in accordance with CAR 602, IFR take-offs for all aircraft are prohibited when the visibility is below the applicable minimum visibility published in the Canada Air Pilot (CAP), or the level of service published in the CFS and CAP for the runway being used. According to the aerodrome chart a departure is authorized from runway 07 if the visibility is at least 1/2SM. If departing from runway 25 a climb in visual conditions up to 3,900 feet is required. At sites without an active ATC Tower (outside ATC operating hours, MF, Unicom, CARS, or advisory sites, etc…) for departures, the aerodrome operating visibility is the lowest of the following visibilities: Ground visibility – (1/4 SM) Any reported RVR – (Not reported) Pilot visibility – (1/2 SM) Therefore since the operating visibility is 1/4 SM, as reported in the latest METAR, and the ceiling will not permit a visual climb to 3,900 feet, a departure is not authorized from either runway 07 or runway 25.

question: Select the correct statement if visibility deteriorates below the published aerodrome operating visibility after an aircraft has commenced taxi for departure.
answers: The aircraft must stop and either wait until visibility improves or be towed back to its starting position on the apron.
Since the aircraft was intending to depart, operations are not deemed to be occurring below the published aerodrome operating visibility and the aircraft may continue to taxi.
Taxiing is authorized if the RVR for the runway of intended takeoff is varying between distances less than and greater than the minimum RVR.
Taxiing is authorized if the aerodrome is equipped with an enhanced taxiway lighting system.
explanation: CAP GEN – Aerodrome Operating Restrictions – Visibility Where the aerodrome operating visibility is less than the minimum visibility published in the CFS, taxi operations are deemed to be occurring below the published aerodrome operating visibility except when: Visibility deteriorates below the published aerodrome operating visibility after the aircraft has commenced taxi for departure (including deicing stop). Visibility deteriorates below the published aerodrome operating visibility after the aircraft has landed and is taxiing to the destination on the aerodrome. The aircraft is taxiing on the manoeuvring area as authorized by ATC in accordance with the aerodrome’s published operational procedures. The aircraft is taxiing for departure at a site without an active ATC Tower, in accordance with the aerodrome’s operational procedures. The aircraft is taxiing on the manoeuvring area for purposes other than take-off or landing as authorized by the Aerodrome Operator in accordance with the aerodrome’s RVOP/LVOP. Example

question: Prior to takeoff, ATC assigns a heading and altitude to a SID. Select the correct statement.
answers: ATC may not amend a SID.
The pilot must confirm that the ATC assigned heading and altitude correspond to those charted in the SID.
The pilot must readback the heading and altitude, and the SID is cancelled.
The pilot must readback the heading and altitude, and the SID is not cancelled.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.5 Standard Instrument Departure (SID) ATC may assign an altitude different from the altitude specified in the SID, provided the altitude is stated and a readback is obtained from the pilot prior to departure. In addition, where vector SIDs are used, ATC may assign a different initial departure heading. However, an ATC revision to any item of a SID does not cancel the SID.

question: A pilot plans to depart under IFR from an aerodrome with an active ATC tower. However, there is a NOTAM indicating that the RVR for the planned departure runway is inoperative. The aerodrome operating visibility is the
explanation: CAP GEN Aerodrome Operating Restrictions – Visibility CAR 602.96 (2)(b) requires that before taking off from, landing at or otherwise operating an aircraft at an aerodrome, the pilot-in-command of the aircraft shall be satisfied that the aerodrome is suitable for the intended operation. Additionally, for Air and Private Operators, the CARs (and associated Standards and Operations Specifications) govern operations below RVR 2600 (1/2 SM). One factor that needs to be considered to ensure compliance with the regulatory requirements above is the Aerodrome Operating Visibility. Sites with an Active ATC Tower At sites with an active Air Traffic Control (ATC) tower for arrivals and departures, the aerodrome operating visibility is in accordance with the following hierarchy: Runway Visual Range (RVR) for the runway of intended use Ground visibility (METAR) Tower visibility Pilot visibility Note : Tower observed visibility does not take precedence over reported ground visibility. Where ground visibility is reported, tower-observed visibility is considered advisory only. However, where ground visibility is either not reported or the visibility reported by the AWOS is non-representative of the prevailing visibility at the airport, tower-reported visibility, when available, replaces ground visibility and needs to be considered in the determination of the aerodrome operating visibility. Sites without an Active ATC Tower At sites without an active ATC Tower (outside ATC operating hours, MF, Unicom, CARS, or advisory sites, etc…) For arrivals, the aerodrome operating visibility is in accordance with the following hierarchy: Runway Visual Range (RVR) for the runway of intended use Ground visibility (METAR) Pilot visibility For departures, the aerodrome operating visibility is the lowest of the following visibilities: Ground visibility (METAR) Any reported RVR Pilot visibility

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYTA AGNEX THREE DEP (1/2) CAP CYTA AGNEX THREE DEP (2/2) When departing from runway 35 under IFR, if an aircraft’s groundspeed during the initial climb is 140 knots, the minimum climb rate is
explanation: AIM RAC 7.7 Obstacle Terrain Clearance IFR departure procedures are expressed in the form of take-off minima on an aerodrome chart. These procedures are based on the premise that, on departure, an aircraft will: cross at least 35 ft above the departure end of the runway; climb straight ahead to 400 ft above aerodrome elevation (AAE) before turning; and maintain a climb gradient of at least 200 ft/NM throughout the climb to a minimum IFR altitude for en route operations. Climb gradients greater than 200 ft/NM may be published. In this case, the aircraft is expected to achieve and maintain the published gradient to the specified altitude or fix, then continue climbing at a minimum of 200 ft/NM until reaching a minimum IFR altitude for en route operations. In this particular example, a climb gradient of 280 ft/NM is required. Using the departure climb rate table as shown below, with a ground speed of 140 knots, an aircraft must maintain a climb rate of 660 ft/min.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYCD AERODROME CHART Given the following weather information, is it possible for an aircraft with a climb gradient of 350 ft/NM to depart under IFR? METAR CYCD 050200Z 27005KT 6SM BR OVC006 06/05 A3017 RMK SF8 CIG RAG SLP220= METAR CYCD 050100Z 28003KT 8SM BKN009 BKN012 OVC045 06/05 A3019 RMK SF5SF2SC1 CIG RAG SLP226= METAR CYCD 050000Z 26003KT 8SM SCT009 BKN012 OVC045 07/05 A3021 RMK SF3SF4SC1 CIG RAG PRESFR SLP232= TAF CYCD 042338Z 0500/0505 05005KT P6SM -RA SCT008 OVC015 TEMPO 0500/0505 2SM -RA BR SCT004 OVC008 RMK NXT FCST WILL BE ISSUED AT 051545Z=
answers: No, the ceiling must allow for a visual climb over the airport to 2,000 feet.
Yes, because the reported visibility is greater than the minimum required visibility.
No, there is a broken layer below 2,000 feet and the required SPEC VIS is 3 SM.
Yes, an aircraft can depart from RWY 34 if it can make use of the YCD NDB.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.7.1 Visual Climb Over The Airport (VCOA) The aircraft cannot depart from either runway 16 or runway 34. A departure from runway 34 is not possible because it requires a minimum climb gradient of 400 ft/NM and a departure from runway 16 is not possible because the latest METAR reports an overcast layer at 600 feet, preventing an aircraft from maintaining visual conditions until 2,000 feet. Visual climbs were developed to provide an alternate IFR departure procedure for aircraft that cannot meet the greater than standard climb gradient specified in the primary instrument departure procedure. Occasionally, VCOA may be the only available departure procedure developed for an aerodrome. VCOA differs from other instrument departure procedures in that the pilot must maintain certain visual references with the ground and obstacles until reaching a given altitude over the aerodrome. Even though the aircraft is being operated with visual references to the ground, it is still departing on an IFR clearance. The VCOA text includes a SPEC VIS and a climb-to altitude in feet above sea level. The SPEC VIS is the minimum visibility in statute miles that a pilot requires to manoeuvre the aircraft while climbing. The climb-to altitude is the minimum altitude above the aerodrome that the aircraft must reach before departing en route. It is the pilot’s responsibility to see and avoid obstacles while climbing visually. The pilot should be familiar with the local terrain and the obstacles that surround the aerodrome and plan the climb appropriately. Taking local traffic and obstacles into consideration, it is advisable that the pilot keep the aerodrome in sight while climbing. The visual climb segment ends when the aircraft crosses the aerodrome at or above the required minimum altitude. From this point on, obstacles will be cleared if the aircraft maintains a minimum climb gradient of 200 ft/ NM to the en-route structure. The PIC should ensure that the reported ceiling is above the climb-to altitude and that the local prevailing visibility is equal to or greater than that required in the procedure.

question: A pilot is departing from an uncontrolled aerodrome with no ATC tower in service. The RVR is not reported, ground visibility is 1SM and pilot visibility is 1 1/4 SM. What is the aerodrome operating visibility?
explanation: CAP GEN Aerodrome Operating Restrictions – Visibility CAR 602.96 (2)(b) requires that before taking off from, landing at, or otherwise operating an aircraft at an aerodrome, the pilot-in-command of the aircraft must be satisfied that the aerodrome is suitable for the intended operation. One factor that needs to be considered to ensure compliance with the regulatory requirements is the Aerodrome Operating Visibility. Sites With an Active ATC Tower At sites with an active Air Traffic Control (ATC) tower for arrivals and departures, the aerodrome operating visibility is in accordance with the following hierarchy: Runway Visual Range (RVR) for the runway of intended use Ground visibility (METAR) Tower visibility Pilot visibility Note : Tower observed visibility does not take precedence over reported ground visibility. Where ground visibility is reported, tower observed visibility is considered advisory only. However, where ground visibility is either not reported or the visibility reported by the AWOS is non-representative of the prevailing visibility at the airport, tower reported visibility, when available, replaces ground visibility and needs to be considered in the determination of the aerodrome operating visibility. Sites Without an Active ATC Tower At sites without an active ATC Tower (outside ATC operating hours, MF, UNICOM, CARS, or advisory sites, etc…), for arrivals, the aerodrome operating visibility is in accordance with the following hierarchy: Runway Visual Range (RVR) for the runway of intended use Ground visibility (METAR) Pilot visibility For departures, the aerodrome operating visibility is the lowest of the following visibilities: Ground visibility (METAR) Any reported RVR Pilot visibility

question: For the purpose of taxiing for departure, will a lower ground visibility report take precedence over a runway visibility report?
answers: At sites without an active control tower, no, since the reported RVR is governing.
At sites without an active control tower, yes, since a reported lower ground visibility is governing.
At sites with an active control tower, yes, since a lower ground visibility is governing.
At sites with an active control tower, no, since a pilot visibility is governing
explanation: CAP GEN Aerodrome Operating Restrictions – Visibility CAR 602.96 (2)(b) requires that before taking off from, landing at or otherwise operating an aircraft at an aerodrome, the pilot-in-command of the aircraft shall be satisfied that the aerodrome is suitable for the intended operation. Additionally, for Air and Private Operators, the CARs (and associated Standards and Operations Specifications) govern operations below RVR 2600 (1/2 SM). One factor that needs to be considered to ensure compliance with the regulatory requirements above is the Aerodrome Operating Visibility. Sites with an Active ATC Tower At sites with an active Air Traffic Control (ATC) tower for arrivals and departures, the aerodrome operating visibility is in accordance with the following hierarchy: Runway Visual Range (RVR) for the runway of intended use Ground visibility (METAR) Tower visibility Pilot visibility Note : Tower observed visibility does not take precedence over reported ground visibility. Where ground visibility is reported, tower observed visibility is considered advisory only. However, where ground visibility is either not reported or the visibility reported by the AWOS is non-representative of the prevailing visibility at the airport, tower reported visibility, when available, replaces ground visibility and needs to be considered in the determination of the aerodrome operating visibility. Sites without an Active ATC Tower At sites without an active ATC Tower (outside ATC operating hours, MF, Unicom, CARS, or advisory sites, etc…) For arrivals, the aerodrome operating visibility is in accordance with the following hierarchy: Runway Visual Range (RVR) for the runway of intended use Ground visibility (METAR) Pilot visibility For departures, the aerodrome operating visibility is the lowest of the following visibilities: Ground visibility (METAR) Any reported RVR Pilot visibility

question: Departure procedures are based on the premise that on departure an aircraft will cross at least . . . . . above the departure end of the runway.
explanation: CAP GEN: IFR Take-Offs Departure procedures meet obstacle clearance requirements and are based on the premise that on departure an aircraft will: cross at least 35 feet above the departure end of the runway; climb on runway heading to 400 feet AAE before turning; and, maintain a climb gradient of at least 200 feet per NM throughout the climb to the minimum altitude for enroute operations.

question: Refer to the Appendices: CAP CYOW Ottawa 4 Departure (1/2) CAP CYOW Ottawa 4 Departure (2/2) An aircraft departing on the Ottawa 4 departure from runway 04 with a groundspeed of 120 knots requires a minimum climb rate of . . . . .
answers: 540 ft/min until reaching the cruise altitude.
270 ft/NM until reaching the cruise altitude.
explanation: Departure Route Description Departures from runway 04 require a climb gradient of 270 ft/NM until reaching 600 feet. If the ground speed is 120 knots, a minimum climb gradient of 270 ft/NM is equivalent to a climb rate of 540 feet/min. This can all be derived by referencing the Departure Route Description and Departure Climb Rate Table as shown below.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYCD Aerodrome Chart METAR CYCD 190500Z CCA 00000KT 2SM -RA BR BKN007 BKN035 OVC140 05/05 A2973 RMK SF3SC3AC2 LAST STFD OBS/NEXT 191400 SLP071= METAR CYCD 190500Z 00000KT 5SM -RA BR SCT007 BKN035 OVC140 05/05 A2973 RMK SF3SC3AC2 SLP071= SPECI CYCD 190424Z VRB02KT 5SM -RA BR SCT007 BKN015 OVC045 06/06 A2972 RMK SF4SC1SC3 -RA INTMT SLP065= METAR CYCD 190400Z 00000KT 3SM -RA BR BKN007 OVC035 06/06 A2970 RMK SF5SC3 CIG VIS LWR S-W SLP060= Is it possible for an aircraft to depart under IFR?
answers: No, there is a broken layer at 700 feet and the ODP requires a VCOA to 2,000 feet from RWY 16
No, there is a broken layer at 700 feet and the ODP requires a VCOA to 2,000 feet from RWY 16 and RWY 34.
No, the reported visibility is below the required SPEC VIS of 3 SM.

explanation: Obstacle Departure Procedure According to the latest METAR, the ceiling is 700 feet which precludes a visual climb to 2,000 feet from runway 19. However, since the visibility is greater than 1/2 SM, an aircraft may depart from runway 34 if it can maintain a minimum climb gradient of 400 ft/NM up to 1,600 feet.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYRM AERODROME CHART Which of the following conditions allow an aircraft to depart from RWY 13 when the MF is not active? A. RVR not available and ground visibility of 3/8 SM. B. RVR of 4,000 feet, ground visibility of 1/2 SM and pilot visibility of 5/8 SM. C. RVR 3,000 feet an ground visibility of 3/8 SM. D. RVR fluctuating between 2,800 and 2,000 feet and ground visibility 3/8 SM. E. RVR not available and ground visibility of 1/2 SM.
explanation: CAP GEN Aerodrome Operating Restrictions – Visibility When departing from a site without an active ATC Tower (outside ATC operating hours, MF, Unicom, CARS, or advisory sites) the aerodrome operating visibility is the lowest of the following visibilities: Ground visibility (METAR) Any reported RVR Pilot visibility According to the aerodrome chart , the takeoff minimum is 1/2 SM visibility. Option A : RVR not available and ground visibility of 3/8 SM – With a ground visibility of 3/8 SM a departure is not possible since it is less than the required minimum of 1/2 SM. Option B: RVR of 4,000 ft, ground visibility of 1/2 SM and pilot visibility of 5/8 SM – Departure is possible since the ground visibility of 1/2 SM is the lowest visibility and not less than the takeoff minimum of 1/2 SM. Option C: RVR 3,000 feet and ground visibility of 3/8 SM – Ground visibility is less than the minimum required visibility for takeoff, therefore departure is not possible Option D: RVR fluctuating between 2,800 and 2,000 feet and ground visibility 3/8 SM – Ground visibility is less than the minimum required visibility for takeoff, therefore departure is not possible Option E: RVR not available and ground visibility of 1/2 SM – Departure is possible since the ground visibility of 1/2 SM is the lowest visibility and not less than the takeoff minimum of 1/2 SM.

question: Prior to takeoff, if advised to change to a specified departure frequency after airborne, the frequency change should be made
answers: as soon as practicable after takeoff.
prior to vacating the control zone.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.8 Release From Tower Frequency If the departure airport is located within a terminal control area, the departing IFR flight will be cleared by the tower to contact a specific control unit on a specified frequency once clear of conflicting airport traffic. At certain locations, flights will be advised prior to takeoff to change to a specified departure frequency. In this case, the change should be made as soon as practicable after takeoff.

question: Select the correct statement regarding standard instrument departures.
answers: ATC may not assign an altitude different from the altitude specified in the SID.
SIDs do not incorporate obstacle and terrain clearance within the procedure.
An ATC revision to any item of a SID cancels the SID.
An ATC revision to any item of a SID does not cancel the SID.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.5 Standard Instrument Departure (SID) The SID, as published, contains an altitude to climb to after departure, however, ATC may assign an altitude different from the altitude specified in the SID, provided the altitude is stated and a readback is obtained from the pilot prior to departure. In addition, where vector SIDs are used, ATC may assign a different initial departure heading. However, an ATC revision to any item of a SID does not cancel the SID.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYUL MONTREAL ONE DEP Select the correct statement regarding the Montreal 1 Departure.
answers: Departures from 06L require a minimum climb rate of 250 ft/min.
Departures from 06R prohibit left turns below 1,300 ft.
An aircraft with a groundspeed of 120 knots during the initial climb segment will require a climb rate of 500 ft/min in order to comply with obstacle clearance requirements.
Jet aircraft are required to initially climb to 3,000 ft.

question: Prior to takeoff, if ATC assigns a heading and altitude to a SID the pilot must
answers: readback the heading and altitude, and the SID is not cancelled.
must readback the IFR clearance in its entirety and the SID is not cancelled.
readback the heading and altitude, and the SID is cancelled.
confirm that the ATC assigned heading and altitude correspond to those depicted on the SID.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.5 Standard Instrument Departure (SID) At certain airports, an instrument flight rules (IFR) departure clearance may include departure instructions known as a standard instrument departure (SID). A SID is a planned IFR air traffic control (ATC) departure procedure, published in the Canada Air Pilot (CAP), for pilot and controller use in graphic and textual form. SIDs provide a transition from the terminal to the appropriate en route structure, and may be either: Pilot navigation SIDs — established where the pilot is required to use the chart as reference for navigation to the en route phase Vector SIDs — established where ATC will provide radar navigational guidance to a filed/assigned route or to a fix depicted on the chart. Pilots are expected to use the SID chart as reference for navigation until radar vectoring NOTE: A SID termination fix may be a NAVAID, intersection, or DME and is normally located on an established airway where the SID terminates and the en route phase of flight commences. The SID, as published, contains an altitude to climb to after departure; however, ATC may assign an altitude different from the altitude specified in the SID, provided the altitude is stated and a readback is obtained from the pilot prior to departure. In addition, where vector SIDs are used, ATC may assign a different initial departure heading. However, an ATC revision to any item of a SID does not cancel the SID.

question: Departure procedures are based on the premise that on departure an aircraft will maintain a climb gradient of at least . . . . . throughout the climb to the minimum altitude for enroute operations.
explanation: CAP GEN: IFR Take-Offs Departure procedures meet obstacle clearance requirements and are based on the premise that on departure an aircraft will: Cross at least 35 feet above the departure end of the runway Climb on runway heading to 400 feet AAE before turning Maintain a climb gradient of at least 200 feet per NM throughout the climb to the minimum altitude for enroute operations.

question: In the absence of a published visibility for a particular runway, an aeroplane
answers: may depart if the takeoff visibility is not less than 1 SM.
may depart if the takeoff visibility is not less than 1/2 SM.
may depart if the takeoff visibility is not less than 1/4 SM.
explanation: CAP GEN Takeoff Minima/Departure Procedures In the absence of a published visibility for a particular runway, a pilot may depart IFR by using a takeoff visibility that will allow avoidance of obstacles on departure. In no case should the takeoff visibility be less than 1/2 SM.

explanation: AIM RAC 7.7 Obstacle and Terrain Clearance The asterisk following all or specific runways refers the pilot to the applicable minimum take-off visibility (1/2 or SPEC VIS) and the corresponding procedures which, if followed, will ensure obstacle and terrain clearance. Procedures may include specific climb gradients, routings, visual climb requirements, locations of close-in obstacles, or combinations thereof. Where a visual climb is stated in the departure procedure, pilots are expected to comply with the specified takeoff minimum visibility (SPEC VIS) corresponding to the appropriate aircraft category listed in the following table.

question: When instructed to fly runway heading, or when flying a SID for which no specific heading is published, a pilot must
answers: remain upwind of the assigned track.
advise ATC if applying a drift correction.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.5 Standard Instrument Departure (SID) When instructed to fly on the runway heading, or when flying a SID for which no specific heading is published, pilots are expected to fly or maintain the heading that corresponds with the extended centreline of the departure runway until otherwise instructed by ATC. Drift correction must not be applied. For instance, if the runway centerline’s magnetic heading is 044°, the pilot should maintain a heading of 044°.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CZVL Aerodrome Chart A pilot departing under IFR from runway 34 at CZVL must maintain a climb gradient of . . . . . ft/NM and maintain runway heading until . . . . . ft AAE before proceeding on course.
explanation: AIM RAC 7.7 Obstacle and Terrain Clearance Aerodromes that have an instrument approach procedure (IAP) published in the Canada Air Pilot (CAP) also have an instrument flight rules (IFR) departure procedure. There are two types of IFR departure procedures: the standard instrument departure (SID) and the obstacle departure procedure (ODP). SIDs are developed to establish a traffic flow while ODPs are pilot initiated. Both types meet obstacle and terrain clearance requirements. IFR departure procedures are expressed in the form of take-off minima on an aerodrome chart. These procedures are based on the premise that, on departure, an aircraft will: cross at least 35 ft above the departure end of the runway; climb straight ahead to 400 ft above aerodrome elevation (AAE) before turning; and maintain a climb gradient of at least 200 ft/NM throughout the climb to a minimum IFR altitude for en route operations. Climb gradients greater than 200 ft/NM may be published. In this case, the aircraft is expected to achieve and maintain the published gradient to the specified altitude or fix, then continue climbing at a minimum of 200 ft/NM until reaching a minimum IFR altitude for en route operations. In the absence of a published visibility for a particular runway, a pilot may depart IFR only if take-off visibility will allow avoidance of obstacles and terrain on departure. In no case should the take-off visibility be less than 1/2 SM (1/4 SM for rotorcraft). Where aircraft limitations or other factors preclude the pilot from following the published procedure, it is the PIC’s responsibility to determine alternative procedures that take into account obstacle and terrain avoidance.

question:
1,000-ft-on-top IFR flight may be conducted provided that the flight
A) is made at least 1,000 ft above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation.
B) is made at least 2,000 ft above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation.
C) visibility is at least one mile.
D) visibility is at least three miles.
E) has been authorized by the appropriate ATC unit.
explanation: AIM RAC 8.7 1,000 Feet on Top IFR Flight The provision to operate 1000 feet-on-top exists so that separation restrictions normally applied by ATC can be temporarily suspended. 1,000-ft-on-top IFR flight may be conducted provided that the: flight is made at least 1,000 ft above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation; flight visibility above the formation is at least three miles; top of the formation is well defined; altitude appropriate to the direction of flight is maintained when cruising in level flight; “1 000-ft-on-top” flight has been authorized by the appropriate ATC unit; and the aircraft will operate within Class B airspace at or below 12,500 ft ASL, Class C, D, or E airspace. NOTE : ATC does not apply separation to aircraft operating 1,000-ft-on-top except in the following conditions: At night between an aircraft operating 1,000-ft-on-top and other aircraft if any of the aircraft are holding. Between aircraft operating 1,000-ft-on-top and an aircraft operating on an altitude reservation approval.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart There is no MOCA published along L602 (A) because
answers: the MOCA is below the MEA.
there is no requirement to publish a MOCA along L-Routes.
the MOCA is equal to the MEA.
the MOCA is above the MEA.
explanation: Minimum Obstacle Clearance Altitude (MOCA) The altitude above sea level (ASL) between specified fixes on airways or air routes that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements for the route segment in question. It is published on aeronautical charts unless it is equal to the MEA.

question: While in cruise, an aircraft requests to descend below the MEA due to icing conditions. If 30 minutes have passed since an aircraft has descended below the MEA and an arrival report has not been filed, ATC will continue to protect the MEA
answers: until the estimated time of landing at the alternate plus thirty minutes.
until search and rescue operations commence.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.4 Descent out of Controlled Airspace ATC may not clear an aircraft to operate below the MEA of an airway, nor below the minimum IFR altitude in other controlled low-level airspace. The pilot, however, may operate at the MOCA, and ATC will approve flight at the MOCA at the pilot’s request. By prior arrangement with ATC, the MEA will be protected in the event that the pilot does not encounter visual conditions at the MOCA. Under this arrangement, the MEA will be protected: until the pilot files an arrival report; for 30 minutes to allow descent to the MOCA and return to the MEA when communication is restored with ATC; or if ATC does not hear from the pilot under (a) or (b), until the aircraft is estimated to have arrived at the filed alternate plus 30 minutes.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart What is the highest MEA between SSM (A) and YSB (B) along V316?
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation The MEA can be read directly from the chart provided as shown below. Click the image to enlarge it

question: What is the maximum holding speed for an aeroplane at 6,000 feet ASL?
explanation: AI MRAC 10.7 Speed Limitation The size of the protected airspace for a holding pattern is based on aircraft speed. Unless otherwise noted on the charts or when a shuttle procedure is specified, holding patterns must be entered and flown at or below the airspeeds listed in the table below.

question: An aircraft, when operated in accordance with IFR within designated mountainous regions, but outside of areas for which minimum altitudes for IFR operations have been established shall be flown at an altitude of at least . . . . . above the highest obstacle within . . . . . of the aircraft in flight.
explanation: AIM RAC 2.12 Mountainous Regions Designated mountainous regions are defined areas specified in the Designated Airspace Handbook where special rules for minimum IFR altitudes apply to ensure obstacle clearance. When operating under IFR within designated mountainous regions, but outside areas with established minimum IFR altitudes, an aircraft must maintain at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 NM in areas 1 and 5, and at least 1,500 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 NM in areas 2, 3, and 4. Designated Mountainous Regions Within Canada

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart What is the MEA along R35 between PR and ZKI?
explanation: Minimum Enroute Altitude The altitude above sea level (ASL) between specified fixes on airways or air routes that assures acceptable navigational signal coverage and that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements.

question: When temperatures are significantly lower than ISA, the minimum altitude recommended for IFR flight on airways within designated mountainous regions is
answers: 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of the aircraft.
1,500 ft above the highest obstacle within 10 NM of the aircraft.
explanation: AIM RAC 8.5 Minimum IFR Altitudes Pressure altimeters are calibrated to indicate true altitude under ISA conditions, and any deviation from ISA will result in an erroneous altimeter reading. When temperatures are extremely cold, true altitudes will be significantly lower than indicated altitudes. Although pilots may fly IFR at the published MEA/ MOCA, in the winter, when air temperatures are much lower than ISA, they should operate at altitudes of at least 1,000 ft above the MEA/MOCA.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Select the correct statement regarding WELLF (A).
answers: It is a compulsory reporting point.
The maximum speed when crossing WELLF is 230 knots.
A GNSS RNAV system is required to identify its location.
It is 90 miles from the YYC VOR/DME.
explanation: AIM RAC 8.1 Position Reports Pilots of instrument flight rules (IFR) and controlled VFR (CVFR) flights are required to make position reports over compulsory reporting points specified on IFR charts, and over any other reporting points specified by air traffic control (ATC). Reporting points are indicated by a symbol on the appropriate charts. The “designated compulsory” reporting point is a solid triangle and the “on request” reporting point symbol is an open triangle. Position reports over an “on request” reporting point are only necessary when requested by ATC.

question: What is the timing for the inbound leg of a holding pattern above 14,000 ft ASL?
explanation: AIM RAC 10.6 Timing The still air time for flying the outbound leg of a holding pattern should not exceed 1 minute if at or below 14,000 ft ASL, or 1 ½ minutes if above 14,000 ft ASL. However, allowance in both heading and timing to compensate for winds may be required. After the initial circuit of the holding pattern, timing should begin abeam the fix or on attaining the outbound heading, whichever occurs later. Pilots should increase or decrease outbound times, in recognition of winds, to achieve 1 or 1 ½ minute (appropriate to altitude) inbound legs to the fix.

question: Unless otherwise indicated, when holding below 14,000 feet ASL and above 6,000 feet ASL, an aircraft’s indicated airspeed must be at or below
explanation: AIM RAC 10.7 Speed Limitation The size of the protected airspace for a holding pattern is based on aircraft speed. Unless otherwise noted on the charts or when a shuttle procedure is specified, holding patterns must be entered and flown at or below the airspeeds listed in the table below.

question: Unless otherwise published, the maximum speed during a climb while executing a shuttle procedure is
explanation: AIM RAC 10.9 Shuttle Procedure A shuttle procedure is defined as a manoeuvre involving a descent or climb in a pattern resembling a holding pattern. Shuttles are generally prescribed on instrument procedure charts located in mountainous areas. In the approach phase, it is normally prescribed where a descent of more than 2,000 ft is required during the initial or intermediate approach segments. It can also be required when flying a missed approach or departure procedure from certain airports in the vicinity of mountain ranges. A shuttle procedure shall be executed in the pattern as published unless instructions contained in an ATC clearance direct otherwise.
