To ensure the aircraft stays within the obstacle clearance protected airspace during a shuttle descent or climb, it must not exceed the airspeed limit published on instrument procedure charts. If no specific airspeed limit is provided, the aircraft should maintain a maximum of 310 KIAS during climbs and adhere to the maximum published holding speeds during descents.

question: A pilot tracking outbound along R-360 of the YOW VOR is currently at 10 DME and is then cleared to hold inbound on R-360 between 15 and 20 DME. Assuming no wind, what is the recommended type of entry?
explanation: AIM RAC 10.5 Entry Procedures The recommended type of entry in the scenario presented in the question would be either a parallel or offset entry as depicted in the figure below.

question: A pilot tracking inbound along R-360 of the YOW VOR is currently 25 DME and is then cleared to hold inbound on R-360 between 15 and 20 DME. Assuming no wind, what is the recommended type of entry?
explanation: AIM RAC 10.5 Entry Procedures The recommended type of entry in the scenario presented in the question would be a direct entry as depicted in the figure below.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart What is the minimum altitude that an aircraft will be able to receive a continuous signal from the Calgary VOR/DME when 5NM north of NUVVE (A) along V112?
explanation: Minimum en route altitude (MEA) The altitude above sea level (ASL) between specified fixes on airways or air routes that assures acceptable navigational signal coverage and that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements.

question: The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft shall, except when taking off or landing, or when being radar-vectored by an air traffic control unit, ensure that the aircraft is operated at or above the . . . . . when the aircraft is on an airway or air route.
explanation: 602.124 Minimum Altitudes to Ensure Obstacle Clearance The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft shall, except when taking off or landing, or when being radar-vectored by an air traffic control unit, ensure that the aircraft is operated at or above the MOCA, when the aircraft is on an airway or air route; or the minimum altitude established by the Minister to ensure obstacle clearance and specified on an IFR chart, when the aircraft is within airspace in respect of which such a minimum altitude has been established.

question: Although pilots may fly IFR at the published MEA or MOCA, in the winter, when air temperatures are much lower than standard, pilots should operate at altitudes of at least . . . . . above the MEA or MOCA.
explanation: AIM RAC 8.5 Minimum IFR Altitudes Pressure altimeters are calibrated to indicate true altitude under standard atmospheric conditions, and any deviation from standard conditions will result in an erroneous altimeter reading. When temperatures are extremely cold, true altitudes will be significantly lower than indicated altitudes. Although pilots may fly IFR at the published MEA or MOCA, in the winter, when air temperatures are much lower than standard, pilots should operate at altitudes of at least 1,000 ft above the MEA or MOCA.

question: Refer to the Appendices: CAP CYXS LOC Z RWY 15 LO Chart Immediately after passing RAPID (A) along V301, an aeroplane operated under IFR is cleared to the Prince George Airport (B) for the LOC Z RWY 15 approach via YXS (C). The initial descent should be no lower than
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation Since the aeroplane is beyond 25 NM from YXS the MSA does not apply. Therefore the pilot should consider a descent to either the safe altitude within a radius of 100 NM or the MEA because the aircraft is established on an airway. Since the MEA is 8,600 feet which is lower than the safe altitude within a radius of 100 NM (12,800 ft) the aircraft can safely descend to the MEA until within 25 miles of YXS, at which point a descent to the MSA is appropriate.

question: A pilot is conducting the ILS RWY 30 to circle for RWY 12. After the circling manoeuvre has commenced, the pilot enters IMC, the recommended procedure is to
answers: remain at the MDA, turn towards the centre of the aerodrome and immediately advise ATC.
initiate a climbing turn towards the centre of the aerodrome and intercept the runway track.
initiate a climbing turn towards the centre of the aerodrome and fly the published missed approach for the ILS RWY 30.
initiate a climbing turn towards the centre of the aerodrome and fly any published missed approach for RWY 12.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.25 Missed Approach Procedure While Visually Manoevring in the Vicinity of the Aerodrome The pilot may have to conduct a missed approach after starting visual manoeuvres. There are no standard procedures in this situation. Thus, unless the pilot is familiar with the terrain, it is recommended that: A climb be initiated The aircraft be turned towards the centre of the aerodrome The aircraft be established, as closely as possible, in the missed approach procedure published for the instrument approach procedure just completed. With the runway in sight at circling MDA, the pilot should execute the missed approach if there is any doubt that the ceiling and visibility are inadequate for manoeuvring safely to the point of touchdown.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYOW ILS RWY 32 (C4189/22 NOTAMR C3327/22 A) CYOW B) 2206061430 C) 2206061800 E) ILS GP RWY 32 U/S) Refer to the NOTAM above. An aircraft equipped with an ILS, a DME and an IFR-certified GNSS receiver will arrive at CYOW at 1615Z on June 6 th , 2022. While enroute the aircraft’s DME receiver fails, select the correct statement.
answers: Since the onboard DME equipment is inoperative, a procedure turn is not authorized below 2,600 feet ASL.
The aircraft is adequately equipped to attempt the LOC/DME RWY 32.
The aircraft is adequately equipped to attempt the ILS/DME RWY 32
No approach may be attempted as DME equipment is required to identify fixes within the final segment.
explanation: AIP ENR 4.3.2 – Use of GNSS in Lieu of Ground-Based Aids (GNSS Substitution) According to the NOTAM reproduced below, the glide path for the ILS RWY 32 will be unserviceable between 1430Z and 1800Z on June 6, 2022. Since the ETA is 1615Z on the same day, the ILS is unserviceable. (C4189/22 NOTAMR C3327/22 A) CYOW B) 220606 1430 C) 220606 1800 E) ILS GP RWY 32 U/S) However, the LOC approach is still available despite the failed DME since the aircraft is equipped with an IFR-certified GNSS receiver and GNSS may be used in lieu of DME during departure, en route, terminal and approach operations. It may also be used in lieu of conventional navigation aids (VOR and NDB) for departure/enroute/terminal operations provided the following conditions are met: an integrity alert is not displayed; and fixes that are part of a terminal instrument procedure are named, charted and retrieved from a current navigation database GNSS may not be used in lieu of ground-based aids for: VOR and NDB final approach segment LNAV guidance on VOR or NDB instrument approach procedures; or LOC LNAV guidance. GNSS Substitution Examples

question: The angular width of the ILS localizer course is approximately
explanation: Localizer The width of the navigational beam may be varied from approximately 3° to 6°, with 5° being normal. It is adjusted to provide a track signal approximately 700 ft wide at the runway threshold. The width of the beam increases so that at 10 NM from the transmitter, the beam is approximately one mile wide.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYKA LOC C If the required visual references for landing are not established, an aircraft with a groundspeed of 110 knots and an indicated airspeed of 130 knots cannot descend below an indicated altitude of . . . . . and should execute a missed approach . . . . .
answers: 2,107 feet; 2 minutes and 4 seconds after passing YKA.
3,240 feet; 2 minutes and 4 seconds after passing YKA.
3,240 feet; within 10 miles after completing the procedure turn.
3,360 feet; 2 minutes and 4 seconds after passing YKA.
explanation: Rate of Descent and Timing When required, rate of descent and timing information is provided for the identified ground speed values. Rate of descent information is provided as a feet/minute value when CDA information is charted for the approach procedure and corresponds to the constant descent angle charted for the approach. Timing information is provided when the approach procedure contains a conventional MAP defined by distance from the FAF. The defined distance of the MAP from the FAF is translated into the number of minutes and seconds to be flown at the specified ground speed value.

question: When conducting the ILS RWY 04 and circling for runway 22, if a missed approach is executed while circling, a pilot unfamiliar with the surrounding terrain should initiate a climbing turn towards the centre of the aerodrome and
answers: fly the published missed approach for the circling procedure.
fly the published missed approach for RWY 04.
fly the published missed approach for RWY 22.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.25 Missed Approach Procedure While Visually Manoevring in the Vicinity of the Aerodrome A pilot may need to execute a missed approach after beginning visual maneuvers. In such cases, there are no standard procedures. Therefore, unless the pilot is familiar with the terrain, it is recommended to initiate a climbing turn toward the center of the aerodrome to establish the aircraft on the missed approach procedure for the instrument approach that was just completed.

question: Select the correct statement regarding an aircraft that has been cleared for a visual approach while on a STAR.
answers: A pilot must navigate in accordance with the published lateral profile until reaching the final approach course.
A pilot must follow the vertical and lateral profiles of a STAR while maintaining visual contact with the ground.
All charted speed restrictions must be respected.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.2.3.17 Cancelling Standard Terminal Arrival (STAR) Procedures Accepting a visual approach clearance automatically cancels the STAR procedure. A STAR may also be cancelled by ATC if required. If ATC cancels a STAR, the pilot should expect alternate instructions (either vectors to the final approach course, or a new route clearance). A STAR that has been cancelled may be reinstated by ATC.

question: IFR landings are governed by
explanation: CAP GEN – Operating Minima Take-offs for all Canadian aircraft are governed by visibility only, approach restrictions by RVR values only, and landings by published DH/MDAs only.

question: H1125/19 NOTAMN Q) CYTH/QGWAU/IV/NBO/E/000/999/5243N05321W999 A) CZQX CZQM CZUL B) 2205121230 C) 2205121330 E) LPV, LP AND WAAS-BASED LNAV/VNAV APCH NOT AVBL Prior to departure of an IFR flight, the NOTAM shown above was issued for the destination airport. Prior to commencing an approach, the PIC discovers that WAAS service is in fact available, the PIC
answers: may conduct a WAAS based approach.
may not conduct a WAAS based approach.
may only conduct a GPS overlay approach.
explanation: AIM COM 5.5.2 Wide Area Augmentation System (WAAS) NOTAM NAV CANADA has implemented a NOTAM system for users of WAAS avionics (TSO C145a/C146a). When WAAS service is expected not to be available for a duration of more than 15 minutes, a NOTAM will be issued. This typically implies a WAAS GEO satellite failure. Pilots should flight plan based on the assumption that the services referred to in a NOTAM will not be available. However, once they arrive at the aerodrome, they may discover that a service is in fact available, in which case they may use the approach safely if they so choose. When LPV, LP and WAAS-based LNAV/VNAV are not available, pilots may fly the LNAV procedure to the published MDA as this will almost always be available to pilots using WAAS avionics.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP – CYTH ILS RWY 06 The latest RVR report is R06/900V1000FT/U and the reported ground visibility is 1/4 SM. Is a private pilot with a valid instrument rating able to conduct the LOC/DME RWY 06?
answers: Yes, since the ground visibility is greater than or equal to 1/8 SM.
Yes, since the RVR is less than the minimum RVR but improving.
No, since the RVR is less than the minimum RVR required.
Yes, since the ground visibility is greater than or equal to 1/4 SM.
explanation: CAP GEN – Approach Ban – General Aviation An approach is authorized whenever: the lowest reported RVR for the runway is at or above minima (CAR 602.129), regardless of the reported ground visibility; the RVR is reported to be fluctuating above and below the minimum RVR; the ground visibility is at least 1/4 SM; the RVR for the runway is unavailable or not reported; or ATS is informed that an aircraft is on a training flight and will conduct a planned missed approach.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYXX ILS RWY 07 In the event of a missed approach when no missed approach instruction has been received, the pilot will
answers: enter a hold at the XX NDB and perform a direct entry.
continue on runway heading and climb to 3,000 ft.
enter a hold over the localizer and perform a direct entry.
enter a hold at the HUH VORTAC and perform a parallel entry.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.26 Missed Approach Procedures Whenever a pilot conducts a published missed approach from an instrument approach procedure, the aircraft must continue along the published final approach course to the published Missed Approach Point (MAP) and follow the published missed approach instructions. The pilot may climb immediately to the altitude specified in the missed approach procedure or assigned by ATC. In the event of a missed approach when no missed approach clearance has been received, the pilot will follow the published missed approach instructions. Should the pilot arrive at the missed approach holding fix prior to receiving further clearance, the pilot will: hold in a standard holding pattern on the inbound track used to arrive at the fix; if there is a published missed approach track to the fix, hold in a standard holding pattern inbound to the fix on this track; if there is a published shuttle or holding pattern at the fix, hold in this pattern regardless of the missed approach track to the fix; or if there are published missed approach holding instructions, hold in accordance with these. In this particular example, an aircraft, upon arriving at HUH must execute a parallel entry into the hold.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYYT ILS RWY 16 When cleared for the ILS RWY 16 and proceeding from OMRUM to the intermediate fix, the localizer signal should be considered reliable from the centreline to a maximum of
explanation: Localizer Area of Reliability The localizer is only guaranteed to be accurate up to 10° on either side of the runway to 18NM. At an angle of 35° on either side of runway centerline, the useful volume is limited to 10NM. However, a note on the approach chart indicates that the localizer is only reliable within 10° of the centreline.

question:
Which of the following is not a visual reference that may be used to continue an approach to landing?
A) Approach lights.
B) Approach slope indicator system.
C) Runway leadoff lights.
D) Airport beacon.
E) Stop bar lights.
explanation: CAP GEN – Landing Minima Pilots are prohibited from descending below the DH or MDA unless the required visual reference is established and can be maintained to complete a safe landing. When the required visual reference is not established or maintained, a missed approach must be initiated. Missed approaches initiated beyond the MAP may not be assured obstacle clearance. The visual references required by the pilot in order to continue the approach to a safe landing should include at least one of the following references for the intended runway and should be distinctly visible and identifiable to the pilot: the runway or runway markings; the runway threshold or threshold markings; the TDZ or TDZ markings; the approach lights; the approach slope indicator system; the runway identification lights (RILS); the threshold and runway end lights; the touchdown zone lights (TDZL); the parallel runway edge lights; or the runway centreline lights.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYXX ILS RWY 07 Unless otherwise instructed by ATC, the PIC of an aircraft executing a missed approach must
answers: turn direct to the HUH VORTAC.
enter a hold over the localizer and perform a direct entry.
enter a hold at the HUH VORTAC and perform a parallel entry.
enter a hold at the XX NDB and perform a direct entry.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.26 Missed Approach Procedures Whenever a pilot conducts a published missed approach from an instrument approach procedure, the aircraft must continue along the published final approach course to the published Missed Approach Point (MAP) and follow the published missed approach instructions. The pilot may climb immediately to the altitude specified in the missed approach procedure or assigned by ATC. In the event of a missed approach when no missed approach clearance has been received, the pilot will follow the published missed approach instructions.

question: The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report
answers: when passing the initial approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no initial approach fix exists.
the pilot-in-command’s intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft ten minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure.
the aircraft position when passing the final approach fix or five minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.12 IFR Reporting Procedures at Uncontrolled Aerodromes The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to or a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report the pilot-in-command’s intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft: five minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing when commencing a circling manoeuvre as soon as practicable after initiating a missed approach procedure Furthermore, the pilot-in-command must also report the aircraft’s position: when passing the fix outbound, where the pilot-in- command intends to conduct a procedure turn or, if no procedure turn is intended, when the aircraft first intercepts the final approach course, when passing the final approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists, and on final approach.

question: Select the correct statement regarding a straight-in landing from an IFR approach.
answers: Minima for a straight-in landing are published when the final approach track intersects the extended runway centre line within 5°.
If unable to determine wind speed and direction a pilot is expected to make an aerial visual inspection of the runway prior to landing.
If an instrument approach procedure does not publish straight-in minima, a straight-in landing is prohibited.
A straight-in approach to landing is prohibited if a procedure turn is depicted on an instrument approach procedure.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.13 IFR Procedures at an Uncontrolled Aerodrome in Uncontrolled Airspace A straight-in landing from an IFR approach should not be used at an uncontrolled aerodrome where air-ground advisory is not available to provide the wind direction, wind speed and runway condition reports required to conduct a safe landing. The pilot should determine the wind and verify that the runway is unobstructed before landing. Where pilots lack any necessary information, they are expected to ensure that a visual inspection of the runway is completed prior to landing. In some cases, this can only be accomplished by conducting a circling approach using the appropriate circling MDA.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYTH ILS RWY 06 If the latest RVR report is R06/900V1800FT/D, can the holder of a private pilot licence endorsed with a valid instrument rating attempt the ILS straight-in approach for runway 06?
answers: Yes, because a localized phenomenon is occurring.
No, since RVR B is not reported.
No, because the reported RVR is less than the advisory RVR.
Yes, because the RVR is varying between distances less than and greater than the minimum RVR.
explanation: CAG GEN – Approach Ban – General Aviation CAR 602.129 specifies that instrument approaches by general aviation aircraft are governed by RVR values only. With certain exceptions, pilots of aircraft are prohibited from completing an instrument approach past the FAF (or where there is no FAF, the point where the final approach course is intercepted) to a runway served by an RVR, if the RVR values as measured for that runway are below the following minima: There are many exceptions to the rule, however, an approach is authorized whenever: the lowest reported RVR for the runway is at or above minima, regardless of reported ground visibility; the RVR is reported to be fluctuating above and below the minimum RVR; the ground visibility is reported to be at least 1/4 mile; the RVR for the runway is unavailable or not reported; or ATS is informed that an aircraft is on a training flight and will conduct a planned missed approach.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYTH ILS RWY 06 The latest RVR report is R06/900V1000FT/U and the reported ground visibility is 1/8 SM. Can the holder of a private pilot licence endorsed with a valid instrument rating conduct the LOC/DME straight in approach for RWY 06?
answers: Yes, if flight visibility is also 1/4 SM or greater.
Yes, if the PIC has informed ATC that the aircraft is on a training flight and intends to initiate a missed approach procedure at or above the MDA.
No, an approach cannot be authorized since the reported visibility is less than 1/4 SM.
No, since the reported RVR is less than the published minimum RVR.
explanation: Approach Ban An approach is authorized whenever: the lowest reported RVR for the runway is at or above minima (CAR 602.129), regardless of reported ground visibility; the RVR is reported to be fluctuating above and below minimum RVR; the ground visibility is reported to be at least 1/4 mile; the RVR for the runway is unavailable or not reported; or ATS is informed that an aircraft is on a training flight and will conduct a planned missed approach.

question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding visual approaches?
answers: For a visual approach to be conducted, there must be an available instrument approach for the runway in use.
A visual approach is an instrument approach procedure.
In the event of a missed approach, pilots should contact ATC for further clearance as soon as possible.
A pilot must have all local traffic in sight in order to be cleared for a visual approach.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.2.1 Missed Approach A visual approach is not an IAP, and except for published visual approach procedures in the CAP, there are no procedures associated with a missed visual approach. If a go-around is necessary, aircraft operating at controlled airports will be issued an appropriate advisory, clearance or instruction by the tower to ensure that separation from other traffic is maintained. If these instructions are required for planning, pilots may request them before the approach clearance, or at any time before initiating the missed approach. ATC instructions will typically guide the pilot to either continue flying the issued IFR clearance, or integrate into the VFR circuit. Controlled Airports At controlled airports, until missed approach instructions are issued, ATC should anticipate that pilots conducting a go-around from a visual approach will: initially fly the runway heading follow the published missed approach instructions for the IAP requested by the pilots and acknowledged by ATC; or follow the published missed approach instructions for the IAP advertised on the ATIS. Uncontrolled Airports At uncontrolled airports, aircraft crews are required to remain clear of clouds and are expected to complete a landing as soon as possible. If a landing cannot be accomplished, the aircraft crew is required to: remain clear of clouds; and maintain separation from other airport traffic. The crew is also expected to contact ATC as soon as possible for further clearance. Separation from other IFR aircraft is only assured once further ATC clearance has been received and acknowledged by the aircraft crew.

question: Refer to the Appendices: CAP CYHM LOC or NDB RWY 30 Cold Weather Correction Chart If the temperature is -20°C, the MDA of the LOC approach for a category B aircraft is closest to
explanation: AIM RAC 9.17.1 Corrections for Temperature Pressure altimeters are calibrated to indicate true altitude under ISA conditions. Any deviation from ISA will result in an erroneous reading on the altimeter. In a case when the temperature is higher than standard, the true altitude will be higher than the figure indicated by the altimeter, and the true altitude will be lower when the temperature is lower than standard. The altimeter error may be significant and becomes extremely important when considering obstacle clearances in cold temperatures. The published minimum IFR altitudes must be adjusted when the ambient temperature on the surface is much lower than that predicted by the standard atmosphere. As a general rule, this is considered to be 0°C or, when MDAs/DAs are 1,000 ft HAA or higher, it begins at 10°C. Sample Calculation Determine the MDA and MDH. The MDA is 1,100 feet and the MDH is 329 feet. Using the data highlighted in the table below, along with the height above the elevation of the altimeter setting (329 feet), use linear interpretation to calculate the correction factor (52.9 feet). Add the correction to the indicated altitude and round up to the nearest ten. 1,100 + 52.9 = 1,152.9. Therefore, the correct MDA to apply is 1,160 feet.

question: The lateral portion of a missed approach can be commenced
answers: after arriving at the minimum descent altitude.
at any point along the final approach.
after passing the final approach fix.
explanation: AIM AIR 2.16.3 Vertical Path Control Techniques The lateral “turning” portion of the missed approach may not be executed prior to the MAP. However, the climb portion of a missed approach procedure may be commenced at any point along the final approach.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CZBF NDB RWY 28 The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct the NDB RWY 28 shall report the aircraft’s position A. when passing the Bathurst NDB outbound. B. after passing the intermediate fix. C. on final approach. D. three minutes before the estimated time of landing. E. when passing the final approach fix.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.12 IFR Reporting Procedures at Uncontrolled Aerodromes The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to or a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report the pilot-in-command’s intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft: five minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing when commencing a circling manoeuvre as soon as practicable after initiating a missed approach procedure Furthermore, the pilot-in-command must also report the aircraft’s position: when passing the fix outbound, where the pilot-in-command intends to conduct a procedure turn or, if no procedure turn is intended, when the aircraft first intercepts the final approach course when passing the final approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists on final approach

question: The ETA to a pilot’s destination is 1148Z. While en route the pilot determines that approach level RAIM will not be available between 1123Z and 1135Z. If the pilot is to attempt an RNAV approach using LNAV lines of minima the pilot should
answers: apply a correction to all procedure altitudes.
continue the approach and monitor the integrity of the GPS receiver.
slow down to ensure the ETA is after 1150Z.
attempt a GPS overlay approach and monitor the ground-based navigational aid.
explanation: AIM COM 5.4.2.1 Area Navigation (RNAV) Approaches with Lateral Guidance Only Pilots using TSO‑C129/C129a avionics should use the RAIM prediction feature (including known satellite outages obtained by NOTAM) to ensure that approach-level RAIM will be supported at the destination or alternate airport for the ETA (±15 min). This should be done before takeoff, and again prior to commencing a GNSS-based approach. If approach-level RAIM is not expected to be available, pilots should advise ATS as soon as practicable and state their intentions (e.g. delay the approach, fly another type of approach, proceed to alternate).

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CNP3 RNAV (GNSS) RWY 28 Neither a TAF nor an Aerodrome Advisory is available for CNP3. The only available approach is the RNAV (GNSS) RWY 28. Assuming no cumulonimbus clouds are forecast, in order to qualify as an IFR alternate there can be no cloud below . . . . . and the visibility must be at least . . . . .
explanation: CAP GEN – Operating Minima (Alternate) Since CNP3 is not served by a TAF or aerodrome advisory, a Graphic Area Forecast (GFA) is the only remaining alternative. Aerodromes served with a GFA may qualify as an alternate provided the forecast weather contains: no cloud lower than 1,000 ft above the lowest useable HAT/HAA; no cumulonimbus clouds; and, a visibility of 3 miles or greater. The lowest useable HAT is 493 feet as shown in the image below. 1,000 feet + 493 feet = 1,500 feet (rounded up to the nearest hundred). Therefore, for CNP3 to qualify as an alternate, there can be no cloud below 1,500 feet, and visibility must be at least 3 miles.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYKA NDB B Select the correct statement regarding an aircraft attempting the NDB B at CYKA.
answers: The MSA is based around the geographic centre of the aerodrome.
An aircraft may attempt a straight-in landing if the required visual reference is available in sufficient time to make a normal approach and landing.
In the event of a missed approach, the aircraft must climb to 4,900 feet then turn left direct to YKA.
Circling manoeuvres are authorized north of the runway.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.22 Straight-In Landing Minima for a straight-in landing are published when a normal rate of descent can be made from the final approach fix (FAF) to the runway threshold and when the final approach track intersects the extended runway centre-line within 30° and within a prescribed distance from the threshold. When either the normal rate of descent or the runway alignment exceeds the criteria, straight-in landing minima are not published and only circling minima apply. The fact that only circling minima are published does not preclude a pilot from landing straight-in if the required visual reference is available in sufficient time to make a normal approach and landing . The term straight-in used in connection with landing should not be confused with its use in straight-in approach minima. An ATC clearance for a straight-in approach merely clears the aircraft for an approach without first completing a procedure turn. The minima that will subsequently be used will be based on considerations such as the runway in use, published minima, aircraft category, etc.

question: The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft that intends to conduct an approach to or a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report
answers: the aircraft's position five minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists.
the pilot-in-command's intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no initial approach fix exists.
the aircraft's position ten minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing.
the pilot-in-command's intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft as soon as practicable after initiating a missed approach procedure.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.12 IFR Reporting Procedures at Uncontrolled Aerodromes The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to or a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report the pilot-in-command’s intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft: five minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing when commencing a circling manoeuvre as soon as practicable after initiating a missed approach procedure Furthermore, the pilot-in-command must also report the aircraft’s position: when passing the fix outbound, where the pilot-in-command intends to conduct a procedure turn or, if no procedure turn is intended, when the aircraft first intercepts the final approach course when passing the final approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists on final approach

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYHM LOC or NDB RWY 30 In order to maintain the published descent angle after ZHM, an aircraft attempting the NDB RWY 30 with a TAS of 130 knots and a groundspeed of 110 knots should descend at a rate of
explanation: CAP GEN – Rate of Descent and Timing When required, rate of descent and timing information is provided for the identified ground speed values. The rate of descent information is provided as a feet/minute value when CDA information is charted for the approach procedure and corresponds to the constant descent angle charted for the approach. Timing information is provided when the approach procedure contains a conventional MAP defined by the distance from the FAF. The defined distance of the MAP from the FAF is translated into the number of minutes and seconds to be flown at the specified ground speed value.

question: Refer to the Appendices: CAP CYHM LOC or NDB RWY 30 Cold Weather Correction Chart If the temperature is -10°C, the applicable MDA for a Category B aircraft attempting the NDB RWY 30 is closest to
explanation: AIM RAC 9.17.1 Corrections for Temperature Pressure altimeters are calibrated to indicate true altitude under ISA conditions. Any deviation from ISA will result in an erroneous reading on the altimeter. In a case when the temperature is higher than the ISA, the true altitude will be higher than the figure indicated by the altimeter, and the true altitude will be lower when the temperature is lower than the ISA. The altimeter error may be significant and becomes extremely important when considering obstacle clearances in cold temperatures. The published minimum IFR altitudes must be adjusted when the ambient temperature on the surface is much lower than that predicted by the standard atmosphere. As a general rule, this is considered to be 0°C or, when MDAs/DAs are 1,000 ft HAA or higher, it begins at 10°C. Sample Calculation Determine the MDA and MDH. The MDA is 1,240 feet and the MDH is 469 feet. Using the data highlighted in the table below, along with the height above the elevation of the altimeter setting (469 feet), use linear interpretation to calculate the correction factor (46.9 feet). Add the correction to the indicated altitude and round up to the nearest ten. 1,240 + 46.9 = 1,286.9. Therefore the MDA that should be used is 1,290 feet.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYYB VOR/DME RWY 08 If the pilot of an aircraft that is on final and 4.3 DME from YYB is notified that the RVR for runway 08 is 600 feet the pilot
answers: must initiate a missed approach since the reported visibility is below the advisory visibility.
must initiate a missed approach since the RVR is below 1,200 feet.
may continue the approach until the FAF at which point the RVR must be at or above 1,200 feet
explanation: AIM RAC 9.19.2 Approach Ban Since the aircraft has passed the approach ban point (LISVU 5 DME), it may continue the approach despite the RVR of 600 feet.

question: When conducting a circling approach, pilots should not descend below the MDA until
answers: the airport beacon is in sight.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.24 Circling Procedures An air traffic controller may specify manoeuvring in a certain direction or area due to traffic considerations, however, the selection of the procedure required to remain within the protected area and to accomplish a safe landing rests with the pilot. There can be no single procedure for conducting a circling approach due to variables such as runway layout, final approach track, wind velocity and weather conditions. The basic requirements are to keep the runway in sight after initial visual contact, and remain at the circling MDA until a normal landing is assured. Examples of various circling approach situations are illustrated below.

question: If visual reference to the ground is established before completion of a specified approach, the pilot should
answers: transition to a visual approach and notify ATC as soon as practicable.
cancel IFR to expedite the flow of traffic.
disregard instrument indications and continue via visual reference.
continue with the entire procedure unless further clearance is obtained.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.3 Approach Clearance When an approach clearance is issued, the published name of the approach is used to designate the type of approach if adherence to a particular procedure is required. If visual reference to the ground is established before completion of a specified approach, the aircraft should continue with the entire procedure unless further clearance is obtained.

question: Ground visibility will only impose an approach ban on commercial operators at aerodromes south of . . . . . latitude.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.19.2.3 Approach Ban – Commercial Operators Ground visibility will only impose an approach ban at aerodromes south of 60ºN latitude. If no RVR, runway visibility, or ground visibility is reported, there are no criteria to impose an approach ban.

question: FAF crossing altitude: 3,000 ft Runway elevation: 500 ft Distance between the FAF and runway threshold: 7 NM Groundspeed: 120 kt When conducting a non-precision approach, the appropriate descent rate using the SCDA technique would be closest to
explanation: Calculating Required Descent Rate In order to calculate the desired descent rate follow the steps outlined below.

question: Which of the following correctly identifies a circling only instrument approach procedure?
explanation: CAP GEN – Circling Only Procedures Approach procedures providing only circling minima are not associated with a specific runway. Instead, these procedures are identified using an alpha character after the navigation type starting with “A” and proceeding forward through the alphabet (A, B, C etc). The next sequential alpha character is assigned to the next circling-only procedure for the site based on its order within the Canadian instrument procedure inventory.

question: If two or more satellites will be unserviceable within 20 minutes of the ETA to an aerodrome only served by one RNAV (GNSS) approach, the approach
answers: may be conducted if a ground-based navaid is used in-lieu of GNSS during the final approach segment.
may be conducted if RAIM will be unavailable for no more than 15 minutes within ± 60 minutes of the ETA.
may not be attempted since the receiver cannot provide a navigation solution.
may not be attempted unless the aerodrome operating visibility is greater than 3 SM.
explanation: AIM RAC 5.9.1 Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) Approaches A procedure that meets the requirement to ensure that approach level RAIM will be available for TSO‑C129/C129a avionics is as follows. Verify via NOTAM that not more than one satellite outage is predicted for a period of 60 minutes before and after the expected ETA. If two or more satellites are anticipated to be unserviceable then it is necessary to determine if approach-level RAIM will be available by using commercially-available RAIM prediction software. The RAIM availability requirement is satisfied if the resulting prediction indicates that RAIM will be not unavailable for a total of more than 15 minutes during the ETA ±60-minute period. Example

question: IFR assigned altitudes accepted by a pilot
answers: should be adjusted to compensate for cold temperatures when operating below 1,000 feet AGL.
should be adjusted to compensate for cold temperatures only when the temperature is below -30°C.
should not be adjusted to compensate for cold temperatures.
should not be adjusted to compensate for cold temperatures unless advised to do so by ATC.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.17.1 Corrections for Temperature With respect to altitude corrections, the following procedures apply: IFR assigned altitudes may be either accepted or refused. Refusal in this case is based upon the pilot’s assessment of temperature effect on obstacle clearance. IFR assigned altitudes accepted by a pilot should not be adjusted to compensate for cold temperatures, i.e. if a pilot accepts “maintain 3,000”, an altitude correction should not be applied to 3,000 ft. Vectoring altitudes assigned by ATC are temperature corrected and require no temperature compensation by pilots. When altitude corrections are applied to a published mandatory altitude or missed approach holding altitude, pilots should advise ATC of the temperature-corrected altitude prior to crossing the associated waypoint.

question: The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report the
answers: estimated time of landing within five nautical miles of the initial approach fix.
approach procedure to be used within ten miles of the initial approach fix.
estimated time of landing five minutes before commencing an approach procedure.
approach procedure to be used five minutes before the commencement of the approach.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.12 IFR Reporting Procedures at Uncontrolled Aerodromes The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to or a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report the pilot-in-command’s intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft: five minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing when commencing a circling manoeuvre as soon as practicable after initiating a missed approach procedure Furthermore, the pilot-in-command must also report the aircraft’s position: when passing the fix outbound, where the pilot-in- command intends to conduct a procedure turn or, if no procedure turn is intended, when the aircraft first intercepts the final approach course, when passing the final approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists, and on final approach.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CZBB RNAV (GNSS) RWY 31 CAP CZBB RNAV (GNSS) RWY 07 An aircraft with an approach speed of 110 KIAS has been cleared for the RNAV (GNSS) 31 and will circle to land on runway 07. The applicable MDA and recommended visibility are . . . . . and . . . . . respectively.
explanation: Circling Minima and Performance Categories Aircraft performance differences influence the required airspace and visibility for safe maneuvers. To ensure proper obstacle clearance and establish landing and departure minima, aircraft are divided into five categories based on speed. Each category has its own set of instrument approach minima, which must be used depending on the aircraft’s indicated airspeed during different phases of flight. For instance, if an aircraft is flying a straight-in approach at 90 KIAS, Category A minima apply. However, if that same aircraft maneuvers during a circling approach at 110 KIAS, the Category B circling minima would be used instead. Circling is the term used to describe an IFR procedure that is conducted by visually manoeuvring an aircraft, after completing an instrument approach, into position for landing on a runway which is not suitably located for a straight-in landing. When determining the correct circling minima refer to the MDA and recommend visibility of the instrument approach procedure used prior to the commencement of the circling manoeuvre. In this particular case, the circling MDA and recommended visibility for a category B aircraft can be determined by referring to the RNAV (GNSS) RWY 31 chart as shown below.

question:
Which of the following are requirements for a contact approach?
A) Familiarity with the aerodrome environment, including local area obstacles, terrain and noise-sensitive areas.
B) The aircraft must be operated clear of clouds and flight visibility must be at least 1 NM.
C) The aircraft must be flown at an altitude of at least 1,000 feet above the highest obstacle located within a horizontal radius of 5 NM.
D) The pilot must request the approach and there must be an approved functioning instrument approach for the runway in use.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.1 Contact Approach A contact approach is an approach wherein an aircraft on an IFR flight plan or flight itinerary having an ATC clearance, operating clear of clouds with at least 1 NM flight visibility and a reasonable expectation of continuing to the destination airport in those conditions, may deviate from the IAP and proceed to the destination airport by visual reference to the surface of the earth. In accordance with CAR 602.124, the aircraft shall be flown at an altitude of at least 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle located within a horizontal radius of 5 NM from the estimated position of the aircraft in flight until the required visual reference is acquired in order to conduct a normal landing. Pilots are cautioned that conducting a contact approach in minimum visibility conditions introduces hazards to flight not experienced when flying IFR procedures. This type of approach will only be authorized by ATC when the pilot requests it, and there is an approved functioning instrument approach or a published GNSS approach for the airport.

question:
Select the correct statements regarding missed approach procedures.
A) If specific missed approach instructions have been received and acknowledged, the pilot is required to comply with the new missed approach instructions before proceeding on course.
B) If a clearance to another destination has been received, the pilot shall, in the absence of other instructions, carry out the published missed approach instructions until at an altitude which will ensure adequate obstacle clearance before proceeding on course.
C) Should the pilot arrive at the missed approach holding fix prior to receiving further clearance, the pilot will hold in a standard holding pattern on the inbound track used to arrive at the fix.
D) The term "on missed approach, right turn climb on course" when directed to a pilot from ATC relieves the pilot from the responsibility to ensure terrain and obstacle clearance.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.26 Missed Approach Procedures Whenever a pilot conducts a published missed approach from an instrument approach procedure, the aircraft must continue along the published final approach course to the published Missed Approach Point (MAP) and follow the published missed approach instructions. The pilot may climb immediately to the altitude specified in the missed approach procedure or assigned by ATC. In the event of a missed approach when no missed approach clearance has been received, the pilot will follow the published missed approach instructions. Should the pilot arrive at the missed approach holding fix prior to receiving further clearance, the pilot will: hold in a standard holding pattern on the inbound track used to arrive at the fix; if there is a published missed approach track to the fix, hold in a standard holding pattern inbound to the fix on this track; if there is a published shuttle or holding pattern at the fix, hold in this pattern regardless of the missed approach track to the fix; or if there are published missed approach holding instructions, hold in accordance with these. If a clearance to another destination has been received, the pilot shall, in the absence of other instructions, carry out the published missed approach instructions until at an altitude which will ensure adequate obstacle clearance before proceeding on course. If specific missed approach instructions have been received and acknowledged, the pilot is required to comply with the new missed approach instructions before proceeding on course, e.g. “on missed approach, climb runway heading to 3,000 feet; right turn, climb on course” or “on missed approach, climb straight ahead to the BRAVO NDB before proceeding on course”. Civil and military air traffic control procedures do not require the air traffic controller to provide terrain and obstacle clearance in their missed approach instructions. Terms such as “on missed approach, right turn climb on course” or “on missed approach, left turn on course” are not to be considered specific missed approach instructions. It remains the pilot’s responsibility to ensure terrain and obstacle avoidance and clearance.

question: What is the recommended procedure to follow in the event of a missed approach during a circling maneuver?
answers: Initiate a climbing turn towards the centre of the aerodrome and fly the published missed approach for the IAP just completed.
Climb on runway heading to the MSA.
Initiate a shuttle climb to the MSA overhead the aerodrome.
Initiate a climbing turn towards the centre of the aerodrome and fly the published missed approach associated with the intended landing runway.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.25 Missed Approach Procedure While Visually Manoevring in the Vicinity of the Aerodrome The pilot may have to conduct a missed approach after starting visual manoeuvres. There are no standard procedures in this situation. Thus, unless the pilot is familiar with the terrain, it is recommended that: A climb be initiated The aircraft be turned towards the centre of the aerodrome The aircraft be established, as closely as possible, in the missed approach procedure published for the instrument approach procedure just completed. With the runway in sight at circling MDA, the pilot should execute the missed approach if there is any doubt that the ceiling and visibility are inadequate for manoeuvring safely to the point of touchdown.

question:
Select the correct statement regarding an aircraft that is cleared by ATC for an approach to an uncontrolled airport.
A) ATC will remain responsible for obstacle clearance.
B) This clearance constitutes authority for the pilot to execute a contact or visual approach.
C) The pilot must advise ATC of the type of approach.
D) The pilot must advise ATC of the landing runway.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.3 Approach Clearance At some locations during periods of light traffic, controllers may issue clearances that do not specify the type of approach. When such a clearance is issued by ATC and accepted by the pilot, the pilot has the option of conducting any published instrument approach procedure. As soon as practicable after receipt of this type of clearance, it is the pilot’s responsibility to advise ATC of the type of published instrument approach procedure that will be carried out, the landing runway and the intended route to be flown. This clearance does not constitute authority for the pilot to execute a contact or visual approach. Should the pilot prefer to conduct a visual approach (published or non-published) or a contact approach, the pilot must specifically communicate that request to the controller.

question: Select the correct statement regarding contact and visual approaches.
answers: ATC may initiate a visual approach but not a contact approach.
ATC may initiate a contact approach as soon as a pilot reports the landing aerodrome in sight, however in order to conduct a visual approach visibility must be greater than 3 SM.
A pilot may request a visual approach after sighting the landing aerodrome and ATC may initiate a contact approach only when visibility is greater than 3 SM.
Weather minima applied to a contact approach are more conservative than those applied to a visual approach.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.1 – 9.6.2 Contact and Visual Approach A contact approach is an approach wherein an aircraft on an IFR flight plan or flight itinerary having an ATC clearance, operating clear of clouds with at least 1 NM flight visibility and a reasonable expectation of continuing to the destination airport in those conditions, may deviate from the IAP and proceed to the destination airport by visual reference to the surface of the earth. A contact approach will only be authorized by ATC when the pilot requests it and there is an approved functioning instrument approach or a published GNSS approach for the airport. A visual approach is an approach wherein an aircraft on an IFR flight plan, operating in VMC under the control of ATC and having ATC authorization, may proceed to the destination airport. It permits aircraft to manage their lateral and vertical flight profiles according to the runway. To gain operational advantages in a surveillance environment, the pilot may request a visual approach, or ATC may initiate one. As both methods differ in terms of flying distance, it is good airmanship for pilots to advise ATC of the planned flight path, especially if it is likely to be unexpected or unpredictable, such as cases involving the widening of the base leg or the inability to shorten the flying distance as anticipated by ATC.

question: Should the pilot arrive at the missed approach holding fix prior to receiving further clearance, the pilot will
answers: commence a climb to the MSA and maintain runway heading.
climb in VMC and orbit over the aerodrome until receiving further clearance.
hold in a left-hand holding pattern on the inbound track used to arrive at the fix.
hold in a standard holding pattern inbound to the fix if there is a published missed approach track to the fix.
explanation: AIMRAC 9.26 Missed Approach Procedures Whenever a pilot conducts a published missed approach from an instrument approach procedure, the aircraft must continue along the published final approach course to the published Missed Approach Point (MAP) and follow the published missed approach instructions. The pilot may climb immediately to the altitude specified in the missed approach procedure or assigned by ATC. In the event of a missed approach when no missed approach clearance has been received, the pilot will follow the published missed approach instructions. Should the pilot arrive at the missed approach holding fix prior to receiving further clearance, the pilot will: hold in a standard holding pattern on the inbound track used to arrive at the fix; if there is a published missed approach track to the fix, hold in a standard holding pattern inbound to the fix on this track; if there is a published shuttle or holding pattern at the fix, hold in this pattern regardless of the missed approach track to the fix; or if there are published missed approach holding instructions, hold in accordance with these. If a clearance to another destination has been received, the pilot shall, in the absence of other instructions, carry out the published missed approach instructions until at an altitude which will ensure adequate obstacle clearance before proceeding on course. If further clearance has not been received prior to reaching OMLUL, aircraft are expected to enter the hold as published.

question: When conducting a contact approach ATC will ensure separation from other
answers: IFR and VFR aircraft and terrain clearance.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.1 Contact Approach A contact approach is an approach wherein an aircraft on an IFR flight plan or flight itinerary having an ATC clearance, operating clear of clouds with at least 1 NM flight visibility and a reasonable expectation of continuing to the destination airport in those conditions, may deviate from the IAP and proceed to the destination airport by visual reference to the surface of the earth. In accordance with CAR 602.124, the aircraft shall be flown at an altitude of at least 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle located within a horizontal radius of 5 NM from the estimated position of the aircraft in flight until the required visual reference is acquired in order to conduct a normal landing. Pilots are cautioned that conducting a contact approach in minimum visibility conditions introduces hazards to flight not experienced when flying IFR procedures. Familiarity with the aerodrome environment, including local area obstacles, terrain, noise sensitive areas, Class F airspace and aerodrome layout, is paramount for a successful contact approach in minimum visibility conditions. Pilots are responsible for the adherence to published noise abatement procedures and compliance with any restrictions that may apply to Class F airspace when conducting a contact approach. NOTE: This type of approach will only be authorized by ATC when: The pilot requests it; and There is an approved functioning instrument approach, a published GNSS or a GNSS overlay approach for the airport. An aircraft that requests a contact approach to an airport served only by a GNSS approach is indicating to ATS that the pilot understands that no ground based approach is available and is confirming that it is able to conduct a GNSS approach. ATC will ensure IFR separation from other IFR flights and will issue specific missed approach instructions if there is any doubt that a landing will be accomplished. Pilots are cautioned that when any missed approach is initiated while conducting a contact approach, obstacle and terrain avoidance is the pilot’s responsibility even though specific missed approach instructions may have been issued by ATC. ATC only ensures appropriate IFR separation from other IFR aircraft during contact approaches.

question: At a controlled airport ATC
answers: is responsible for maintaining adequate wake turbulence separation between aircraft conducting visual approaches.
cannot initiate a visual approach until a pilot reports sighting the preceding aircraft.
cannot initiate a visual approach unless the reported ceiling at the destination airport is 1,000 ft or more above the minimum IFR altitude.
may initiate a visual approach if the pilot reports sighting the airport but not the preceding aircraft.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.2 Visual Approach A visual approach is one in which a pilot manages the lateral and vertical profiles of the aircraft visually with reference to the runway. The aircraft is still operating under IFR but must remain in VMC. Provided that the reported ceiling at the destination airport is at least 500 feet above the minimum IFR altitude and ground visibility is at least 3SM, pilots may request, or ATC may initiate a visual approach. This is contingent on the pilot reporting to ATC that the airport is in sight. At controlled airports, pilots must also have any preceding traffic in sight. If the pilot is unable to visually identify the aircraft ahead, ATC will maintain separation until the preceding aircraft has landed or until the pilot confirms that the traffic is in sight.

question: In the event of a missed approach during a contact approach where ATC provides missed approach instructions
answers: obstacle and terrain clearance is the pilot's responsibility.
traffic separation is the responsibility of the pilot and obstacle and terrain clearance is the responsibility of ATC.
obstacle and terrain clearance is a shared responsibility between ATC and the pilot.
obstacle and terrain clearance is the responsibility of ATC.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.1 Contact Approach A contact approach is an approach wherein an aircraft on an IFR flight plan or flight itinerary having an ATC clearance, operating clear of clouds with at least 1 NM flight visibility and a reasonable expectation of continuing to the destination airport in those conditions, may deviate from the IAP and proceed to the destination airport by visual reference to the surface of the earth. Pilots are cautioned that conducting a contact approach in minimum visibility conditions introduces hazards to flight not experienced when flying IFR procedures. Familiarity with the aerodrome environment, including local area obstacles, terrain, noise sensitive areas, Class F airspace and aerodrome layout, is paramount for a successful contact approach in minimum visibility conditions. Pilots are responsible for the adherence to published noise abatement procedures and compliance with any restrictions that may apply to Class F airspace when conducting a contact approach.

question: The PIC of an IFR aircraft intending to conduct an approach at an uncontrolled aerodrome must broadcast their intentions
answers: 10 minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure.
5 minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure.
only if the aerodrome is within controlled airspace.
only if the approach will culminate in a circling manoeuvre.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.12 IFR Reporting Procedures at Uncontrolled Aerodromes The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to or a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report the pilot-in-command’s intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft: five minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing when commencing a circling manoeuvre as soon as practicable after initiating a missed approach procedure Furthermore, the pilot-in-command must also report the aircraft’s position: when passing the fix outbound, where the pilot-in-command intends to conduct a procedure turn or, if no procedure turn is intended, when the aircraft first intercepts the final approach course when passing the final approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists on final approach

question: A contact approach requires
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.1 Contact Approach A contact approach is an approach wherein an aircraft on an IFR flight plan or flight itinerary having an ATC clearance, operating clear of clouds with at least 1 NM flight visibility and a reasonable expectation of continuing to the destination airport in those conditions, may deviate from the IAP and proceed to the destination airport by visual reference to the surface of the earth. In accordance with CAR 602.124, the aircraft must be flown at an altitude of at least 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle located within a horizontal radius of 5 NM from the estimated position of the aircraft in flight until the required visual reference is acquired in order to conduct a normal landing. Pilots are cautioned that conducting a contact approach in minimum visibility conditions introduces hazards to flight not experienced when flying IFR procedures. Familiarity with the aerodrome environment, including local area obstacles, terrain, noise sensitive areas, Class F airspace and aerodrome layout, is paramount for a successful contact approach in minimum visibility conditions. Pilots are responsible for the adherence to published noise abatement procedures and compliance with any restrictions that may apply to Class F airspace when conducting a contact approach. NOTE: This type of approach will only be authorized by ATC when: the pilot requests it; and there is an approved functioning instrument approach or a published GNSS approach for the airport.

question:
Select the correct statements regarding an aircraft that has been cleared by ATC for an approach to an uncontrolled airport while the aircraft is established on an airway.
A) It is the pilot's responsibility to advise ATC of the type of published instrument approach procedure that will be carried out and the landing runway.
B) ATC will remain responsible for obstacle clearance throughout the entirety of the approach.
C) The clearance constitutes authority for the pilot to execute a contact or visual approach.
D) It is the pilot's responsibility to advise ATC of the intended route to be flown.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.3 Approach Clearance At some locations during periods of light traffic, controllers may issue clearances that do not specify the type of approach. When such a clearance is issued by ATC and accepted by the pilot, the pilot has the option of conducting any published instrument approach procedure. In addition, the pilot also has the option of proceeding by the route so cleared by ATC in a previous clearance, by any published transition or feeder route associated with the selected procedure, or by a route present position direct to a fix associated with the selected instrument approach procedure. Pilots who choose to proceed to the instrument procedure fix via a route that is off an airway, air route or transition are responsible for maintaining the appropriate obstacle clearance, complying with noise abatement procedures and remaining clear of Class F airspace. As soon as practicable after receipt of this type of clearance, it is the pilot’s responsibility to advise ATC of the type of published instrument approach procedure that will be carried out, the landing runway and the intended route to be flown. This clearance does not constitute authority for the pilot to execute a contact or visual approach. Should the pilot prefer to conduct a visual approach (published or non-published) or a contact approach, the pilot must specifically communicate that request to the controller.

question: The RAIM alert limit for non-precision approaches is
explanation: AIM COM 5.3.1 Aircraft-Based Augmentation System (ABAS) RAIM and FDE functions in current IFR-certified avionics are considered ABAS. RAIM can provide the integrity for the en route, terminal, and NPA phases of flight. FDE improves the continuity of operation in the event of a satellite failure and can support primary-means oceanic operations. RAIM uses extra satellites in view to compare solutions and detect problems. It usually takes four satellites to compute a navigation solution and a minimum of five for RAIM to function. The availability of RAIM is a function of the number of visible satellites and their geometry. It is complicated by the movement of satellites relative to a coverage area and temporary satellite outages resulting from scheduled maintenance or failures. If the number of satellites in view and their geometry do not support the applicable alert limit (2 NM en route, 1 NM terminal and 0.3 NM NPA), RAIM cannot guarantee the integrity of the position solution. Note that this does not imply a satellite malfunction. In this case, the RAIM function in the avionics will alert the pilot, but will continue providing a navigation solution. Except in cases of emergency, pilots must discontinue using GNSS for IFR navigation when such an alert occurs.

question: FAF crossing altitude: 4,000 ft Runway elevation: 1,500 ft Distance between the FAF and runway threshold: 6.9 NM Groundspeed: 110 kt When conducting a non-precision approach, the appropriate descent rate using the SCDA technique would be closest to
explanation: Calculating Required Descent Rate In order to calculate the desired descent rate follow the steps outlined below.

question: Pilots should ensure that approach level RAIM will be supported at the destination or alternate airport for the ETA ±15 min. This should be done
answers: before takeoff, and again prior to commencing a GNSS-based approach.
15 minutes prior to commencing a GNSS-based approach.
60 minutes prior to commencing a GNSS-based approach.
after takeoff, and again 15 minutes prior to commencing a GNSS-based approach.
explanation: AIM COM 5.4.2.1 Area Navigation (RNAV) Approaches with Lateral Guidance Only Pilots using TSO-C129/C129a avionics should use the RAIM prediction feature (including known satellite outages obtained by NOTAM at KGPS) to ensure that approach-level RAIM will be supported at the destination or alternate airport for the ETA (±15 min). This should be done before takeoff, and again prior to commencing a GNSS-based approach. If approach-level RAIM is not expected to be available, pilots should advise ATS as soon as practicable and state their intentions (e.g. delay the approach, fly another type of approach, proceed to alternate).

question: Minima for a straight-in landing are usually published when the final approach track intersects the extended runway centre-line within . . . . . and within a prescribed distance from the threshold.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.22 Straight-In Landing Minima Minima for a straight-in landing are published when a normal rate of descent can be made from the final approach fix (FAF) to the runway threshold and when the final approach track intersects the extended runway centre-line within 30° and within a prescribed distance from the threshold. Otherwise, only circling minima are published. However, the fact that only circling minima are published does not preclude a pilot from landing straight-in if the required visual reference is available in sufficient time to make a normal approach and landing.

question: Only RVR ‘B’ is currently reported by ATC. The minimum RVR value needed to conduct an instrument approach is
explanation: AIM RAC 9.19.2 Approach Ban CAR 602.129 specifies that instrument approaches by general aviation aircraft are governed by RVR values only. With certain exceptions, pilots of aircraft are prohibited from completing an instrument approach past the FAF (or where there is no FAF, the point where the final approach course is intercepted) to a runway served by an RVR, if the RVR values as measured for that runway are below the following minima: The following exceptions to the above prohibitions apply to all aircraft when: the below-minima RVR report is received, the aircraft is inbound on approach and has passed the FAF, or where there is no FAF, the point where the final approach course is intercepted; the pilot-in-command has informed the appropriate ATC unit that the aircraft is on a training flight and that the pilot-in-command intends to initiate a missed approach procedure at or above the DH or the MDA, as appropriate; the RVR is varying between distances less than and greater than the minimum RVR; the RVR is less than the minimum RVR, and the ground visibility at the aerodrome where the runway is located is reported to be at least one-quarter statute mile; or the pilot-in-command is conducting a precision approach to CAT III minima.

question: Select the correct statement regarding WAAS-based instrument approach procedures.
answers: A NOTAM will be issued if WAAS-based LNAV/VNAV service is unavailable for a period of more than 15 minutes.
If operating in an area of marginal WAAS availability a pilot may not attempt an RNAV approach using LPV lines of minima.
Altitude corrections for temperature must be applied if the OAT falls below the standard temperature.
Vertical guidance along the final approach segment is entirely dependent on a remote altimeter setting.
explanation: AIM COM 5.5.2 Wide Area Augmentation System (WAAS) NOTAM A NOTAM will be issued whenever the FAA advises NAV CANADA that LPV, LP and WAAS-based LNAV/VNAV service is unavailable for more than 15 minutes. Pilots should flight plan based on the assumption that the services referred to in a NOTAM will not be available. However, once they arrive at the aerodrome, they may discover that a service is in fact available, in which case they may use the approach safely if they so choose. When LPV, LP and WAAS-based LNAV/VNAV are not available, pilots may fly the LNAV procedure to the published MDA, as this will almost always be available to pilots using WAAS avionics.

question: Refer to the Appendices: LO Chart CAP CYXS LOC Z RWY 15 After passing RAPID (A) on V301 direct to YXS, an aircraft without an onboard GNSS receiver is cleared for the LOC Z RWY 15 into CYXS. If the pilot elects to remain on V301, the initial descent should be to no lower than
explanation: Minimum Enroute Altitude Since the aircraft is established on an airway it can descend to the MEA which is 8,600 feet. Descent to the MSA however, cannot be commenced because the aircraft is still beyond 25 NM from YXS when overhead RAPID.

question: The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report
answers: the aircraft's position when passing the final approach fix or two minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists.
the aircraft's position when passing the final approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists.
their intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft ten minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing.
when passing the initial approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no initial approach fix exists.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.12 IFR Reporting Procedures at Uncontrolled Aerodromes The pilot-in-command of an IFR aircraft who intends to conduct an approach to or a landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome shall report the pilot-in-command’s intentions regarding the operation of the aircraft: five minutes before the estimated time of commencing the approach procedure, stating the estimated time of landing when commencing a circling manoeuvre as soon as practicable after initiating a missed approach procedure Furthermore, the pilot-in-command must also report the aircraft’s position: when passing the fix outbound, where the pilot-in- command intends to conduct a procedure turn or, if no procedure turn is intended, when the aircraft first intercepts the final approach course, when passing the final approach fix or three minutes before the estimated time of landing where no final approach fix exists, and on final approach.

question: If a landing cannot be accomplished after a visual approach into an uncontrolled airport, the pilot should
answers: remain clear of clouds and maintain separation from other airport traffic.
maintain a distance of 500 feet from all clouds and maintain separation from other airport traffic.
explanation: AIM RAC 9.6.2.1 Missed Approach A visual approach is not an instrument approach procedure, and except for published visual approach procedures in the CAP, visual approaches have no missed approach segment. If a landing cannot be accomplished, pilots are required to remain clear of clouds and maintain separation from other airport traffic. Pilots are also expected to contact ATC as soon as possible for further clearance. ATC separation from other IFR aircraft is only assured once further ATC clearance has been received and acknowledged by the aircraft crew.

question: During a prolonged coordinated turn in IMC a pilot may feel the sensation of
explanation: Prolonged Turns in IMC During a prolonged turn in IMC, the vestibular system will no longer be able to sense that the aircraft is in a turn. Therefore, without any visual reference to the ground, a pilot may feel as though the aircraft is straight and level.

question: The primary reason a pilot may become disoriented during a white-out is
answers: the eye's sensitivity to light reflected off white surfaces.
explanation: Whiteout An atmospheric optical phenomenon of the polar regions in which the observer appears to be engulfed in a uniformly white glow. Shadows, the horizon, and clouds are not discernible. Sense of depth and orientation is lost and only very dark, nearby objects can be seen. Whiteout occurs over an unbroken snow cover beneath a uniformly overcast sky. Blowing snow may be an additional cause.

question: Spatial disorientation is most likely to occur if
answers: body sensations are used to determine flight attitudes.
vision is used to overcome the limitations of the vestibular system.
the eyes are moved sporadically while scanning for traffic.
the pilot is suffering from the effects of fatigue.
explanation: Spatial Disorientation and Illusions Spatial disorientation specifically refers to the lack of orientation with regard to the position, attitude, or movement of the airplane in space. The body uses three integrated systems that work together to ascertain orientation and movement in space. Vestibular system – Organs found in the inner ear that sense position by the way we are balanced Somatosensory system – Nerves in the skin, muscles, and joints that, along with hearing, sense position based on gravity, feeling, and sound Visual system – Eyes, which sense position based on what is seen All this information comes together in the brain and, most of the time, the three streams of information agree, giving a clear idea of where and how the body is moving. Flying can sometimes cause these systems to supply conflicting information to the brain, which can lead to disorientation. During a flight in visual meteorological conditions (VMC), the eyes are the major orientation source and usually prevail over false sensations from other sensory systems. When these visual cues are removed, as they are in instrument meteorological conditions (IMC), false sensations can cause a pilot to quickly become disoriented.

question: An aircraft flying an approach into a strong head wind encounters a sudden tailwind near the ground. The wind shear hazard to be expected is a sudden
answers: decrease in indicated airspeed and loss of lift.
decrease in groundspeed and loss of lift.
increase in groundspeed and increase in lift.
increase in indicated airspeed and increase in lift.
explanation: Wind Shear Wind shear is a difference in wind speed or direction over a relatively short distance in the atmosphere. It is commonly found near microbursts, downbursts, thunderstorms, fronts, low-level jets, mountains, and inversions. Wind shear has significant effects on the control of an aircraft, and it has been the sole or contributing cause of many aircraft accidents. If flying through an area with an increasing headwind, or a decreasing tailwind the indicated airspeed will increase. If flying through an area with a decreasing headwind, or an increasing tailwind the indicated airspeed will decrease.

question: Hypoxia is hazardous because it
answers: creates a feeling of pins and needles in the hands and feet.
explanation: AIM AIR 3.2.1 Hypoxia The literal definition of hypoxia is “low oxygen”. Therefore, hypoxia implies a lack of sufficient oxygen for the body to operate normally. Its onset is insidious and may be accompanied by a feeling of well-being, known as euphoria. Even minor hypoxia impairs night vision and slows reaction time. More serious hypoxia interferes with reasoning, gives rise to unusual fatigue and, finally, results in a loss of consciousness. Hypoxia is classified into four different types; all are relevant to pilots and merit consideration.

question: Situational awareness is the
answers: perception of environmental elements and the projection of their status into the near future.
analysis of the probability and possible severity of an accident from exposure to various hazards.
effective use of all resources including crew members to achieve safe and efficient operations.
measure of the pilot's ability to apply appropriate decision-making techniques.
explanation: Situational Awareness Situational awareness is the accurate perception and understanding of all the factors and risk elements that affect safety before, during, and after the flight.
