explanation: AIM MET 2.0 Pilot Weather Reports (PIREPs) UACN10 CYXU 032133 YZ UA /OV YXU 090010 /TM 2120 /FL080 /TP PA31 /SK 020BKN040 110OVC /TA ‐12 /WV 030045 /TB MDT BLO 040 /IC LGT RIME 020‐040 /RM NIL TURB CYYZ‐CYHM 032133 Date/Time of Issue: 3rd day of the month, at 2133Z.

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN34 Which statement is correct regarding a flight departing CYHZ at 0100Z destined for CYQX?
answers: Upper level mechanical turbulence is forecast during the descent into CYQX.
Turbulence is forecast between 3,000 feet and 6,000 feet during the cruise portion of the flight.
Nil to light turbulence is forecast during the initial departure and climb from CYHZ.
If the flight is conducted at 5,500 feet, it will overfly an area of severe mixed icing.
explanation: AIM MET 4.11 Clouds and Weather Chart If no turbulence is reported, as is the case in the immediate vicinity of CYHZ, as per the “Comments” section, nil to light turbulence is expected.

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN34 What weather conditions can be expected at (B)?
answers: Local areas of blowing snow and ceilings of 400 feet AGL.
An overcast layer with a base between 3,000 feet and 5,000 feet.
explanation: AIM MET 4.9 Comments Box The comments box provides information that the weather forecaster considers important. It is also used to describe elements that are difficult to render pictorially or, if added to the depiction, would cause the chart to become cluttered. In this particular example, (B) depicts an area of BKN cloud layers between 5,000 feet and 8,000 feet. However, the forecast also notes localized areas with visibility of 1SM in blowing snow and a ceiling of 400 feet AGL.

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN33 Determine what clouds if any, are forecasted over CYGL.
answers: No clouds are forecast over CYGL.
Localized fog with a base at 200 feet and tops at 1,500 feet.
A broken layer with a base at 8,000 feet and tops at 18,000 feet.
A broken layer with a base at 12,000 feet and tops at 24,000 feet.
explanation: AIM MET 4.0 Graphic Area Forecast A scalloped border encloses organized areas of clouds where the sky condition is either broken (BKN) or overcast (OVC). The GFA provided depicts CYGL within a scalloped border indicating an organized area of broken layers with a base of 12,000 feet and tops at 24,000 feet.

question: Except as required over higher terrain, low-level jet streams (LLJs) are only included on a GFA at or below
explanation: AIM MET 4.12 Low Level Jets (LLJs) Included on the GFA when it is expected to have a peak core speed of 50 knots or more. It may be included at speeds between 35 and 45 kt when significant associated turbulence or shear is expected. In general, LLJs are not included if they are above 6,000 ft ASL, except as required over higher terrain.

question: Refer to the Appendices: GFACN33 (1/2) GFACN33 (2/2) What kind of meteorological conditions can be expected on an IFR flight departing from CYGK to CYUL at 0700Z?
answers: Localized moderate mixed icing and no turbulence.
Moderate mixed icing and moderate mechanical turbulence.
Nil to light rime icing in cloud above the freezing level and nil to light turbulence.
explanation: AIM MET 4.0 Graphic Area Forecast With a departure of 0700Z, a pilot can expect to avoid the icing and turbulence from the advancing cold front.

question: TAF CYXE 281139Z 2812/2912 24010G25KT WS011/ 27050KT 3SM –SN BKN010 OVC040 TEMPO 2818/2901 1 1/2SM –SN BLSN BKN008 PROB30 2820/2822 1/2SM SN VV005 FM290130Z 28010KT 5SM –SN BKN020 BECMG 2906/2908 000000KT P6SM SKC RMK NXT FCST BY 281800Z According to the TAF, between 0600Z and 0800Z

visibility will temporarily be reduced to 1 1/2 SM in light snow.
visibility will be 3 SM in light snow.
the winds will gradually become calm.
explanation: AIM MET 7.0 Aerodrome Forecasts (TAFs) If a permanent change in a few weather elements is forecast to occur gradually, with conditions evolving over a period of time (normally one to two hours, but not more than four hours), the new conditions that differ from those immediately prior are indicated after “BECMG”. Normally only those elements for which a change is forecast to occur will follow “BECMG”. Any forecast weather element not indicated as part of the “BECMG” group remains the same as in the period before the onset of the change. In this example, winds are forecast to be calm between 0600Z and 0800Z. question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN31 Which of the following hazardous weather conditions may be expected during a flight from CYVR to CYEG departing at 1230Z?
answers: A broken cloud layer at 1,800 feet.
Light drizzle, fog and ceilings of 100 feet AGL.
explanation: GFA Analysis A flight departing CYVR at 1230Z destined for CYEG will be flying through an area of freezing rain and continuous precipitation. Broken tops at 18,000 feet exist in the first portion of the flight with tops rising to 24,000 feet. Most general aviation aircraft will not be able to climb above the weather.

question: Refer to the Appendix: Weather Symbol If seen on a GFA, this image indicates an area of
explanation: AIM MET 4.11 Clouds and Weather Chart Areas of showery or intermittent precipitation are shown as hatched areas enclosed by a dashed green line when colour is used. Areas of continuous precipitation are shown as stippled areas enclosed by a solid green line when colour is used.

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN31 Which statement best describes the weather associated with a flight from CYXY to CYEG?
answers: Shortly after departure an aircraft may experience patchy clouds and 3SM visibility in light snow.
The charted TROWAL is forecast to move west, away from the aircraft's intended flight path.
Freezing drizzle is forecast along the route of flight.
Winds at CYXY are generally weaker than those at CYEG.
explanation: AIM MET 4.0 Graphic Area Forecast (GFA) The flight path parallels a TROWAL (blue line with red barbs) and traverses an area of continuous precipitation. During departure, the aircraft may also experience weather associated with “B” depicted on the GFA. The textual description is found in the comments section.The comments box provides information that the weather forecaster considers important (e.g. formation or dissipation of fog, increasing or decreasing visibility). It is also used to describe elements that are difficult to render pictorially or, if added to the depiction, would cause the chart to become cluttered

question: TAF CYXE 281139Z 2812/2912 24010G25KT WS011/ 27050KT 3SM –SN BKN010 OVC040 TEMPO 2818/2901 1 1/2SM –SN BLSN BKN008 PROB30 2820/2822 1/2SM SN VV005 FM290130Z 28010KT 5SM –SN BKN020 BECMG 2906/2908 000000KT P6SM SKC RMK NXT FCST BY 281800Z According to the TAF
answers: a layer of windshear is forecast from the surface to 1,100 ft ASL.
a layer of windshear is forecast from the surface to 1,100 ft AGL.
the prevailing visibility throughout the forecast period is 3SM in moderate snow.
at 0130Z on the 29th there will be a temporary reduction in visibility to 1 1/2 SM.
explanation: AIM MET 7.0 Aerodrome Forecasts (TAFs) Wind shear is reported if there is strong evidence to expect significant, non-convective wind shear that could adversely affect aircraft operation within 1,500 ft AGL over the aerodrome. The height of the top of the shear layer (in hundreds of feet AGL) is given, followed by the forecast wind speed and direction at that height. While the main effect of turbulence is related to erratic changes in altitude and attitude of the aircraft, the main effect of wind shear is the rapid gain or, more critical, loss of airspeed. Therefore, for forecasting purposes, any cases of strong, non-convective low-level wind shear within 1,500 ft AGL will be labelled as “WS.” TAF CYXE 281139Z 2812/2912 24010G25KT WS011/ 27050KT 3SM –SN BKN010 OVC040 TEMPO 2818/2901 1 1/2SM –SN BLSN BKN008 PROB30 2820/2822 1/2SM SN VV005 FM290130Z 28010KT 5SM –SN BKN020 BECMG 2906/2908 000000KT P6SM SKC RMK NXT FCST BY 281800Z

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFA At 0600Z the freezing level at Mary’s Harbour (A) is closest to
explanation: AIM MET 4.12 Icing, Turbulence and Freezing Level Chart Freezing level contours are indicated on a GFA by dashed lines. The height of the freezing level is indicated to the nearest multiple of 2,500 ft using the standard heights in hundreds of feet above sea level (e.g. SFC, 25, 50, 75, 100, meaning surface, 2,500, 5,000, 7,500, 10,000). When more than one freezing level is forecast, only the lowest level needs to be indicated, unless meteorological conditions are expected to be relevant to aviation safety (e.g. freezing precipitation aloft).

question: A TAF with an indicated validity period from 1100Z to 2300Z that was issued at 1040Z is considered valid from
explanation: AIM MET 7.0 Aerodrome Forecasts A TAF is considered to be valid from the moment it is issued. For example, a TAF with an indicated period of validity from 1100Z to 2300Z that was issued at 1040Z is considered to be valid from 1040Z until it is amended, until the next scheduled TAF for the same aerodrome is issued or until the period of validity ends and no new TAF has been issued. The maximum period of validity for a TAF is 30 hours.

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN34 On a flight departing CYZV at 1800Z destined for CYYT, what kind of weather might be expected during the climb after departure?
answers: Mixed icing between 3,000 feet ASL and 18,000 feet ASL.
Mixed icing between 4,000 feet AGL and 10,000 feet AGL.
Low level wind shear and mechanical turbulence from the surface up to 4,000 feet AGL.
Mixed icing between 4,000 feet ASL and 10,000 feet ASL.
explanation: GFA Interpretation The first third of the flight path passes through an area of mixed moderate icing between 4,000 feet ASL and 10,000 feet ASL. Remember that all altitudes are ASL unless otherwise noted.

question: WSCN23 CWAO 162225 CZWG SIGMET A4 VALID 162225/170225 CWEG‐ CZWG WINNIPEG FIR SQLN TS OBS WTN 20NM OF LINE / N4929 W09449/25 SW CYQK – /N5104 W09348/CYRL – /N5209 W09120/60 NW CYPL TOP FL340 MOV E 15KT NC RMK GFACN33= Which of the following statements is true regarding the SIGMET provided?

explanation: AIM MET 6.0 Significant Meteorological Information (SIGMET) An observed line of thunderstorms is over northwestern Ontario late in the day. This is the fourth SIGMET message issued for this event since 0000Z on the day concerned. The thunderstorms have tops of FL340 and are moving eastward. question: A SIGMET is issued
answers: to advise pilots of potentially hazardous weather conditions up to and including 24,000 feet.
to indicate the main areas where instrument meteorological weather conditions are forecast to exist.
to advise pilots of the occurrence of weather phenomena which may affect the safety of aircraft operations.
if changes to a forecast warrant the issuance of an amendment to a GFA.
explanation: AIM MET 6.0 SIGMET Information message issued by a meteorological watch office (MWO) to advise pilots of the occurrence or expected occurrence of specified weather phenomena, which may affect the safety of aircraft operations, and the development of those phenomena in time and space.

question: Which of the following is correct regarding an AIRMET?
answers: AIRMETs automatically amend the relevant GFA.
AIRMETs will indicate areas of severe icing.
AIRMETs are reported in the remarks section of the affected GFA.
explanation: AIM MET 4.13 Graphic Area Forecast (GFA) Amendments Once issued, a SIGMET or AIRMET message automatically amends the current and relevant graphic area forecast (GFA). The remark (RMK) found in the national version of those messages indicates the GFA region(s) to which the SIGMET or AIRMET applies.

question: If the ATIS advises that SIGMET Charlie is in effect a pilot may contact . . . . . for more information regarding the SIGMET.
explanation: AIM RAC 1.1.2.1 Flight Information Centres (FICs) Upon request from an aircraft, an FIC provides meteorological information such as SIGMETs, AIRMETs, PIREPs, aerodrome routine meteorological reports (METARs), aviation selected special weather reports (SPECIs) , aerodrome forecasts (TAFs), altimeter settings, and lightning information.

question: A complete blockage of the pitot pressure source and its drain vent in flight will most likely result in
answers: an airspeed indication of zero knots.
a constant airspeed indication in level flight regardless of the power setting.
a decrease in indicated airspeed during a climb.
a constant indicated airspeed during a descent.
explanation: Blocked Pitot Tube If the aircraft were to descend while the pitot tube is obstructed, the pressure in the pitot system, including the diaphragm, would remain constant. But as the descent is made, the static pressure would increase against the diaphragm causing it to compress, thereby resulting in an indication of decreased airspeed. Conversely, if the aircraft were to climb, the static pressure would decrease allowing the diaphragm to expand, thereby showing an indication of greater airspeed. Therefore, if no change in altitude is made, the airspeed indicator would be unable to register any changes in airspeed. At altitudes above the blockage, the airspeed indicator will overread. Below the blockage, it will underread. A blocked pitot tube will only affect the airspeed indicator. Altimeter and vertical speed indications will remains normal

question: If a pitot tube becomes blocked but its drain hole remains unobstructed
answers: the airspeed indicator will overread during a climb and underread during a descent.
the airspeed indicator will underread during a climb and overread during a descent.
the airspeed indicator will indicate zero.
the altimeter will overread and the vertical speed indicator will indicate a momentary climb.
explanation: Blocked Pitot System The ASI operates by comparing total (ram air) pressure measured by the pitot tube with static pressure measured by the static ports. When the pitot tube is blocked, ram air can no longer enter the system. The air already in the pitot system escapes through the open drain hole, leaving the system filled with only static pressure. Inside the ASI there is a diaphragm. The interior of the diaphragm is normally exposed to total pressure and is surrounded by static pressure within the instrument casing. Normally, the difference between these pressures causes the diaphragm to move, which in turn drives the airspeed needle. However, in this scenario, the blockage prevents any ram air pressure from entering the pitot tube. With only static pressure on both sides of the diaphragm—inside the instrument casing and inside the diaphragm itself—there is no pressure differential to move the diaphragm. This lack of movement results in the ASI indicating zero.

question: If the alternate static source is selected
answers: the VSI will indicate a momentary descent.
the altimeter will indicate a slightly higher altitude than normal.
the VSI will indicate a constant descent.
the ASI will indicate an airspeed lower than the actual airspeed.
explanation: Alternate Static Source An alternate static source is provided in some aircraft to provide static pressure should the primary static source become blocked. The alternate static source is normally found inside the flight deck. Due to the venturi effect of the air flowing around the fuselage, the air pressure inside the flight deck is lower than the exterior pressure. When the alternate static source pressure is used, the following instrument indications are observed: The altimeter will overread and indicate an altitude that is higher than what would normally be indicated. The ASI indicates an airspeed greater than the actual airspeed. The VSI shows a momentary climb and then stabilizes if the altitude is held constant. Each pilot is responsible for consulting the Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM) or the Pilot’s Operating Handbook (POH) to determine the amount of error that is introduced into the system when utilizing the alternate static source.

question: Deceleration errors in the magnetic compass would be most pronounced on headings of
explanation: Acceleration Error A magnetic compass is composed of a magnet attached to a float which rests on a pivot assembly allowing it to rotate freely. If we were right over the equator, the earth’s magnetic field would be parallel to the surface and the compass would be level. But at 40°N, for example, the magnetic lines dip and the magnet will try to align itself with the earth’s magnetic field which is no longer parallel to the surface. This causes the magnet’s center of gravity to shift. In the northern hemisphere, as an aircraft accelerates on an easterly heading, the compass will resist that acceleration because of its inertia. The resisting force acts through the magnet’s centre of gravity. This causes the magnet to rotate and indicate an apparent turn to the north. After the speed stabilizes the compass will indicate correctly once again. When decelerating, the forces will cause the compass to show an apparent turn to the south. These errors only occur on easterly and westerly headings. Accelerating or decelerating on northerly or southerly headings does not cause any error since the center of gravity and the pivot point are aligned with the aircraft’s heading. On northerly or southerly headings the magnet’s CG is in line with the forces of acceleration, so no moment is created and no apparent turn is indicated. The moment created around the magnet during an acceleration or deceleration on an easterly or westerly heading causes it to rotate and incorrectly indicate a turn. Memory Aid This can be a rather complicated topic so use the memory aid ANDS to recall the acceleration errors associated with a magnetic compass.

question: In the event of a static blockage
answers: the altimeter will freeze and the vertical speed indicator will indicate zero.
the altimeter and vertical speed indicator will overread.
the altimeter and the vertical speed indicator will freeze.
the airspeed indicator will overread during a climb and underread during a descent.
explanation: Blocked Static System If the static system becomes blocked but the pitot tube remains clear, the ASI continues to operate; however, it is inaccurate. The airspeed indicates lower than the actual airspeed when the aircraft is operated above the altitude where the static ports became blocked because the trapped static pressure is higher than normal for that altitude. When operating at a lower altitude, a faster than actual airspeed is displayed due to the relatively low static pressure trapped in the system. A blockage of the static system also affects the altimeter and VSI. Trapped static pressure causes the altimeter to freeze at the altitude where the blockage occurred. In the case of the VSI, a blocked static system produces a continuous zero indication.

question: During a climb the airspeed indicator indicates an increase in airspeed and an increase in pitch angle results in a further increase in airspeed. What is the most probable explanation for this?
answers: A blockage of both the pitot pressure and static pressure sources.
Failure of both attitude indicating gyros.
explanation: Blockage of the Pitot-Static System If both the pitot tube opening and the drain hole become clogged simultaneously, the pressure in the pitot tube is trapped and no change will be indicated on the airspeed indicator. However, if the static port is unblocked while the pitot tube is blocked and the aircraft changes altitude, a change will be observed on the airspeed indicator. The change is not related to a change in airspeed but a change in static pressure.

question: In the northern hemisphere, a magnetic compass is most affected by southerly turning errors when turning from
explanation: Turning Errors An error in an aircraft magnetic compass. In the Northern Hemisphere, the compass lags behind the turn when turning left or right through a northerly heading and races ahead when turning through a southerly heading. The error results because the float on which the compass magnets are mounted is unbalanced to compensate for the vertical component of the earth’s magnetic field. It is most pronounced during turns made from initial north-south courses.

question: The airspeed indicator will indicate zero when
answers: the pitot tube is blocked and the drain hole is open.
the pitot tube and drain hole are blocked.
the alternate static source is used.
explanation: Blocked Pitot Tube If the pitot tube becomes blocked and its associated drain hole remains clear, ram air is no longer able to enter the pitot system. Air already in the system vents through the drain hole, and the remaining pressure drops to ambient (outside) air pressure. Under these circumstances, the ASI reading decreases to zero because the ASI senses no difference between the air pressure entering the diaphragm and the static pressure surrounding it within the instrument casing. The apparent loss of airspeed is not usually instantaneous but happens very quickly.

question: A magnetic compass will indicate a turn towards the north if an aircraft
answers: decelerates while on an easterly heading.
enters a right turn from an easterly heading.
accelerates while on an easterly heading.
enters a left turn from a westerly heading.
explanation: Acceleration Error The magnetic dip and the forces of inertia cause magnetic compass errors when accelerating and decelerating on easterly and westerly headings. Because of the pendulous type mounting, the aft end of the compass card is tilted upward when accelerating and downward when decelerating during changes in airspeed. When accelerating on either an easterly or westerly heading, the error appears as a turn indication toward the north. When decelerating on either of these headings, the compass indicates a turn toward the south. A mnemonic for the effect of acceleration error is the word “ANDS” (Acceleration- North/Deceleration-South).

question: During a straight climb at a constant airspeed, a magnetic compass
explanation: Magnetic Compass During a constant speed climb or descent, the magnetic compass will indicate properly. Errors in the compass are associated with changes to the aircraft’s velocity vector. This may be due to a turn or a change in airspeed.

question: Accuracy of the DME system is within
answers: ±0.5 NM or 3% of the distance, whichever is smaller.
±0.5 NM or 2% of the distance, whichever is greater.
±0.5 NM or 3% of the distance, whichever is greater.
±0.3 NM or 5% of the distance, whichever is greater.
explanation: AIM COM 4.7 Distance Measuring Equipment Distance Measuring Equipment (DME) operates through two-way signal transmissions between the aircraft and the DME ground station. The aircraft sends out paired pulses at a specific spacing, which are received by the ground station. The ground station then responds by transmitting paired pulses back to the aircraft on a different frequency. The airborne DME unit measures the time required for this signal exchange and translates it into the distance from the aircraft to the ground station. It’s important to note that the distance provided by DME is slant range distance, not actual horizontal distance. The DME system’s accuracy is within ±0.5 NM or three percent of the distance, whichever is greater.

question: A radar altimeter can best be defined as a
answers: self contained unit capable of determining an aircraft's altitude above mean sea level.
self contained unit capable of determining the true height of an aircraft above ground.
satellite based navigational aid capable of providing the true height of an aircraft above ground.
ground based navigational aid capable of determining an aircraft's true height above ground.
explanation: Radar Altimeter A radar altimeter uses a transceiver and a directional antenna to broadcast a carrier wave at 4.3 GHz from the aircraft straight towards the ground. This wave is frequency-modulated at 50 MHz and travels at a known speed. When the wave hits surface features, it reflects back to the aircraft, where a second antenna receives the returning signal. By calculating the travel time of these radio signals and knowing their speed, a radar altimeter can accurately measure the aircraft’s true altitude above the ground.

question: The onboard DME receiver will exhibit the greatest error when an aircraft is at a
answers: high altitude and far from a station.
high altitude and near a station.
low altitude and far from a station.
low altitude and near a station.
explanation: AIM COM 4.7 Distance Measuring Equipment DME is used for measuring the slant range between the aircraft and the ground station. It operates in the UHF band and is subject to line-of-sight limitations. The DME receiver onboard an aircraft sends a pulse signal to the ground based DME station, which then responds to the interrogating aircraft. The receiver in the aircraft measures the time delay between the sent and received pulses and calculates the slant range distance. There is no azimuth information, only distance. Some DMEs can also provide groundspeed and time-to-station readouts. DME is least accurate when an aircraft is at high altitude directly overhead the station.

question:
An onboard weather radar system can detect
A) rain.
B) snow.
C) volcanic ash.
explanation: Weather Radar The airborne weather radar system is an essential tool for pilots to assess the intensity of convective weather ahead of the aircraft. In this respect, it enables the strategic and tactical planning of a safe flight trajectory. Weather radar technology has evolved significantly and a range of enhanced products is now available. If properly used, they permit pilots’ workload to be significantly reduced while substantially reducing encounters with adverse weather. Principle of Operation Knowledge of the radar principle is paramount in order to accurately tune this system and interpret the weather radar display correctly. Weather detection is based on the reflectivity of water droplets. The weather echo appears on the respective display with a colour scale that goes from red (high reflectivity) to green (low reflectivity). The weather radar echo returns vary in intensity as a function of the droplet size, composition and quantity. For example, a water particle is five times more reflective than an ice particle of the same size.

question: If an aircraft is directly overhead a DME station at 12,000 feet AGL, the onboard DME receiver will indicate approximately
explanation: Distance Measuring Equipment (DME) Distance measuring equipment (DME) is a system that relies on both aircraft-installed and ground-based components, with the ground station typically co-located with a VHF omnidirectional range (VOR) or, in some cases, an instrument landing system (ILS). DME provides the pilot with the slant-range distance to the DME transmitter, which differs from the horizontal distance displayed by a GPS receiver. Slant-range distance accounts for both the horizontal distance and the aircraft’s altitude above the station, making it slightly greater than the GPS-calculated distance. For example, if an aircraft is flying 12,000 feet directly above a DME transmitter, the DME receiver will display a distance of two nautical miles, whereas a GPS receiver will show a distance of zero miles.

question: Select the incorrect statement regarding radar altimeters.
answers: Radar altimeters are an essential component to Ground Proximity Warning Systems.
Radar altimeters cannot detect terrain directly ahead of the aircraft.
An accurate radar altimeter indication is dependent on having a correct altimeter setting.
Radar altimeters indicate altitude above ground level.
explanation: Radar Altimeter A radar altimeter measures the altitude of an aircraft or other object above the ground by emitting radio waves and analyzing the time it takes for these waves to bounce back after hitting the surface below. It operates by sending short bursts of radio frequency energy directly downwards and then calculating the distance based on the time delay between the transmission and reception of the reflected signal. The altitude is determined by the formula that relates the speed of the radio waves (which is the speed of light) and the measured time interval. By calculating the travel time of radio signals, a radar altimeter accurately determines the aircraft’s height above the ground.

question: Select the incorrect statement regarding the use of weather radar.
answers: The shape of an echo has little influence on its intensity.
A convective cloud may be dangerous, even if the radar echo is weak.
Weather behind strong cells may not be detected.
The weather radar echo returns vary in intensity as a function of the droplet size, composition and quantity.
explanation: Weather Radar Limitations One of the weather radar limitations is that it indicates only the presence of liquid water. The consequence is that a thunderstorm does not have the same reflectivity over its altitude range because the quantity of liquid water in the atmosphere decreases with the altitude. Yet, the convective cloud and associated threats may extend significantly above the upper detection limit of the weather radar (called “radar top”). This means that reflectivity is not directly proportional to the level of risk that may be encountered: a convective cloud may be dangerous, even if the radar echo is weak. This is particularly true for equatorial overland regions where converging winds produce large scale uplifts of dry air. The resulting weather cells have much less reflectivity than mid-latitude convective cells. However, turbulence in or above such clouds may have a higher intensity than indicated by the image on the weather radar display. On the other hand, air close to the sea can be very humid. In this case, thermal convection will produce clouds that are full of water: these clouds will have a high reflectivity, but may not necessarily be a high threat. Consequently, limitations of weather radars must be well understood and complemented by basic meteorological knowledge of the crew and, where possible, visual observation. Attenuation Another limitation of the weather radar is called attenuation. The weather radar display depends on signal returns: the more intense the precipitation, the less distance the radar can see through. Therefore when the radar echo is unable to make the two way trip through heavy precipitation, an ‘attenuation’ effect occurs. The result is twofold. First, the size, shape and intensity of that weather may not be accurately displayed to the pilot. What appears to be a thin band of precipitation could in fact be the leading edge of a much larger area of precipitation. Secondly, any weather behind such strong cells will not be detected. This can result in unexpected weather unfolding only after the cell has been circumnavigated. Weather Radar Display Showing the Effects of Attenuation Interpreting the Weather Radar Shapes, more than colours, should be observed carefully in order to detect adverse weather conditions. Closely spaced areas of different colours usually indicate highly turbulent zones. Weather radar display showing closest spaced are of different colours Some shapes are good indicators of severe hail and signify strong vertical drafts. Fast changing shapes, whatever form they take, also indicate high weather activity. Shapes indicating adverse weather

question: Secondary surveillance radar
answers: can determine the position of an aircraft without an operating transponder.
cannot determine the position of an aircraft without an operating transponder.
is a passive surveillance system that can detect precipitation.
can calculate an aircraft's position by determining the range and azimuth of reflected energy.
explanation: AIM COM 7.2 Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) SSR determines aircraft range by measuring the time interval between transmitting a signal to, and receiving a reply from an airborne transponder. Unlike primary surveillance radar (PSR) which determines the range and azimuth of reflected energy, SSR cannot provide a position for an aircraft without an operating transponder.

question: From a TACAN, civil aircraft with a DME receiver can receive
answers: range information but not bearing information.
bearing information but not range information.
explanation: Tactical Air Navigation System (TACAN) A tactical air navigation system, commonly referred to by the acronym TACAN, is a navigation system used by military aircraft. It provides the user with bearing and distance (slant-range) to a ground station. It is a more accurate version of the VOR/DME system that provides bearing and range information for civil aviation. The DME portion of the TACAN system is available for civil use; at VORTAC facilities where a VOR is combined with a TACAN, civil aircraft can receive VOR/DME readings.

question: Regarding onboard weather radar, which type of precipitation is the most reflective?
explanation: Weather Radar The airborne weather radar system is an essential tool for pilots to assess the intensity of convective weather ahead of the aircraft. In this respect, it enables the strategic and tactical planning of a safe flight trajectory. Weather radar technology has evolved significantly and a range of enhanced products is now available. If properly used, they permit pilots’ workload to be significantly reduced while substantially reducing encounters with adverse weather. Principle of Operation Knowledge of the radar principle is paramount in order to accurately tune this system and interpret the weather radar display correctly. Weather detection is based on the reflectivity of water droplets . The weather echo appears on the respective display with a colour scale that goes from red (high reflectivity) to green (low reflectivity). The weather radar echo returns vary in intensity as a function of the droplet size, composition and quantity. For example, a water particle is five times more reflective than an ice particle of the same size.

question: Primary surveillance radar
answers: assists ATC in positive aircraft identification via data transmitted from an aircraft.
determines aircraft range by measuring the interval between transmitting a signal to and receiving a reply from an airborne transponder.
determines aircraft range by interrogating a GNSS constellation of 28 satellites.
is a passive surveillance system and does not rely on information transmitted from an aircraft.
explanation: AIM COM 7.1 Primary Surveillance Radar (PSR) Primary surveillance radar (PSR) computes target positions by determining the range and azimuth of transmitted and reflected radio frequency energy. It is a passive surveillance system and therefore does not rely on information transmitted from the aircraft. Secondary surveillance radar (SSR) determines aircraft range by measuring the interval between transmitting an interrogation to and receiving a reply from an airborne transponder. SSR is a cooperative surveillance system and does not provide a position for an aircraft without an operating transponder. SSR offers significant operational advantages to air traffic control (ATC), such as increased range, positive identification and aircraft altitude, when the aircraft has an altitude-encoding transponder. The principle of primary surveillance radar is simple – shout, then listen

question: Attenuation of weather radar signals may
answers: prevent the detection of echoes along a given flight path.
exaggerate the intensity of echoes along a given flight path.
cause interference with radio communication and navigational equipment.
explanation: Radar Attenuation Radar attenuation is simply the absorption or reflection of radar signals as the radar pulse penetrates an area of precipitation, preventing that radar from detecting any additional cells that lie behind the first storm. Consequently, what appears to be a thin or inexistent band of precipitation could in fact be the leading edge of a much larger area of precipitation. Furthermore, any weather behind such strong shadowing cells will not be detected. This can result in unexpected weather unfolding only after the cell has been circumnavigated.

question: Regarding onboard weather radar, too low of a tilt setting results in the inability to distinguish
explanation: Weather Radar An onboard weather radar is a Primary Surveillance Radar (PSR) that computes areas of precipitation by determining the range and azimuth of transmitted and reflected radio frequency energy. The primary use of weather radar is to aid the pilot in avoiding thunderstorms and associated turbulence. It is used for avoiding, not penetrating severe weather. Weather radar detects only precipitation, it does not detect minute cloud droplets. Therefore a clear area on a radar screen does not indicate that an area is also free of cloud. The best radar reflectors are raindrops and wet hail, the larger the raindrop the better it reflects. Extreme weather can also be identified by certain patterns displayed on a radar screen such as hooks, fingers or scalloped edges Tilt Management Tilt management is the single most important factor for the effective use of an airborne weather radar system. Too low of a tilt setting results in excessive ground returns and the inability to distinguish weather from ground clutter. With the tilt set too high, the beam will scan over the top of the weather. Either way, the pilot will not distinguish crucial weather data.

question: Which of the following techniques is most effective when evaluating the intensity of a storm cell with an onboard weather radar system?
answers: Scan the upper regions of the cell.
Scan the lower and middle regions of a thunderstorm cell.
Increase the tilt to minimize the effect of radar attenuation.
Reduce the gain to minimize the effect of radar attenuation.
explanation: Tilt Management To analyze a convective cell, the flight crew should use the tilt knob to obtain a correct display and point the weather radar beam to the most reflective part of the cell. At high altitudes, a thunderstorm may contain ice particles that have low reflectivity. If the tilt setting is not adapted, the display may indicate only the upper (less reflective) part of the convective cloud (overscanning). As a result, the flight crew may underestimate or not detect a thunderstorm. In order to get accurate weather detection, the weather radar antenna should also be pointed toward lower levels (i.e. below freezing level), where water can still be found. If a red area is found at a lower level, the antenna tilt should then be used to scan the area vertically. The presence of yellow or green areas at high altitudes, above a red cell, may indicate a very turbulent area. In most cases in flight, the adequate antenna tilt setting shows some ground returns at the top edge of the display, which may be difficult to differentiate from genuine weather echoes. A change in antenna tilt rapidly changes the shape and colour of ground returns and eventually causes them to disappear. This is not the case for weather echoes. Some weather radars are fitted with a Ground Clutter Suppression (GCS) function. When turned ON, it suppresses the ground return from the display. Pilots use the radar tilt function to adjust the radar beam angle, scanning different altitudes for weather hazards ahead and ensuring accurate detection of potential storms or turbulence.

question: Areas of severe thunderstorms may be difficult to detect using weather radar because
answers: of the absorption of radar signals as the radar pulse penetrates an area of precipitation.
weather radar systems cannot determine the intensity of echo returns.
it indicates only the presence of liquid water.
ground clutter makes it difficult to differentiate terrain from precipitation.
explanation: Weather Radar Attenuation A limitation of the weather radar is called ‘shadowing’ or ‘attenuation’. The weather radar display depends on signal returns: the more intense the precipitation, the less distance the radar can see through. Therefore when the radar echo is unable to make the two-way trip through heavy precipitation, a ‘shadowing’ effect occurs. The result is twofold. First, the size, shape and intensity of that weather may not be accurately displayed to the pilot. What appears to be a thin band of precipitation could in fact be the leading edge of a much larger area of precipitation. Secondly, any weather behind such strong shadowing cells will not be detected.

question: RVR “A” is located adjacent to the
explanation: RVR Locations

question: RVR is not reported unless it is . . . . . or less.
explanation: AIM MET 8.0 – The Aerodrome Routine Meteorological Report (METAR) Code The runway visual range (RVR) for the touchdown zone of up to four available landing runways is reported as a 10-min average, based on the maximum runway light settings at the time of the report. It is included if the prevailing visibility is 1 SM or less, and/or the RVR is 6,000 ft or less.

question: What difference, if any, is there between DME distance and GPS distance to a VOR/DME station?
answers: DME distance will be larger than GPS distance when directly over the VOR/DME.
GPS distance will be less accurate than DME distance due to atmospheric attenuation.
DME distance will be more accurate than GPS distance as an aircraft approaches the VOR/DME station.
There will be no significant difference.
explanation: Distance Measuring Equipment (DME) Distance measuring equipment (DME) is a system requiring both aircraft-installed and ground-based equipment, with the latter normally co-located with a VHF omnidirectional radio range (VOR) or, sometimes, an instrument landing system (ILS). DME provides the pilot with the slant-range distance to the DME transmitter. It is unlike a GPS receiver which displays the actual horizontal distance between an aircraft and any other location. Slant-range distance will always be slightly greater than GPS distance because the slant range also includes the aircraft’s height above the station. For example, the DME receiver in an aircraft 12,000 feet directly above a DME transmitter will read two nautical miles, whereas a GPS receiver will indicate zero miles.

question: . . . . . is caused by the reflection of radio waves by the ionosphere and is most pronounced just before and after sunrise or sunset.
explanation: Coastal Refraction Caused by the reflection of radio waves by the ionosphere. It causes the ADF pointer to fluctuate and is most pronounced just before and after sunrise/sunset. The effect is minimized by flying at a higher altitude or selecting an NDB operating at a lower frequency.

question: The DME system operates in the
answers: VHF band and is limited to line-of-sight reception.
UHF band and is limited to line-of-sight reception.
UHF band and is not limited to line-of-sight reception.
MF band and is limited to line-of-sight reception.
explanation: AIM COM 4.7 Distance Measuring Equipment (DME) Distance measuring equipment (DME) functions by the two-way transmission of signals in the UHF band between the aircraft and the DME site. Paired pulses at a specific spacing are sent out from the aircraft and are received by the ground station. The ground station then transmits paired pulses back to the aircraft on a different frequency. The time required for this signal exchange is measured in the airborne DME unit and is translated into the distance from the aircraft to the ground station. Distance information received from DME is slant range distance and not actual horizontal distance. The accuracy of the DME system is within ±0.5 NM or three percent of the distance, whichever is greater. DME is collocated with most Canadian VHF omnidirectional range (VOR) installations (VOR/DME) and with many instrument landing systems (ILS) and localizers (LOCs). In some cases, DME is also collocated with non-directional beacons (NDBs) to provide improved navigation capability.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart An aircraft is en-route to Kuujjuaq (A) along AR39 (B) and 17 nautical miles southwest of Kangiqsualujjuaq (C). The ADF should be tuned to which frequency?
explanation: Changeover Point (COP) The COP is a point along the route or airway segment between two adjacent navigation facilities or waypoints where a changeover in navigation guidance should occur. At this point, the pilot should change the navigation receiver frequency from the station behind the aircraft to the station ahead. Note that it is not typically depicted if the COP is the halfway point. Refer to the changeover point highlighted in the chart below. If the aircraft is southwest-bound along AR39 the pilot must tune and identify the YLU NDB (295 kHz) until it is 15 miles out and then tune and identify the VP NDB (390 kHz).

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart What is the total distance from the Prince Rupert NDB (A) to the Terrace NDB (B) via R35 and B25?
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation The total distance from PR to ZKI along R35 is 63 NM and the total distance from ZKI to XT along B25 is 19 NM. Therefore the total distance from PR to XT along R35 and B25 is the sum of both legs (63 + 19 = 82 NM).

question: When navigating using an NDB, coastal effect can be minimized by selecting stations
answers: directly in front of or behind the aircraft.
at which the navigational signal crosses the shoreline at an angle of 30° or more.
explanation: Coastal Refraction Refraction is negligible perpendicular (90°) to the coast, but increases as the angle of incidence decreases, leading to inaccurate ADF indications. The effect can be minimized by flying at higher altitudes or by using NDBs situated closer to the coast. Refraction bends the navigation signal resulting in an inaccurate indication of an aircraft’s position

question: An aircraft is maintaining a heading of 177° along a bearing of 170° from an NDB. ATC requests that the aircraft intercept an inbound track of 360°. In order to intercept the desired course using a 30° intercept angle, the aircraft should turn to a heading of
explanation: Course Interception When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the scenario as presented. Most exam questions can be solved following the steps outlined below: If given a heading, ignore it, the current heading is irrelevant. Determine the aircraft’s bearing relative to the NDB and draw a line representing that bearing as shown below (170°) Draw a line indicating the bearing which the aircraft must intercept. It may help to think of this like a VOR. In essence, the aircraft must track 360° inbound along the 180° radial. Although technically it is not a radial, students have more success when they can find commonalities between NDBs and VORs. In theory, there are two headings that may be flown to interpret the 360° bearing to the NDB at the given intercept angle. These can be determined by adding the intercept angle to the desired bearing or subtracting the intercept angle from the desired bearing. That is, either a heading of 330° or 030° may be appropriate. However, since the aircraft is east of the desired bearing, only a heading of 330° will result in the aircraft intercepting the correct bearing. If the aircraft was west of the desired bearing, then a heading of 030° would be appropriate.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart What is the frequency for the Rankin Inlet NDB?
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation The frequency can be read directly from the chart as shown below.

question: An aeroplane’s current heading is 275° and it is maintaining a bearing of 270° from an NDB. In order to intercept the 030° bearing to the NDB at 90°, the aeroplane must turn to a heading of
explanation: Course Interception When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the scenario as presented. Most exam questions can be solved following the steps outlined below: If given a heading, ignore it, the current heading is irrelevant. Determine the aircraft’s current location relative to the NDB (270°). Draw a line indicating the bearing that the aircraft must intercept (210° inbound on a track of 030°). In theory, there are two headings that may be flown to interpret the 210° bearing inbound at the given intercept angle. These can be determined by adding the intercept angle to the desired radial or subtracting the intercept angle from the desired radial. That is, either a heading of 120° or 300° may be appropriate. However, since the aircraft is northwest of the desired bearing, only a heading of 120° will result in the aircraft intercepting the desired radial. If the aircraft was southeast of the desired radial then a heading of 300° would be appropriate.

question: If flying near a thunderstorm, the ADF pointer will
explanation: ADF Limitations If flying near a thunderstorm, the ADF pointer will point to sources of lighting rather than the selected station.

question: Refer to the Appendix: Automatic Direction Finder (ADF) If the magnetic bearing to the station is 058°, the magnetic heading is closest to
explanation: Magnetic Bearing, Relative Bearing and Magnetic Heading The magnetic heading can be calculated by subtracting the relative bearing from the magnetic bearing. The magnetic bearing is given and the relative bearing can be determined from the ADF indication provided (250°). Therefore the magnetic heading is 058° – 250° = -192°. A negative value may seem strange, but by adding 360°, a more intuitive value is obtained (168°). It is important to note that both answers are technically correct, however, headings are conventionally measured in a clockwise direction, not a counter-clockwise direction.

question: An aeroplane’s current heading is 345° and it is maintaining a bearing of 350° from an NDB. In order to intercept the 150° bearing to the NDB at 90°, the aeroplane must turn to a heading of
explanation: Course Interception When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the scenario as presented. Most exam questions can be solved following the steps outlined below: If given a heading, ignore it, the current heading is irrelevant. Determine the aircraft’s bearing relative to the NDB and draw a line representing that bearing as shown below (350°) Draw a line indicating the bearing which the aircraft must intercept. It may help to think of this like a VOR. In essence, the aircraft must track 150° inbound along the 330° radial. Although technically it is not a radial, often times students have more success when they can find commonalities between NDBs and VORs. In theory, there are two headings that may be flown to interpret the 150° bearing to the NDB at the given intercept angle. These can be determined by adding the intercept angle to the desired bearing or subtracting the intercept angle from the desired bearing. That is, either a heading of 240° or 060° may be appropriate. However, since the aircraft is northeast of the desired bearing, only a heading of 240° will result in the aircraft intercepting the correct bearing. If the aircraft was south of the desired bearing, then a heading of 240° would be appropriate.

question: Refer to the Appendix: Instrument Indications What is the magnetic bearing TO the station?
explanation: Magnetic Bearing Magnetic bearing is the direction to or from a radio transmitting station measured relative to magnetic north. The magnetic bearing to a station can be calculated by finding the sum of the magnetic heading and relative bearing.

question: Refer to the Appendix: VOR Indicator The VOR receiver indicates that an aircraft is on the . . . . . radial.
explanation: VOR Interpretation The receiver is tuned to the 170° radial and the indicator shows the aircraft is west of the desired radial. The deviation is 3 dots (~6°) therefore the current radial is 170° + 6° = 176°.

question: Refer to the Appendix: VOR Indication What heading will intercept radial 210° inbound at 30°?
explanation: Course Intercept When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the question as presented to you. Most questions can be solved following the steps outlined below: Determine the current location relative to the selected radial . The CDI indicates that easterly headings are required to intercept the selected radial/track. Therefore we can determine the aircraft’s current position is west of the selected radial/track. Note that when actually tracking inbound to the station in this situation, the OBS would be set to 030°, not 210°. Draw the radial which you intend to intercept. Determine intercept heading . To intercept the 210° radial inbound we have one of two options: fly a heading of 060° or 360°. Since the aircraft is west of the desired radial, only a heading of 060° will result in an actual intercept.

question: When over a landmark located along a victor airway, if the indicated radial varies by more than . . . . . from the published radial, the VOR receiver should not be used for IFR navigation.
explanation: AIM 4.5.1 – 4.5.2 VOR Accuracy and Checks Aircraft VOR equipment may be checked while airborne by flying over a landmark located on a published radial and noting the indicated radial. Equipment that varies more than ±6° from the published radial should not be used for IFR navigation. The aircraft’s VOR receiver over Albertville on V41 has successfully passed an airborne check, indicating within ±6° of the published 012° radial.

question: Refer to the Appendix: VOR Indications Select the correct statement assuming that both receivers are independent of each other and are tuned to the same ground station.
answers: Since the difference between the two receivers is greater than 5° they are considered inoperative.
Since the difference between the two receivers is only 4° they are considered operational.
Since the difference between the two receivers is less than 6° they are considered operational.
Since the difference between the two receivers is only 2° they are considered operational.
explanation: AIM 4.5.1 – 4.5.2 VOR Accuracy and Checks A pilot can conduct a dual VOR check only if the aircraft is equipped with two VORs that are independent of each other. Each receiver must be tuned to the same station and the bearing to the station noted. A difference greater than 4° between the aircraft’s two VOR receivers indicates that one of the aircraft’s receivers may be beyond acceptable tolerance.

question: Refer to the Appendix: Primary Flight Display The aircraft is on radial . . . . . from the YOW VOR.
explanation: Primary Flight Display The aircraft’s position relative to multiple navigation aids can be determined using the primary flight display (PFD). The CDI below is receiving data from the onboard GPS sensor and bearing one is displaying information relative to the VOR. The aircraft’s position can be quickly by reading the value indicated by the tail of the pointer.

question: Refer to the Appendix: VOR Indicator What heading will intercept radial 310° inbound at 30°?
explanation: Course Interception When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the scenario as presented. Most exam questions can be solved following the steps outlined below: If given a heading, ignore it, the current heading is irrelevant Draw a line indicating the radial which the aircraft must intercept (310°). Determine the aircraft’s position relative to the selected radial using the VOR indication provided. In this particular case, the aircraft is south of the desired radial. In theory, there are two headings that may be flown to interpret the 310° radial inbound at the given intercept angle. These can be determined by adding the intercept angle to the desired radial or subtracting the intercept angle from the desired course. That is, either a heading of 100° or 160° may be appropriate. However, since the aircraft is south of the desired radial, only a heading of 100° will result in the aircraft intercepting the desired radial. If the aircraft was north of the desired radial then a heading of 285° would be appropriate.

question: The current heading is 140° and the CDI is centred with a ‘TO’ indication when 120° is selected on the OBS. What heading will intercept the 240° radial outbound from the VOR station at an angle of 45°?
explanation: Course Interception When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the scenario as presented. Most exam questions can be solved following the steps outlined below: If given a heading, ignore it, the current heading is irrelevant. Since the CDI is centred with a ‘TO’ indication and 120° selected in the OBS, it can be concluded that the aircraft is northwest of the VOR along the 300° radial. Draw a line representing the 300° radial. Draw a line indicating the radial that the aircraft must intercept (240°). In theory, there are two headings that may be flown to intercept the 240° radial at the given intercept angle. These can be determined by adding the intercept angle to the desired radial or subtracting the intercept angle from the desired radial. That is, either a heading of 195° or 285° may be appropriate. However, since the aircraft is north of the desired radial, only a heading of 195° will result in the aircraft intercepting the correct radial. If the aircraft was south of the desired radial, then a heading of 285° would be appropriate.

question: VOR reception at an altitude of 1,500 feet AGL is approximately
explanation: AIM COM 4.5 VOR VOR reception is subject to line-of-sight restrictions and range varies with aircraft altitude. Subject to shadow effect, reception at an altitude of 1,500 ft above ground level (AGL) is about 50 NM. Aircraft operating above 30,000 ft normally receive VOR at a distance of 150 NM or more. explanation: Determining Position Using Two VORs Two lines define a single point in space. Therefore, if a person can define an aircraft’s bearing relative to two navigation aids, the aircraft’s position can be determined. By drawing two lines as shown in the image below the aircraft’s position is known.

question: Refer to the Appendix: VOR Indication What heading will intercept radial 210° inbound at 60°?
explanation: Course Interception When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the scenario as presented. Most exam questions can be solved following the steps outlined below: If given a heading, ignore it, the current heading is irrelevant. Determine the aircraft’s location relative to the VOR and draw a line representing the selected radial as shown in the image below. Since the OBS selection is 210° and the TO/FROM flag indicates FROM, it can be determined that the aircraft is west of the selected radial (210°). Draw a line indicating the radial that the aircraft must intercept (210°). In theory, there are two headings that may be flown to intercept the 210° radial inbound (course of 030°). However, since the aircraft is west of the desired radial, only a heading of 090° will result in the aircraft intercepting the correct radial. question: Refer to the Appendices: LO Chart VOR Indications An aircraft equipped with dual VOR receivers tunes VOR 1 to YDF (A) and VOR 2 to YQX (B). Where is the aircraft located?
