question: The CDI is centred with OBS set to 080° and the TO/FROM flag indicates ‘TO’. In order to intercept the 220° radial outbound from the station at an angle of 30°, turn to a heading of
explanation: VOR Intercept When solving intercept questions, it is best to draw the scenario as presented to you. Most questions of this type can be solved following the steps outlined below: Determine the current location relative to the VOR. If there is a ‘FROM’ indication and the CDI is centred the aircraft is on the indicated radial. If there is a ‘TO’ indication with the CDI centred, the aircraft is on the reciprocal of what is indicated. Draw the desired intercept radial. Determine intercept heading. To intercept the 220° radial outbound we have one of two options: fly a heading of 190° or 250°. Since the aircraft is northwest of the desired radial, only a heading of 190° will lead to an actual intercept.

question:
An aircraft equipped with a VOR/DME receiver can determine
A) the distance from a TACAN.
B) bearing from a TACAN.
C) bearing from a DME.
D) bearing from a VOR.
explanation: Tactical Air Navigation Systems (TACAN) and VOR/DME TACAN, short for Tactical Air Navigation, is a navigation system primarily used by military aircraft. However, it also provides some benefits to general aviation aircraft. TACAN provides both bearing and distance information to the user from a ground station. For military aircraft, it serves as a critical tool for navigation and targeting purposes. It offers precise and real-time data on the aircraft’s relative position to the ground station. In the context of general aviation, the distance measuring equipment (DME) portion of the TACAN system can be utilized by civil aircraft. At specific facilities called VORTAC (VOR/TACAN), where a VOR (VHF Omnidirectional Range) station is combined with a TACAN, general aviation aircraft can access DME information in addition to VOR navigation. By utilizing the DME component of a VORTAC facility, general aviation aircraft can benefit from accurate distance measurements, allowing pilots to determine their distance from the station or other waypoints. This information aids in flight planning, situational awareness, and precise navigation for general aviation operators. While TACAN primarily caters to military needs, the availability of DME functionality at VORTAC facilities extends some advantages of the system to general aviation aircraft in terms of enhanced navigation capabilities and improved flight management.

question: A difference greater than . . . . . between the aircraft’s two VOR receivers indicates that one of the aircraft’s receivers may be beyond acceptable tolerance.
explanation: AIM COM 4.5.1 VHF Omnidirectional Range (VOR) Receiver Checks Where RNAV routes have not been published, VOR remains the primary NAVAID for use in Canada. It is important that the accuracy of the aircraft equipment be checked in accordance with principles of good airmanship and aviation safety. While standard avionics maintenance practices are used for checking aircraft VOR receivers, dual VOR equipment may be checked by tuning both sets to the same VOR facility and noting the indicated bearings sent to that station. A difference greater than 4° between the aircraft’s two VOR receivers indicates that one of the aircraft’s receivers may be beyond acceptable tolerance. In such circumstances, the cause of the error should be investigated and, if necessary, corrected before the equipment is used for an IFR flight. Example VOR 1 and VOR 2 on board the same aircraft are both tuned to the North Bay VOR/DME (YYB). VOR 1 is centred with a 090° indication while VOR 2 is centred with a 097° indication. Since the difference between both indicators is greater than 4°, the VORs have failed the accuracy check and neither should be used since it cannot be determined which one is erroneous in-flight.

question: A minimum of . . . . . satellites is required for RAIM to function.
explanation: AIM COM 5.3.1 Aircraft Based Augmentation Systems (ABAS) RAIM, FD (Fault Detection) and FDE (Fault Detection and Exclusion) functions in current IFR-certified avionics are considered ABAS. RAIM can provide the integrity for the en route, terminal, and NPA phases of flight. FDE improves the continuity of operation in the event of a satellite failure and can support primary-means oceanic operations. RAIM uses extra satellites in view to compare solutions and detect problems. It usually takes four satellites to compute a navigation solution, and a minimum of five for RAIM to function. The availability of RAIM is a function of the number of visible satellites and their geometry. It is complicated by the movement of satellites relative to a coverage area and temporary satellite outages resulting from scheduled maintenance or failures. If the number of satellites in view and their geometry do not support the applicable alert limit (2 NM en route, 1 NM terminal and 0.3 NM NPA), RAIM is unable to guarantee the integrity of the position solution. (Note that this does not imply a satellite malfunction.) In this case, the RAIM function in the avionics will alert the pilot, but will continue providing a navigation solution. Except in cases of emergency, pilots must discontinue.

question: If an aircraft passes abeam a VOR station and the CDI deflects through full scale but no TO or FROM flag appears
answers: the VOR transmitter is undergoing maintenance.
the aircraft is crossing a radial that is 90° to that selected on the OBS.
explanation: VOR Interpretation When an aircraft is crossing a radial that is 90° to that selected on the OBS, the CDI will deflect through full scale.

question: The RAIM alert limit for the terminal phase of flight is
explanation: AIM COM 5.3.1 Aircraft-Based Augmentation System (ABAS) In modern IFR-certified avionics, Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM) and Fault Detection and Exclusion (FDE) are key components of Aircraft-Based Augmentation Systems (ABAS). RAIM ensures the integrity of navigation during the en route, terminal, and non-precision approach (NPA) phases of flight, while FDE enhances operational continuity in the event of a satellite failure. RAIM operates by utilizing additional satellites in view to compare position solutions and detect any discrepancies. Normally, four satellites are required to compute a basic navigation solution, but a minimum of five is needed for RAIM to perform integrity monitoring. The availability of RAIM is highly dependent on both the number of visible satellites and their geometry. This can be complicated by satellite movement relative to the coverage area, as well as temporary satellite outages due to scheduled maintenance or technical failures. If the number of satellites in view and their geometry do not meet the required alert limits (2 NM for en route, 1 NM for terminal, and 0.3 NM for NPA operations), RAIM is unable to guarantee the integrity of the position solution. It’s important to note that this limitation doesn’t necessarily indicate a satellite malfunction. In such cases, RAIM will issue an alert to the pilot, signalling a potential issue with position integrity, while still providing a navigational solution. However, unless an emergency situation arises, pilots are required to discontinue using GNSS for IFR navigation when a RAIM alert occurs.

question: Fault Detection and Exclusion (FDE) requires a minimum of . . . . . satellites with good geometry to function.
explanation: AIM COM 5.3.1 Aircraft Based Augmentation Systems (ABAS) Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM) uses extra satellites in view to compare solutions and detect problems. It usually takes four satellites to compute a navigation solution, and a minimum of five for RAIM to function. The availability of RAIM is a function of the number of visible satellites and their geometry. Some avionics go beyond basic RAIM by having a Fault Detection and Exclusion (FDE) feature that allows the avionics to detect which satellite is faulty, and then to exclude it from the navigation solution. FDE requires a minimum of six satellites with good geometry to function. It has the advantage of allowing continued navigation in the presence of a satellite malfunction.

question: . . . . . satellites are required in order to obtain a 3-dimensional position fix using GPS.
explanation: Global Positioning System (GPS) in Aviation The GPS receiver measures the time it takes for a signal to arrive from three transmitting satellites. Since radio waves travel at 186,000 miles per second, the distance to each satellite can be calculated. The intersection of these ranges provides a two dimensional position of the aircraft. It is expressed in latitude/longitude coordinates. By incorporating the distance to a fourth satellite, the altitude above the surface of the earth can be calculated as well. This results in a three dimensional fix. Additional satellite inputs refine the accuracy of the position.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CNF4 RNAV (GNSS) RWY 13 At 50 miles from northeast of CNF4, an aircraft is cleared direct to PIBKA for the RNAV (GNSS) RWY 13. What is the initial altitude that this clearance authorizes the pilot to descend to?
explanation: Safe Altitude Within a Radius of 100 NM The lowest altitude that may be used under instrument meteorological conditions (IMC) that will provide a minimum vertical clearance of 1,000 ft or, in a designated mountainous region, 1,500 or 2,000 ft, as appropriate, rounded up to the next 100-ft increment, under conditions of standard temperature and pressure, above all obstacles located in an area contained within a radius of 100 NM of the aerodrome geometric centre.

question: Fault detection and exclusion (FDE)
answers: allows for a three-dimensional position and resolves the time difference between receiver and satellite clocks.
allows an aircraft's onboard avionics to detect which satellite is faulty, and then to exclude it from the navigation solution.
allows an aircraft's onboard avionics to detect a faulty ground based navigation aid.
uses extra satellites in view to compare solutions and detect problems.
explanation: AIM COM 5.3.1 Aircraft-Based Augmentation System (ABAS) RAIM uses extra satellites in view to compare navigation solutions and detect problems. It usually takes four satellites to compute a navigation solution and a minimum of five for RAIM to function. The availability of RAIM is a function of the number of visible satellites and their geometry. If the number of satellites in view and their geometry does not support the applicable alert limit (2 NM en route, 1 NM terminal and 0.3 NM NPA), RAIM is unable to guarantee the integrity of the position solution, however, this does not imply a satellite malfunction. The RAIM function in the avionics will alert the pilot but will continue providing a navigation solution. Except during emergencies, pilots must discontinue using GNSS for IFR navigation when such an alert occurs. Some avionics go beyond basic RAIM functionality by incorporating a Fault Detection and Exclusion (FDE) feature that can detect which satellite is faulty and then exclude it from the navigation solution. FDE requires a minimum of six satellites with good geometry to function. It has the advantage of allowing continued navigation in the presence of a satellite malfunction.

question: Select the correct statement regarding RAIM.
answers: RAIM has no fault detection function.
RAIM availability for approach can be predicted by onboard receivers or with PC-based computer software.
RAIM uses extra satellites in view to compensate for poor satellite geometry and outages.
RAIM and FDE functions in current IFR-certified avionics are forms of space-based augmentation systems.
explanation: AIM COM 5.3.1 Aircraft-Based Augmentation System (ABAS) The level of RAIM or FDE availability for a certain airspace at a certain time is determined by an analysis of satellite geometry, rather than signal measurement. This is why it can be predicted by receivers or with PC-based computer software. The difference between the two methods is that the receivers use the current constellation in their calculations while the PC software can use a constellation definition that takes into account scheduled satellite outages.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYAM ILS RWY 12 Assume that the only useable approach at CYAM is the ILS RWY 12. Which of the following forecast weather conditions allow CYAM to be used as an IFR alternate? A. OVC 006 – 2 SM B. BKN 008 – 1 SM C. OVC 005 – 3 SM D. OVC 007 – 1 1/2 SM
explanation: CAP GEN: Operating Minima – Alternate Since there is only one precision approach available, the alternate weather requirements are 600 – 2 or 300 – 1 above the lowest useable HAT and visibility, whichever is greater. The lowest usable HAT and visibility are 200 feet and 1/2 mile respectively as shown in the excerpt of the approach chart below. Therefore the alternate weather requirement is 600 – 2 or 500 – 1 1/2, whichever is greater. Furthermore, 600 – 2 is considered a standard alternate minimum. Should the selected alternate weather requirements meet the standard minima, then the minima summarized in the table below are also authorized.

question: . . . . . are low-level controlled fixed RNAV routes depicted on En-Route Low Altitude charts and require GNSS RNAV systems for use.
explanation: AIM RAC 11.4.4 Fixed RNAV Routes T-routes are low-level controlled fixed RNAV routes depicted on En-Route Low Altitude charts and require GNSS RNAV systems for use. The airspace associated with T-routes extends upward from 2,200 feet AGL, 10 NM either side of the centreline, and does not splay. The MOCA provides obstacle protection for only 6 NM either side of the track centreline and does not splay.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart G1 (A) is
answers: a commonly used track or transition.
explanation: LF/MF Airways G1 is an LF/MF airway that connects two NDBs. NDBs are the basis of the LF/MF airway and air route system.

question: A pilot is diverting to the alternate aerodrome filed in the IFR flight plan, the aerodrome is equipped with two ILS approaches on parallel runways. Select the correct statement.
answers: The pilot shall apply an alternate weather minima of 600-2 or 300-1 above the lowest usable HAT and visibility, whichever is greater.
The pilot shall apply an alternate weather minima of 400-1 or 200-1/2 above the lowest usable HAT and visibility, whichever is greater.
The pilot shall apply an alternate weather minima of 800-2 or 300-1 above the lowest usable HAT and visibility, whichever is greater.
The alternate weather minima does not apply.
explanation: CAP GEN – Operating Minima The alternate aerodrome weather minima requirements published in the CAPGEN are for planning purposes only. An approach is authorized whenever: the lowest reported RVR for the runway is at or above minima (CAR 602.129), regardless of reported ground visibility the RVR is reported to be fluctuating above and below minimum RVR the ground visibility is reported to be at least 1/4 mile the RVR for the runway is unavailable or not reported ATS is informed that an aircraft is on a training flight and will conduct a planned missed approach.

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN34 Could an airport at ② with no TAF and a usable HAT/HAA of 435 feet be used as an IFR alternate?
answers: Yes, since there are no CB forecast in the vicinity of the aerodrome, forecast visibility is not less than 3 miles and no cloud lower than 1,000 feet above the lowest useable HAT/HAA is forecast.
Yes, since there are no CB forecast in the vicinity of the aerodrome, forecast visibility is not less than 1 mile and no cloud lower than 2,000 feet above the lowest useable HAT/HAA is forecast.
Yes, since there are no CB forecast in the vicinity of the aerodrome, forecast visibility is not less than 1/4 mile and no cloud lower than 1,000 feet above the lowest useable HAT/HAA is forecast.
Yes, since there are no CB forecast in the vicinity of the aerodrome, forecast visibility is not less than 1 mile and no cloud lower than 1,000 feet above the lowest useable HAT/HAA is forecast.
explanation: CAP GEN – Operating Minima (Alternate) Aerodromes served with a GRAPHIC AREA FORECAST (GFA) may qualify as an alternate provided: No clouds lower than 1,000 ft above the lowest useable HAT/HAA are forecast No cumulonimbus clouds are forecast Visibility is not less than 3 miles

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart On an IFR flight from CYWK (A) to CYZV (B) via the V316, what is the lowest altitude that can be filed if the aircraft is equipped with an onboard GNSS receiver?
explanation: AIM COM 5.4.1 Domestic En Route and Terminal Operations The magnetic track from CYWK (A) to CYZV (B) is 191° and the highest MOCA along that portion of V316 is 4,600 ft. When using GNSS to navigate along VHF/UHF or LF/MF airways, ground-based NAVAID reception is not an issue. This means that pilots using GNSS for navigation can file or request an altitude below the MEA, but at or above the MOCA, to avoid icing, optimize cruise altitude, or in an emergency. However, an ATS clearance to fly below the MEA could depend on issues such as radio communication reception and the base of controlled airspace. In the rare case of a RAIM alert while en route below the MEA, and out of range of the NAVAID, pilots should advise ATS and climb to continue the flight using alternate means of navigation.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Select the correct statement regarding the aeronautical chart provided
answers: The MEA between YQM and IPTAX on V300 is 2,200 feet.
YQM is an "on request" reporting point.
Pilots of eastbound aircraft on V300 towards YQM should tune and identify YQM when the aircraft is within 43 miles of YQM.
YQM is located at the geographic centre of Greater Moncton Romeo Leblanc International Airport.
explanation: Changeover Point (COP) The COP is a point along the route or airway segment between two adjacent navigation facilities or waypoints where a changeover in navigation guidance should occur. At this point, the pilot should change the navigation receiver frequency from the station behind the aircraft to the station ahead. Note that it is not typically depicted if the COP is the halfway point.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart The elevation of Norway House (A) is
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation The elevation is 734 feet and can be read directly from the chart provided as shown below.

question: Aerodrome forecasts that contain the term PROB may be used to determine the weather suitability of an aerodrome as an alternate provided that
answers: the forecast PROB condition is not below the published alternate minima requirements for that aerodrome.
the forecast PROB condition is not below the appropriate landing minima for that aerodrome.
the forecast PROB condition is considered to be applicable at of the start of the PROB time period and these conditions are not to be below the published alternate minima requirements.
the forecast PROB condition is considered to be applicable at the end of the PROB time period and these conditions are not to be below the published alternate minima requirements.
explanation: CAP GEN: Operating Minima – Alternate Aerodrome forecasts (TAF) that contain the terms BECMG, TEMPO or PROB may be used to determine the weather suitability of an aerodrome as an alternate, provided that: where conditions are forecast to improve, the forecast BECMG condition shall be considered to be applicable as of the end of the BECMG time period, and these conditions shall not be below the published alternate minima requirements for that aerodrome. where conditions are forecast to deteriorate, the forecast BECMG condition shall be considered to be applicable as of the start of the BECMG time period, and these conditions shall not be below the published alternate minima requirements for that aerodrome. the forecast TEMPO condition shall not be below the published alternate minima requirements for that aerodrome. the forecast PROB condition shall not be below the appropriate landing minima for that aerodrome.

question: Refer to the Appendix: GFACN31 Assuming that the lowest useable HAT/HAA at CYBL ① is 200 feet and there is no TAF available, can CYBL be used as an IFR alternate?
answers: Yes, CYBL may be used as an IFR alternate since visibility is not forecast to be below 1/4 SM.
No, CYBL may not be used as an IFR alternate since ceilings are not forecast to be 1,000 feet above the lowest useable HAT/HAA and visibility is less than 3 SM.
No, CYBL may not be used as an IFR alternate since ceilings are not forecast to be 500 feet above the lowest useable HAT/HAA and visibility is less than 5 SM.
Yes, CYBL may be used as an IFR alternate since ceilings are not forecast to be below the lowest useable HAT/HAA.
explanation: CAP GEN – Operating Minima (Alternate) Aerodromes served with a GRAPHIC AREA FORECAST (GFA) may qualify as an alternate provided the forecast weather contains: No cloud lower than 1,000 ft above the lowest useable HAT/HAA No cumulonimbus Visibility not less than 3 miles CYBL is in an area forecast to have ceilings as low as 500 feet, which is below 1,000 feet above the lowest useable HAT/HAA. Furthermore, forecast visibility is less than 3SM. Therefore CYBL cannot be used as an IFR alternate.

question: Where a satellite-based approach is planned at both the destination and alternate, they must be separated by a minimum of
explanation: Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima Requirements When a satellite-based approach is planned at both the destination and alternate, the aerodromes must be separated by a minimum of 75 NM if both are in Nunavut or north of 56 degrees North latitude in Quebec and Labrador. If either or both aerodromes are located elsewhere in Canada, they must be separated by at least 100 NM. If both the destination and alternate are located in the area shaded in blue, they must be separated by at least 75 NM. If either or both the destination and alternate are located in the area shaded in yellow, they must be separated by at least 100 NM.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart The highest altitude between YPE (A) and YMM (B) along V325 that assures acceptable navigational signal coverage and that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements is
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation The minimum en route altitude (MEA) is the altitude above sea level (ASL) between specified fixes on airways or air routes that assures acceptable navigational signal coverage and that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements. It can be read directly from the LO chart as shown in the image below.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYQU VOR RWY 07 TAF CYHU 280239Z 2803/2824 26005KT P6SM BKN030 BKN050 TEMPO 2803/2806 BKN020 BKN030 FM280600 21006KT P6SM BKN020 TEMPO 2806/2810 5SM BR SCT020 FM281000 VRB03KT P6SM SCT020 TEMPO 2810/2815 3SM BR PROB30 2811/2815 2 1/2 SM SN OVC007 FM281500 09005KT P6SM FEW220 BECMG 2816/2818 09010KT FM282000 09010KT P6SM OVC090 FM282300 09012KT 3SM -RA BR BKN007 OVC015 RMK NXT FCST BY 280600Z= The planned ETA to CYQU is 1100Z. If the only available approach is the VOR RWY 17, can CYQU be used as an IFR alternate?
answers: No, because there is a 30% probability the ceilings will be below the alternate weather minima.
No, because the visibility is forecast to drop below the alternate minimum.
Yes, because the forecast visibility and ceiling will be above the required landing minima.
Yes, because there is no forecast ceiling and visibility is 3 SM.
explanation: CAP GEN: Operating Minima – Alternate Since only a non-precision approach is available at the alternate, the alternate weather minima requirements are 800-2 or 300-1 above the lowest useable HAT/HAA and visibility, whichever is greater. How to Determine the Minimum Ceiling and Visibility The minimum ceiling is 800 feet or 300 feet above the lowest useable HAT, whichever is greater. The lowest usable HAT is 665 feet and can be read directly from the chart. 665 + 300 = 965 feet. Therefore the minimum ceiling is 1,000 feet. Note that ceiling values in aviation forecasts are established in 100-ft increments. Up to 20 ft, use the lower 100-ft increment. Above 20 ft, use the next higher 100-ft increment. The minimum visibility is 2 miles or 1 mile above the advisory visibility published on the approach chart, whichever is greater. Since the published visibility is 2 miles, it is the limiting case and the minimum visibility is 3 miles. Interpreting the TAF Now that the minimum ceiling and visibility have been determined (1000 – 3), the suitability of CYQU as an alternate can be verified. TAF CYHU 280239Z 2803/2824 26005KT P6SM BKN030 BKN050 TEMPO 2803/2806 BKN020 BKN030 FM280600 21006KT P6SM BKN020 TEMPO 2806/2810 5SM BR SCT020 FM281000 VRB03KT P6SM SCT020 TEMPO 2810/2815 3SM BR PROB30 2811/2815 2 1/2 SM SN OVC007 FM281500 09005KT P6SM FEW220 BECMG 2816/2818 09010KT FM282000 09010KT P6SM OVC090 FM282300 09012KT 3SM -RA BR BKN007 OVC015 RMK NXT FCST BY 280600Z= FM281000 VRB03KT P6SM SCT020 – Ceiling and visibility are above alternate minima. TEMPO 2810/2815 3SM BR – Ceiling and visibility are above alternate minima. PROB30 2811/2815 2 1/2 SM SN OVC007 – Ceiling and visibility above landing the minima. Note that the PROB condition must only be above the landing minima, not the alternate minima. CYQU is a suitable IFR alternate.

question: If no IFR approach is available at a pilot’s desired alternate aerodrome, the forecast weather must be no lower than . . . . . above a minimum IFR altitude that will permit a VFR approach and landing.
explanation: Canada Air Pilot – Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima When a TAF is provided for an aerodrome in which no IFR approach is available, the forecast weather must be no lower than 500 feet above a minimum IFR altitude that will permit a VFR approach and landing.

question: The vertical coverage of a LO chart extends
answers: up to but not including 22,000 feet ASL.
up to but not including 18,000 feet ASL.
up to and including 18,000 feet ASL.
explanation: Enroute Low Altitude (LO) Chart The LO chart series, comprising 10 charts, depicts aeronautical radio information, airways system, controlled/uncontrolled airspace structure, special use airspace, communication stations and selected aerodromes. It is used for IFR route planning and inflight navigation. It is not suitable for VFR navigation as it provides no obstacle, terrain, road or other data needed for visual flight. Vertical coverage is up to, but not including, 18,000 feet ASL. The information contained on these charts is current only to the effective date. Since enroute information may be amended or cancelled, NOTAMs and AIP Supplements must be consulted to ensure that only current information is used for flight operations.

question: Select the correct statement regarding mandatory IFR routes.
answers: Each route includes a start or end waypoint/navigation aid.
Each mandatory IFR route contains a published MOCA and MEA.
The use of mandatory IFR Routes ensure that IFR operations will remain clear of class F airspace.
Mandatory IFR routes begin at an airway.
explanation: CFS – Mandatory IFR Routes Under the planning section of the CFS, a table of mandatory IFR routes is published for airports under Canadian ATS control. Its purpose is to guide pilots in planning their route of flight, minimize route changes during the operational phase of flight and aid in the efficient and orderly management of air traffic. Cooperation by all pilots in filing mandatory IFR routes will result in fewer traffic delays and will better provide for efficient departure, enroute, and arrival air traffic service. Each route includes a start or end point but does not begin with an airway and does not specify a MOCA or MEA. The pilot is responsible for determining the appropriate cruising altitude by considering the aircraft’s track, en route weather conditions, minimum obstacle clearance requirements, and navigational signal coverage.

question: When selecting a suitable alternate aerodrome with satellite-based approaches identified using a procedure identification of “RNAV (GNSS)” credit may be taken for LNAV/VNAV lines of minima when the aircraft is certified for barometric LNAV/VNAV. credit may not be taken for LPV or LP lines of minima. credit may be taken for LNAV lines of minima. credit may be taken by general aviation operators. credit may be taken for RNP 0.30 lines of minima only.
explanation: Canada Air Pilot: Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima Credit may be taken for satellite-based approaches provided that: Predicted satellite outages have been taken into account and the pilot-in-command verifies that approach-level RAIM or WAAS integrity is expected to be available at the proposed ETA for any aerodrome. For GPS TSO C129/C129a avionics, periodically during the flight, and at least once before the mid-point of the flight to the destination, the pilot-in-command verifies that approach-level RAIM is expected to be available at the planned destination and/or alternate ETA. For RNP Approach navigation specifications [procedure identification RNAV (GNSS) RWY XX]: No credit may be taken for LPV or LP lines of minima Credit may be taken for LNAV/VNAV lines of minima when the aircraft is certified for barometric LNAV/VNAV Credit may be taken for LNAV lines of minima For RNP AR Approach navigation specifications [procedure identification RNAV (RNP) RWY XX]: No credit may be taken by General Aviation operators

question: Credit for an alternate aerodrome may be taken for satellite-based approaches provided that
answers: the pilot-in-command verifies a ground based navigational aid is expected to be available at the planned destination and alternate ETA.
the alternate aerodrome is within controlled airspace.
predicted satellite outages have been taken into account.
the alternate aerodrome and destination aerodrome are separated by no more than 150 NM.
explanation: Canada Air Pilot: Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima Credit may be taken for satellite-based approaches provided that: Predicted satellite outages have been taken into account. The pilot-in-command verifies that approach-level RAIM or WAAS integrity is expected to be available at the proposed ETA for any aerodrome. For GPS TSO C129/C129a avionics, periodically during the flight, and at least once before the mid-point of the flight to the destination, the pilot-in-command verifies that approach-level RAIM is expected to be available at the planned destination and/or alternate ETA. Where a satellite-based approach is planned at both the destination and alternate, the aerodromes are separated by a minimum of 100 NM. For RNP Approach navigation specifications [procedure identification RNAV (GNSS) RWY XX]: No credit may be taken for LPV or LP lines of minima. Credit may be taken for LNAV/VNAV lines of minima when the aircraft is certified for barometric LNAV/VNAV. Credit may be taken for LNAV lines of minima.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CZBF NDB RWY 28 A pilot of an aircraft not equipped with IFR-certified GPS avionics wishes to select CZBF as an IFR alternate. If the only approach that relies on a ground-based navigational aid is the NDB RWY 28, what is the lowest useable alternate weather minimum?
explanation: Canada Air Pilot: Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima It may be tempting to select 800 – 2 since only a non-precision approach is available. However, recall that the highest of, 800 – 2 and (HAT + 300) – (VIS + 1) must be used. The HAT and VIS can be read directly off the NDB RWY 28 approach chart. Make sure to use the values appropriate to the category of aircraft. In this case, all categories have the same minimums. HAT + 300 = 641 + 300 = 941 ft. 941 ft is then rounded up to 1,000 ft. Note that, up to 20 ft, use the lower 100 ft increment and above 20 ft, use the next higher 100 ft increment. Since the charted visibility is 2 SM, the alternate aerodrome minimum visibility is 2 + 1 = 3 SM. Therefore the alternate weather minimum is 1000 – 3.

question: Aerodromes served with an AERODROME ADVISORY forecast may qualify as an alternate provided the forecast weather is no lower than . . . . . above the lowest usable HAT/HAA and the visibility is not less than . . . . .
explanation: CAP GEN – Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima Requirements Aerodromes served with an AERODROME ADVISORY forecast may qualify as an alternate provided the forecast weather is no lower than 500 ft above the lowest usable HAT/HAA and the visibility is not less than 3 miles. Example CYEU 082340Z 0900/0902 24015KT P6SM SCT040 OVC080 TEMPO 0900/0902 P6SM -SHRA BKN025 OVC060 RMK ADVISORY OFFSITE . NXT FCST BY 091100Z Aerodromes served with an AERODROME ADVISORY forecast may qualify as an alternate provided the forecast weather is no lower than 500 ft above the lowest usable HAT/HAA and the visibility is not less than 3 miles. Refer to Figure 1, if no other approach at CYEU offers a lower HAT, the alternate minima is determined by adding 500 feet to the charted HAT and rounding the result down to 1,000 feet. According to the CAP GEN, up to 20 ft, round down to the lower 100 ft increment. Above 20 ft, use the next higher 100 ft increment. Thus, the alternate minimum would be 1,000 – 3. Figure 1: RNAV (GNSS) RWY 28 instrument approach chart.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Select the correct statement regarding Pine Lake aerodrome (A).
answers: It is a joint civil and military airport.
The length of the longest runway is between 2,970 feet and 3,069 feet.
The length of the longest runway is 3,250 feet.
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation In many respects, aerodrome data displayed on a LO chart is quite similar to the way it is depicted on VNCs and VTAs. In this particular question, the correct statement is that the length of the longest runway available is between 2,980 feet and 3,069 feet.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYHU RNAV (GNSS) RWY 24R After arriving at the filed alternate (CYHU), the reported ceiling is overcast at 500 feet and the visibility is 1 SM. If the only available approach at CYHU is the RNAV (GNSS) RWY 24R a pilot
answers: may attempt the approach since alternate minimums are applicable only during flight planning.
may not attempt the approach because there is no suitable ground based approach available.
may not attempt the approach since the reported ceiling is lower than standard alternate minimums.
may not attempt the approach because the ceiling is below published minima.
explanation: CAR 602.123 Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima No pilot-in-command of an aircraft shall include an alternate aerodrome in an IFR flight plan or IFR flight itinerary unless available weather information indicates that the ceiling and visibility at the alternate aerodrome will, at the expected time of arrival, be at or above the alternate aerodrome weather minima specified in the Canada Air Pilot. However, once airborne, an approach is authorized into any aerodrome – including the alternate – whenever: the lowest reported RVR for the runway is at or above minima (CAR 602.129), regardless of reported ground visibility; the RVR is reported to be fluctuating above and below the minimum RVR; the ground visibility is reported to be at least 1/4 mile; the RVR for the runway is unavailable or not reported; or ATS is informed that an aircraft is on a training flight and will conduct a planned missed approach.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Select the correct statement regarding L707 (A).

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart The altitude between YQM (A) and YYG (B) along V300 that assures acceptable navigational signal coverage and that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements is
