question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Select the incorrect statement regarding an aircraft 30 miles south of HNS (A), northbound along V428.
answers: This route crosses an international boundary.
A cruise altitude of 9,000 feet is sufficient to ensure obstacle clearance at any point along V428 between HNS and YXY.
The highest MRA along V428 between HNS and YXY is 10,000 feet.
The MOCA changes at a mileage breakdown point 31 miles north of HNS.
explanation: Lo Chart Interpretation The MOCA changes several times along this route segment. Be careful when reading low charts and do not assume the MOCA, MEA or MRA remain constant along a particular airway.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart The airspace outlined in blue is
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation The outlined area indicates controlled airspace above 12,500 feet ASL in Canada.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart The pilot-in-command of an aircraft overhead the Koloe NDB (A) that would like to contact an area control centre may do so using
explanation: Glossary for Pilots and Air Traffic Services Personnel – Peripheral Station (PAL) A PAL is an unstaffed VHF/UHF transmitter/receiver facility used to facilitate direct contact between pilots and controllers. Sometimes these facilities are not equipped with emergency frequencies 121.5 MHz and 243.0 MHz. Call signs for PALs correspond to the ATS unit responsible for the radio frequency, not the physical location of the frequency. Call sign information for a PAL is displayed on various aeronautical publications and charts. To facilitate the operator who could be doing multiple RCOs, it would be appropriate to state which airport you are near on initial contact.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart To receive remote aerodrome advisory services (RAAS) at Red Lake airport, the proper frequency to use is
explanation: AIM RAC 1.1.2.3 Flight Information Centres (FICs) and Flight Service Stations (FSSs) Aerodrome Advisory Services (AAS) are provided by Flight Service Specialists working on-site at Flight Service Stations (FSS), and a Remote Communications Outlet (RCO), may be used by Flight Service Specialists to provide Remote Aerodrome Advisory Services (RAAS) to an off-site aerodrome. The services they provide include local weather information, airport advisory services (information on traffic in the area, runway conditions, wind and altimeter and other information required by pilots), and vehicle control and emergency assistance. They can also relay ATC clearances and make suggestions to pilots, but they do not issue instructions.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart The highest altitude between DEBUM (A) and TUKIR (B) that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements but does not assure an acceptable navigational signal is
explanation: Minimum Obstacle Clearance Altitude (MOCA) The altitude above sea level (ASL) between specified fixes on airways or air routes that meets the IFR obstacle clearance requirements for the route segment in question.

question: A LO chart is used for operations below
explanation: Enroute Low Altitude Chart (LO Chart) The LO chart is intended for use in the low-level airspace structure (below 18,000 feet ASL). The LO chart series consists of 10 charts and depicts aeronautical radio information, airways, the controlled/uncontrolled airspace structure, special use airspace, communication stations and selected aerodromes. It is used for IFR route planning and inflight navigation.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Can an aircraft that is not equipped with a functioning GNSS receiver fly directly from YPC (A) to YCO (B) along an established airway or air route?
answers: Yes, along either L741 or BR22 if the aircraft is equipped with a functioning ADF.
Yes, but only along BR22 if the aircraft is equipped with a functioning ADF.
No, GNSS is required to navigate along BR22 or L741.
Yes, but only along L741 if the aircraft is equipped with a functioning VOR receiver.
explanation: AIM RAC 11.4.4 Fixed RNAV Routes L-routes are low-level uncontrolled fixed RNAV routes depicted on En Route Low Altitude charts using green dashed lines and require GNSS RNAV systems for use. The MOCA provides obstacle protection for only 6 NM either side of the track centreline and does not splay. BR22 is a low-level air route defined using a pair of NDBs. Air routes are contained within Class G airspace. They are basically the same as a low-level airway, except that they extend upwards from the surface of the earth, are not controlled, and ATC separation is not provided to IFR or VFR aircraft. The lateral dimensions are identical to those of a low-level airway.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Select the correct statement regarding L707.
answers: It is a low-level uncontrolled RNAV route and requires a GNSS RNAV systems for use.
It is a low-level uncontrolled RNAV route and requires an ADF for use.
It is a low-level uncontrolled RNAV route and requires an VOR receiver for use.
It is a low-level controlled RNAV route and requires a GNSS RNAV systems for use.
explanation: AIM RAC 11.4.4 RNAV Routes L-routes are low-level uncontrolled fixed RNAV routes depicted on Enroute Low Altitude charts using green dashed lines and require GNSS RNAV systems for use.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYZT ILS RWY 11 SPECI CYZT 180235Z 18004KT 10SM BKN064 OVC077 06/04 A2927 RMK SC5AC3 SLP913= METAR CYZT 180200Z 19007KT 8SM -RA BKN039 OVC059 06/04 A2926 RMK SC5SC3 SLP910= METAR CYZT 180100Z 19008KT 12SM -SHRA SCT022 BKN043 OVC070 06/05 A2922 RMK CU3SC3AC2 SLP896= METAR CYZT 180000Z 23005KT 8SM -SHRA BKN022 OVC040 06/05 A2919 RMK SC5SC3 PRESRR SLP885= TAF CYZT 180038Z 1801/1813 23005KT P6SM SCT020 OVC040 TEMPO 1801/1813 1SM -SHRA BR BKN010 OVC040 BECMG 1808/1810 11007KT RMK NXT FCST BY 180700Z= A pilot is considering selecting CYZT as an alternate aerodrome. Approaches available at CYZT include the ILS RWY 11 and a variety of RNAV (GNSS) approaches. If the ETA to CYZT is 1230Z can it be used as a suitable IFR alternate?
answers: No, because the forecast ceiling and visibility at the planned ETA are less than 600 feet and 2 SM respectively.
No, because TEMPO periods may not be used to determine the weather suitability of an aerodrome as an alternate.
Yes, because the forecast ceiling and visibility at the planned ETA are 800 feet and 1 SM respectively.
Yes, because the forecast ceiling and visibility at the planned ETA are 600 feet and 2 SM respectively.
explanation: Canada Air Pilot: Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima First, determine the alternate aerodrome weather minima. Since CYZT has one usable precision approach, the alternate minima are 600 – 2 or 300 – 1 above the lowest usable HAT/HAA and visibility, whichever is greater. NOTE: 600-2 and 800-2 are considered to be Standard Alternate Minima. Should the selected alternate weather requirements meet the standard minima, then the following minima summarized in the table below are also authorized. Keep this in mind! Next, refer to the TAF and determine the forecast ceilings and visibility at the planned ETA at CYZT which is 1230Z. TAF CYZT 180038Z 1801/1813 23005KT P6SM SCT020 OVC040 TEMPO 1801/1813 1SM -SHRA BR BKN010 OVC040 BECMG 1808/1810 11007KT RMK NXT FCST BY 180700Z= Between 0100Z and 1300Z there is a TEMPO. Visibility will be 1SM and the ceiling will be 1,000 feet. A gradual change (BECMG) will begin at 0800Z but only a change in the prevailing winds is forecast. Although forecast visibility at the ETA is 1SM, since CYZT has one precision approach, standard alternate minima can be applied. A visibility of 1SM is thus acceptable as long as the forecast ceiling is greater than or equal to 800 feet, which it is. Therefore CYZT is an acceptable alternate aerodrome.

question: On an IFR LO Chart, a compass rose in Northern Domestic Airspace is oriented to
explanation: Compass Rose The compass rose is oriented to magnetic north except in Northern Domestic Airspace where VHF/UHF aids may be oriented to true or grid north.

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart Select the correct statement regarding MUSKK (A).
answers: It is a compulsory reporting point.
It is 103 miles west of VLR along V300.
The minimum obstacle clearance height is 14,000 feet.
It is on the 285° radial from YWG.
explanation: LO Chart Interpretation

question: Refer to the Appendix: LO Chart T697 (A) is a low-level
answers: uncontrolled fixed RNAV route and requires a GNSS RNAV systems for use.
uncontrolled fixed RNAV route and does not require a GNSS RNAV systems for use.
controlled fixed RNAV route and requires a GNSS RNAV system for use.
controlled fixed RNAV route and does not require a GNSS RNAV system for use.
explanation: AIM RAC 11.4.4 Fixed Area Navigation (RNAV) Routes T-routes are low-level controlled fixed RNAV routes depicted on En Route Low Altitude charts using black dashed lines and require GNSS RNAV systems for use. The airspace associated with T-routes extends upward from 2,200 ft AGL, 10 NM either side of the centreline, and does not splay. The MOCA provides obstacle protection for only 6 NM either side of the track centreline and does not splay.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CZBF ILS RWY 18 If the ILS RWY 18 is available and the winds are forecast to favour runway 18 at the ETA, what is the corresponding alternate aerodrome weather minima?
explanation: Canada Air Pilot: Alternate Aerodrome Weather Minima The alternate weather minima requirements are dependent on the number and type of approaches available at the proposed alternate aerodrome. In this particular example, since one useable precision approach is available, the appropriate alternate weather minimum requirement is 600 – 2 or (HAT + 300) – (VIS + 1), whichever is greater. The HAT and VIS can be read directly from the approach chart, however, be sure to read the minima corresponding to the appropriate category of aircraft. In this case, all categories have the same minima. 300 – 1 above the lowest useable HAT and visibility is 500 – 1 1/2 which is less than 600 – 2. Therefore, the alternate weather minimum is 600 – 2.

question: Refer to the Appendix: CAP CYYB VOR/DME RWY 08 The only available approach at CYYB is the VOR/DME RWY 08. Given the information below and an ETA of 1100Z, is CYYB a suitable alternate? TAF CYYB 050038Z 0501/0513 13007KT P6SM SCT150 BECMG 0503/0505 10015G25KT FM050600 10015G25KT P6SM SCT080 TEMPO 0506/0508 P6SM -SN OVC040 FM050800 10020G30KT P6SM -SN SCT025 OVC040 TEMPO 0508/0510 2SM -SN BLSN VV020 FM051100 12017G27KT 2SM SN BLSN VV008 TEMPO 0511/0513 1 1/2SM -SN BLSN VV009 RMK NXT FCST BY 050700Z=
answers: No, because the forecast ceiling and visibility will be lower than the landing minima.
No, because the ceiling and visibility is forecast to be 800 feet and 2 SM respectively, which are below the required alternate minima.
Yes, because the ceiling and visibility is forecast to be above the required alternate minima at the planned ETA.
Yes, because the ceiling and visibility is forecast to be 2,000 feet and 2SM respectively, which are above the required alternate minima.
explanation: CAP GEN: Operating Minima – Alternate Since there is only one non-precision approach available, the alternate weather requirements are 800 – 2 or 300 – 1 above the lowest useable HAT/HAA and visibility, whichever is greater. The lowest usable HAT and visibility are 332 feet and 1 mile respectively as shown in the excerpt of the approach chart below. Therefore the alternate weather requirement is 800 – 2 or 700- 2, whichever is greater. Note, up to 20 feet, use the lower 100 feet increment and above 20 feet, use the next higher 100 ft increment. Furthermore, 800 – 2 is considered a standard alternate minimum. Should the selected alternate weather requirements meet the standard minima, then the minima summarized in the table below are also authorized. Therefore, if the ceiling and visibility are greater than any of the combinations listed below, CYBB may be used as an IFR alternate: 800 – 2 900 – 1 1/2 1,000 – 1

question: What is the basic width on each side of the centreline for a VHF/UHF (Victor) airway?
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — The basic VHF/UHF airway width is 4 NM on each side of the centreline. The airway splays at 4.5° beyond the basic width boundary near each VOR facility.

question: What is the basic width on each side of the centreline for an LF/MF airway (NDB to NDB)?
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — The basic LF/MF airway width is 4.34 NM on each side of the centreline. The airway splays at 5° beyond the basic width boundary from each NDB facility.

question: At what angle does a VHF/UHF airway splay beyond the basic width boundary from a VOR facility?
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — VHF/UHF airway width increases between points where lines diverging 4.5° on each side of the centreline from the designated VOR facility intersect the basic width boundary.

question: At what angle does an LF/MF airway splay beyond the basic width boundary from an NDB facility?
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — LF/MF airway width increases between points where lines diverging 5° on each side of the centreline from the designated NDB facility intersect the basic width boundary.

question: Where a Victor airway is established based on a VOR/VORTAC and NDB, what airway boundaries apply?
answers: The boundaries of a VHF/UHF airway (4 NM, 4.5° splay)
The boundaries of an LF/MF airway (4.34 NM, 5° splay)
Intermediate boundaries of 4.17 NM on each side
The narrower of the two airway types applies
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — Where a Victor airway is established based on a VOR/VORTAC and NDB, the boundaries of that airway will be those of an LF/MF airway (4.34 NM, 5° splay).

question: Refer to the T-Route diagram. What are the obstacle protection dimensions of a low-level T-Route?
answers: 2 NM primary plus 2 NM secondary on each side
4 NM primary plus 2 NM secondary on each side
6 NM primary plus 2 NM secondary on each side
4 NM primary only, with no secondary area
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — Low-level controlled fixed RNAV routes (T-Routes) have dimensions of 4 NM of primary obstacle protection area, plus 2 NM of secondary obstacle protection area on each side of the centreline.

question: The MOCA for a T-Route provides obstacle protection for how far either side of the track centreline?
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — The MOCA for T-Routes provides obstacle protection for only 6 NM either side of the track centreline and does not splay. The associated airspace extends 10 NM either side.

question: How are L-routes (low-level uncontrolled fixed RNAV routes) depicted on En Route Low Altitude charts?
explanation: AIM RAC — L-routes are low-level uncontrolled fixed RNAV routes depicted on En Route Low Altitude charts using green dashed lines. They require GNSS RNAV systems and the MOCA provides obstacle protection for only 6 NM either side of the track centreline.

question: What is the minimum obstacle clearance altitude (MOCA) for airways and air routes within designated mountainous areas 2, 3, and 4?
explanation: AIM RAC — The established MOCA for IFR operations is 1,500 ft for airways and air routes within designated mountainous areas 2, 3, and 4. It is 2,000 ft in mountainous areas 1 and 5, and 1,000 ft outside designated mountainous areas.

question: What is the MOCA for airways and air routes within designated mountainous areas 1 and 5?
explanation: AIM RAC — The established MOCA is 2,000 ft for airways and air routes within designated mountainous areas 1 and 5. Mountainous areas 2, 3, and 4 require 1,500 ft, and non-mountainous areas require 1,000 ft.

question: What is the normal radius of a control zone that has a civil control tower within a terminal control area?
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — Control zones having a civil control tower within a terminal control area normally have a 7-NM radius. Others have a 5-NM radius, with the exception of a few which have a 3-NM radius.

question: To what altitude are civil control zones capped, unless otherwise specified?
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — Control zones are capped at 3,000 ft AAE unless otherwise specified.

question: What is the typical radius and cap for a military control zone?
answers: 5 NM radius, capped at 3,000 ft AAE
7 NM radius, capped at 5,000 ft AAE
10 NM radius, capped at 6,000 ft AAE
10 NM radius, capped at 3,000 ft AAE
explanation: AIM RAC 2.7.1 — Military control zones usually have a 10-NM radius and are capped at 6,000 ft AAE, compared to civil control zones which are capped at 3,000 ft AAE.

question: At what altitude does the Southern Control Area (SCA) begin?
explanation: AIM RAC — The Southern Control Area (SCA) begins at 18,000 ft ASL and above. Above 18,000 ft ASL, all flights in the SCA must be conducted under IFR.

question: At what altitude does the Northern Control Area (NCA) begin?
explanation: AIM RAC — The Northern Control Area (NCA) begins at FL 230 and above.

question: At what altitude does the Arctic Control Area (ACA) begin?
explanation: AIM RAC — The Arctic Control Area (ACA) begins at FL 270 and above.

question: A difference greater than how many degrees between an aircraft's two VOR receivers indicates one may be beyond acceptable tolerance?
explanation: AIM RAC — A difference greater than 4° between the aircraft's two VOR receivers indicates that one of the receivers may be beyond acceptable tolerance and should be investigated before IFR use.

question: An airborne VOR check that varies more than what value from the published radial should not be used for IFR navigation?
explanation: AIM RAC — VOR equipment that varies more than ±6° from the published radial should not be used for IFR navigation. The accuracy of published VOR radials is ±3°.

question: What is the published accuracy of a VOR airway radial?
explanation: AIM RAC — The accuracy of course alignment for published VOR radials is ±3°. On an airway check, equipment within ±6° is acceptable for IFR use.

question: When using a VOT (VOR Test Signal) to check a VOR receiver, what is the acceptable tolerance?
explanation: AIM RAC — VOT check tolerance is ±4°. The VOR indicator should show 180° TO or 360° FROM when tuned to a VOT. A ground or airborne check on a published radial must also be within ±4°.

question: What is the flight-checked accuracy of an NDB system for an instrument approach?
explanation: AIM RAC — NDB systems are flight checked to an accuracy of at least ±5° for an approach and ±10° for en route. Larger errors are possible due to propagation disturbances such as sunrise/sunset, terrain, shoreline refraction, and electrical storms.

question: Coastal refraction errors with an NDB are more pronounced when signals cross a shoreline at what angle?
explanation: AIM RAC — NDB coastal refraction is more pronounced when a beam is received from an inland NDB that crosses a shoreline at less than 30°. Signals crossing at shallow angles are bent toward the shoreline.

question: With a fixed-card ADF, where does the needle always point?
explanation: AIM RAC — A fixed-card ADF has a needle that always points to the station. The needle indicates the relative bearing (RB) to the NDB from the aircraft's nose.

question: What is the normal reliable coverage of an ILS localizer within 10° of either side of the course centreline?
explanation: AIM RAC — The normal, reliable coverage of ILS localizers is 18 NM within 10° of either side of the course centreline, and 10 NM within 35° of the course centreline.

question: What is the normal reliable coverage of an ILS localizer within 35° of either side of the course centreline?
explanation: AIM RAC — The normal, reliable coverage of ILS localizers is 10 NM within 35° of the course centreline, and 18 NM within 10° of the course centreline.

question: How many times daily are Graphic Area Forecast (GFA) charts issued?
explanation: AIM MET — GFA charts are issued four times daily, approximately 30 minutes before the beginning of the forecast period. They cover weather at or below 24,000 ft ASL.

question: At what wind speed threshold are winds included in a Graphic Area Forecast (GFA)?
explanation: AIM MET — GFA winds are included if above 20 kt, and gusts are included if above 30 kt.

question: In a GFA, the notation 'QS' beside a weather system means the system is moving at what speed?
explanation: AIM MET — QS in a GFA means the weather system is quasi-stationary, moving less than 5 kt.

question: What spatial coverage does the abbreviation 'PTCHY' indicate?
explanation: AIM MET — In GFA non-convective cloud descriptions: LCA = Local (25% or less), PTCHY = Patchy (26–50%), XTNSV/INTMT = Extensive/Intermittent (greater than 50%).

question: Which category applies when the ceiling is between 1,000 ft and 3,000 ft AGL and/or visibility is between 3 and 5 SM?
explanation: AIM MET — MVFR (Marginal VFR) applies when ceiling is between 1,000 ft and 3,000 ft AGL and/or visibility is between 3 and 5 SM. IFR applies below 1,000 ft AGL and/or less than 3 SM.

question: Which type of ice is described as rough, milky, and opaque, formed by the instantaneous freezing of small supercooled water droplets?
explanation: AIM MET — Rime ice is rough, milky, opaque ice formed by the instantaneous freezing of small supercooled water droplets. It forms in layer cloud at temperatures of 0°C to –15°C and is extensive in the horizontal.

question: What action is required when severe icing is encountered?
answers: Monitor closely for the next hour
Use de-icing equipment if encounter is prolonged
De-icing or anti-icing equipment fails to control the hazard; immediate diversion is necessary
Diversion required only if de-icing equipment is unavailable
explanation: AIM MET Table 2.3 — Severe icing: The rate of accumulation is such that de-icing or anti-icing equipment fails to reduce or control the hazard. Immediate diversion is necessary.

question: An aircraft certified for flight in known icing conditions meets what level of icing protection?
explanation: AIM MET — An aircraft certified for known icing meets moderate icing requirements. Encountering severe icing in a 'known icing' certified aircraft still requires immediate action to exit the icing environment.

question: Hoar frost may form on wing surfaces at ambient temperatures well above freezing due to which cause?
answers: Ice crystals in the upper atmosphere
Cold-soaked fuel in the wing tanks
Warm moist air descending over the wing
explanation: AIM MET — Hoar frost may form on the upper or lower surface of the wings at ambient temperatures well above the freezing point due to the cold soaked fuel in the wing tanks.

question: Which type of precipitation is most commonly associated with warm fronts and causes severe clear icing?
explanation: AIM MET — Freezing rain, often associated with warm fronts, causes severe clear icing. Clear ice forms from large supercooled water droplets at temperatures between 0°C and –25°C in convective cloud.

question: What does the code 'FEW' indicate for cloud coverage?
explanation: AIM MET Table 8.2 — FEW = less than 1/8 to 2/8 summation amount. SCT (scattered) = 3/8 to 4/8, BKN (broken) = 5/8 to less than 8/8, OVC (overcast) = 8/8. VV (Vertical Visibility) constitutes a ceiling.

question: TAF aerodrome forecasts are intended to relate to weather conditions within how many NM of the centre of the runway complex?
explanation: AIM MET — TAFs are intended to relate to weather conditions for flight operations within 5 NM of the centre of the runway complex.

question: For how long are standard Canadian aerodrome forecasts (TAF) valid?
explanation: AIM MET — Standard aerodrome forecasts (TAF) are valid for 12 hours. International or Military TAFs are valid for 24 hours. TAFs are issued approximately 30 minutes prior to the validity period.

question: When a TAF contains a BECMG group indicating improving conditions, for alternate planning purposes, the BECMG conditions are applicable as of when?
answers: The start of the BECMG time period
The midpoint of the BECMG time period
The end of the BECMG time period
Throughout the entire BECMG time period
explanation: CAR 602 — Where conditions are forecast to improve, the BECMG condition shall be considered applicable as of the end of the BECMG time period, and conditions must not be below published alternate minima.

question: When a TAF contains a BECMG group indicating deteriorating conditions, for alternate planning purposes, the BECMG conditions are applicable as of when?
answers: The start of the BECMG time period
The midpoint of the BECMG time period
The end of the BECMG time period
Throughout the entire BECMG time period
explanation: CAR 602 — Where conditions are forecast to deteriorate, the BECMG condition shall be considered applicable as of the start of the BECMG time period, and conditions must not be below published alternate minima.

question: For alternate aerodrome planning, a TAF PROB condition must not be below what requirement?
explanation: CAR 602 — The forecast PROB condition shall not be below the appropriate landing minima for that aerodrome, whereas BECMG and TEMPO conditions must both meet alternate minima.

question: Refer to the alternate aerodrome weather minima table. When two or more usable precision approaches are available at the alternate (each providing straight-in minima to separate suitable runways), what is the alternate weather requirement?
answers: 600-2 or 300-1 above lowest HAT/VIS, whichever is greater
400-1 or 200-1/2 above lowest HAT/VIS, whichever is greater
800-2 or 300-1 above lowest HAT/HAA, whichever is greater
explanation: CAP GEN Table 3.7 — When two or more usable precision approaches are available, each providing straight-in minima to separate suitable runways, the requirement is 400-1 or 200-1/2 above the lowest usable HAT and visibility, whichever is greater.

question: Refer to the alternate minima table. When one usable precision approach is available at the alternate, what are the alternate weather requirements?
answers: 400-1 or 200-1/2 above lowest HAT, whichever is greater
600-2 or 300-1 above lowest HAT and visibility, whichever is greater
800-2 or 300-1 above lowest HAT/HAA, whichever is greater
Forecast weather no lower than 500 ft above minimum IFR altitude
explanation: CAP GEN Table 3.7 — When one usable precision approach is available, the alternate weather requirement is 600-2 or 300-1 above the lowest usable HAT and visibility, whichever is greater.

question: When only a non-precision approach is available at the alternate aerodrome, what are the alternate weather requirements?
answers: 600-2 or 300-1 above lowest HAT, whichever is greater
700-2 or 400-1 above lowest HAT/HAA, whichever is greater
800-2 or 300-1 above lowest HAT/HAA, whichever is greater
1,000-3 or 500-1 above lowest HAT/HAA, whichever is greater
explanation: CAP GEN Table 3.7 — When only a non-precision approach is available, the alternate weather requirement is 800-2 or 300-1 above the lowest usable HAT/HAA and visibility, whichever is greater.

question: Refer to the standard alternate minima table. When the standard alternate minimum is 600-2 (one precision approach), which additional ceiling/visibility combinations are also authorized?
explanation: CAP GEN — When 600-2 is the standard alternate minimum, the following combinations are also authorized: ceiling 700 with visibility 1½, or ceiling 800 with visibility 1.

question: For an aerodrome served only with a Graphic Area Forecast (GFA) to qualify as an alternate, which condition is required?
answers: No cloud lower than 500 ft above the lowest HAT/HAA
No cloud lower than 1,000 ft above lowest HAT/HAA, no CB, and visibility not less than 3 SM
No cloud lower than 2,000 ft AGL and visibility not less than 5 SM
Ceiling above 1,500 ft and visibility not less than 3 SM
explanation: CAR 602 — For a GFA alternate: (a) no cloud lower than 1,000 ft above the lowest usable HAT/HAA; (b) no cumulonimbus; and (c) visibility not less than 3 SM.

question: How much fuel must a propeller-driven aeroplane carry for IFR flight when an alternate aerodrome is specified in the flight plan?
answers: To destination + missed approach + alternate + 30 minutes
To destination + missed approach + alternate + 45 minutes
explanation: CAR 602 — A propeller-driven aeroplane must carry enough fuel to fly to and execute an approach and missed approach at the destination, fly to and land at the alternate aerodrome, and then fly for 45 minutes.

question: How much fuel must a turbojet aeroplane carry for IFR flight when no alternate aerodrome is required?
explanation: CAR 602 — A turbojet aeroplane (when no alternate is required) must carry enough fuel to fly to and execute an approach and missed approach at the destination, and then fly for 30 minutes.

question: What minimum flight visibility is required for a pilot to conduct a contact approach?
explanation: AIM RAC — A contact approach requires ATC authorization, operating clear of clouds, and at least 1 SM flight visibility with a reasonable expectation of continuing to the destination in those conditions.

question: Who must initiate a contact approach?
answers: ATC can offer it to the pilot
The pilot must request it from ATC
It is automatically authorized when VMC exists
The tower broadcasts authorization on ATIS
explanation: AIM RAC — A contact approach must be requested by the pilot. ATC does not initiate contact approaches — the pilot must specifically request it and receive ATC authorization.

question: A pilot-in-command shall notify ATC of a TAS change when the intended change is what percentage or more of the TAS specified in the IFR flight plan?
explanation: CAR 602.76 — A pilot shall notify ATC as soon as practicable of any change to the TAS where the change intended is 5% or more of the TAS specified in the IFR flight plan or clearance.

question: What change in Mach number requires a pilot to notify ATC when operating on a Mach number clearance?
explanation: CAR 602.76 — A pilot must notify ATC where a change in Mach number is intended to be 0.01 or more of the Mach number included in the ATC clearance.

question: When must an arrival report for an IFR flight plan be submitted if no SAR time is specified?
answers: Not later than 1 hour after the last reported ETA
Not later than 2 hours after the last reported ETA
explanation: CAR 602.77 — Where no SAR time is specified in the IFR flight plan, the arrival report shall be submitted not later than one hour after the last reported ETA.

question: When must an arrival report for an IFR flight itinerary be submitted if no SAR time is specified?
answers: Not later than 1 hour after the last reported ETA
Not later than 12 hours after the last reported ETA
Not later than 24 hours after the last reported ETA
Not later than 6 hours after the last reported ETA
explanation: CAR 602 — Where no SAR time was specified in the flight itinerary, an arrival report shall be filed not later than 24 hours after the last reported ETA.

question: What is the earliest time a pilot should obtain an IFR clearance before engine start?
explanation: AIM RAC — The earliest a pilot should obtain an IFR clearance is 5 minutes before engine start.

question: What is the required inspection interval for the pitot-static system when used for IFR operations?
explanation: CAR 625 — The pitot-static system must be inspected within the preceding 24 months for IFR flight. The transponder also requires a 24-month inspection.

question: What is the required inspection interval for a magnetic compass on an IFR aircraft?
explanation: CAR 625 — The magnetic compass must be inspected within the preceding 12 months, while the pitot-static system and transponder require 24-month inspections.

question: What is the allowable calibration tolerance for an altimeter used for IFR flight?
explanation: CAR 605 — Altimeters must be calibrated within ±50 ft for IFR operations.

question: To operate IFR in Class G uncontrolled airspace, what additional navigation equipment is required beyond standard IFR instruments?
answers: ADF and an outside air temperature (OAT) gauge
explanation: CAR 605.18 — To operate IFR in Class G uncontrolled airspace, the aircraft must have ADF and an outside air temperature (OAT) gauge in addition to standard IFR equipment.

question: How many satellites are required for a GPS receiver to provide a 3D position fix?
explanation: AIM RAC — GPS requires a minimum of 4 satellites to compute a 3D position fix (latitude, longitude, and altitude).

question: How many satellites are required for GPS Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM)?
explanation: AIM RAC — RAIM requires 5 satellites to monitor and verify the integrity of the GPS position solution. If fewer than 5 suitable satellites are in view, RAIM cannot provide integrity monitoring.

question: How many satellites are required for GPS Fault Detection and Exclusion (FDE / D.E.)?
explanation: AIM RAC — GPS Fault Detection and Exclusion (D.E.) requires 6 satellites, which allows a faulty satellite to be identified and excluded while still maintaining integrity monitoring.

question: What is the GPS CDI full-scale deflection sensitivity in the en route phase of flight?
explanation: AIM RAC — GPS CDI sensitivity: En route = 5 NM full-scale deflection. This transitions to 1.0 NM in the terminal area (at 30 NM from destination) and 0.3 NM in the approach phase (at 2 NM before FAF).

question: What is the GPS CDI full-scale deflection sensitivity in the terminal area?
explanation: AIM RAC — GPS CDI sensitivity: Terminal area = 1.0 NM full-scale deflection. This change typically occurs at 30 NM from the destination and lasts until 2 NM before the FAF.

question: What is the GPS CDI full-scale deflection sensitivity in the approach phase?
explanation: AIM RAC — GPS CDI sensitivity: Approach phase = 0.3 NM full-scale deflection. This change occurs 2 NM before the FAF and continues through to the missed approach point (MAP).

question: What is the Non-WAAS GPS en route alert limit (the distance within which RAIM will alert if position error is exceeded)?
explanation: AIM RAC — For Non-WAAS GPS: en route alert limit is 2 NM, terminal is 1 NM, and approach (2 NM from FAF) is 0.3 NM. WAAS improves the en route alert limit to 2 NM as well but with greater integrity.

question: What is the RAIM availability requirement for IFR approach operations?
answers: Unavailable for no more than 5 minutes during ETA ±30 min
Unavailable for no more than 15 minutes during ETA ±60 min
Unavailable for no more than 30 minutes during ETA ±60 min
explanation: AIM RAC — The RAIM availability requirement is satisfied if RAIM will be unavailable for a total of 15 minutes or less during the ETA ±60-minute period.

question: On what cycle is a GNSS/GPS IFR electronic navigation database normally updated?
explanation: AIM RAC — GNSS avionics used for IFR flight require an electronic database that can be updated, normally on 28- or 56-day cycles.

question: When flying a GNSS overlay approach, is it necessary to monitor the underlying traditional navigation aid?
answers: Yes, but only within 10 NM of the airport
No, monitoring the underlying nav aid is not necessary
Only if the underlying aid is a VOR
explanation: AIM RAC — When flying a GNSS overlay approach, it is not necessary to monitor the underlying traditional navigation aid. The overlay approach can even be flown if the underlying nav aid is out of service or NOTAM'd.

question: Before using a GPS approach procedure, when must approach-level RAIM be confirmed as available?
explanation: AIM RAC — Before using GPS approach procedures, approach-level RAIM must be available within ±15 minutes of the estimated time of arrival.

question: If two GPS approaches are planned — one for the destination and one for the alternate — what distance requirement must be met?
explanation: AIM RAC — If using two GPS approaches, one for the destination and one for the alternate, the two aerodromes must be at least 100 NM apart.

question: Under a standard IFR departure, at what height above aerodrome elevation (AAE) may an aircraft begin to turn after takeoff?
explanation: AIM RAC / CAP GEN — A standard IFR departure requires the aircraft to climb straight ahead to 400 ft above aerodrome elevation (AAE) before turning. The aircraft must also cross the departure end of the runway at least 35 ft above it.

question: What is the minimum climb gradient that must be maintained throughout an IFR standard departure?
explanation: AIM RAC — A standard IFR departure must maintain a climb gradient of at least 200 ft/NM throughout the climb to the minimum IFR altitude for en route operation.

question: When a departure procedure plate shows 'NOT ASSESSED' in the takeoff minima box, what takeoff minima must be used?
explanation: CAP GEN — NOT ASSESSED means IFR departures have not been assessed for obstacle clearance. Pilots are responsible for determining minimum climb gradients. Takeoff minima of ½ SM or RVR 2600 must be applied.

question: In the visibility hierarchy for IFR takeoff, which source has the highest priority?
explanation: AIM RAC — The visibility hierarchy for IFR takeoff is: (1) RVR value — highest priority, (2) Accredited observer (Tower/FSS/CARS station), (3) PIC (based on 200 ft between runway lights).

question: When a pilot-in-command self-reports visibility for an IFR takeoff, what is the reference used?
explanation: AIM RAC — When the PIC reports visibility for takeoff purposes, it is based on 200 ft spacing between runway lights.

question: VFR OTT operations are only permitted during what portion of a flight?
answers: During the cruise portion of the flight only
explanation: CAR 602.116 — VFR OTT is allowed during the day only, and during the cruise portion of the flight only.

question: What is the minimum vertical distance from cloud required for VFR OTT operations?
explanation: CAR 602.116 — For VFR OTT, the aircraft must be operated at a vertical distance from cloud of at least 1,000 ft.

question: When operating VFR OTT between two cloud layers, what minimum separation must exist between those layers?
explanation: CAR 602.116 — Where the aircraft is operated between two cloud layers during VFR OTT, those layers must be at least 5,000 ft apart.

question: An aircraft takes 120 seconds to change its ADF relative bearing by 10°. Using the time-to-station formula, how many minutes until the aircraft reaches the NDB?
explanation: AIM RAC — Time to NDB (minutes) = time (seconds) ÷ degrees of relative bearing change = 120 ÷ 10 = 12 minutes.

question: An aircraft with a groundspeed of 120 kt takes 6 minutes to reach an NDB station. What is the aircraft's distance from the NDB?
explanation: AIM RAC — Distance to NDB (NM) = GS (kt) × Time to NDB (minutes) ÷ 60 = 120 × 6 ÷ 60 = 12 NM.

question: What type of altitude is used as the decision point for precision instrument approaches?
explanation: CAP GEN — Decision Altitude (DA) or Decision Height (DH) is used for precision approaches (ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV). Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA) is used for non-precision approaches.

question: The Minimum Sector Altitude (MSA) on an instrument approach chart is calculated from what reference point?
explanation: CAP GEN — MSA is calculated from the centre of the aerodrome and provides 1,000 ft clearance above all objects within a 25 NM radius.

question: Refer to the aircraft category speeds table. An aircraft with a Vref of 100 KIAS falls into which approach category?
explanation: CAP GEN — Aircraft approach categories by speed: Category A = up to 90 KIAS (includes all rotorcraft), Category B = 91 to 120 KIAS, Category C = 121 to 140 KIAS, Category D = 141 to 165 KIAS, Category E = above 165 KIAS.

question: If Vref is not specified for an aircraft, how is the approach category speed determined?
answers: Use 1.2 times stall speed in landing configuration (Vso)
Use 1.3 times stall speed in landing configuration (Vso)
explanation: CAP GEN — If Vref is not specified, the approach category is based on 1.3 times the stall speed in the landing configuration (1.3 × Vso).

question: During a missed approach, which of the following actions is prohibited?
answers: Retracting flaps after initiating the go-around
Descending below the DH or MDA
Contacting ATC to report the missed approach
explanation: CAP GEN — During a missed approach, a pilot must not descend below the Decision Height (DH) or Minimum Descent Altitude (MDA). Climb must not be initiated until the Missed Approach Waypoint (MAWP).

question: What is the allowable tolerance for a DME system used for IFR navigation?
explanation: AIM RAC — DME tolerance is 0.5 NM or 3%, whichever is greater. This same tolerance applies when using GPS as a substitute for DME.

question: Where is DME slant range error the greatest?
answers: At a 45° angle to the station at medium altitude
explanation: AIM RAC — Maximum DME slant range error occurs directly above the station. An aircraft far away and at low altitude experiences the least slant range error.

question: Within what frequency band do VORs operate?
explanation: AIM RAC — VORs operate within the frequency range 112.0 to 117.95 MHz. Note: 108.0 to 111.95 MHz is shared with ILS localizers.

question: Within what frequency band do NDB/ADF systems operate?
explanation: AIM RAC — ADF/NDB systems operate in the frequency range 190–515 kHz. Unlike VHF signals, NDB signals are not line-of-sight and produce both ground waves and sky waves.

question: On an IFR chart, what does an underline beneath a navigation aid frequency or identifier box indicate?
answers: The facility has no voice capability on that frequency
explanation: AIM RAC — On IFR charts, if a navigation aid identifier or frequency is underlined, it means the facility has no voice capability on that frequency.

question: What is the formula for calculating the approximate line-of-sight VOR reception range?
explanation: AIM RAC — VOR reception range (NM) ≈ 1.23 × √altitude AGL (in feet). For example, at 10,000 ft AGL: 1.23 × √10,000 = 1.23 × 100 = 123 NM.

question: What does a pilot mean when declaring 'MINIMUM FUEL' to ATC?
answers: A fuel emergency requiring immediate priority landing
The aircraft has reached a fuel state requiring commitment to a specific aerodrome with no additional delay acceptable
Less than 30 minutes of fuel remaining
The aircraft cannot accept any holding instructions under any circumstances
explanation: AIM RAC — Minimum Fuel is not a distress or urgency condition, but describes a situation where the aircraft's fuel supply has reached a state where it is committed to land at a specific aerodrome and no additional delay can be accepted.

question: Which PAPI category applies to an aircraft with an eye-to-wheel height (EWH) in the approach configuration between 10 ft and 25 ft?
explanation: AIM RAC Table 7.2 — PAPI Category P2 applies to aircraft with an eye-to-wheel height (EWH) in the approach configuration between 10 ft (3 m) and 25 ft (7.5 m). P3 applies to 25 ft to less than 45 ft.

question: How much minimum cross country time is required for a holder of an instrument rating?
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements

question: What is the minimum instrument time required to hold an instrument rating?
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements

question: What is the minimum dual instrument time that is needed from a licensed flight instructor for an initial IFR rating?
answers: There is no legal requirement to have a flight instructor teach instruments
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements

question: How many minimum hours on type does an IFR holder need when applying for an IFR for a Group 1,2 or 3 aircraft?
answers: There is no requirement on type but there is a checkout needed to fly.
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements

question: How much ground simulator time (FTD) are you allowed towards the 40 hour total needed for an IFR rating?
answers: You may not use simulator time for an initial IFR rating
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements

question: In order to hold an IFR rating you must have completed a cross country under actual or simulated IMC. The minimum distance for this cross country is…?
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements

question: The 40 hours of instrument time required for an IFR rating must include 15 hours from the holder of which of the following?
answers: A CPL holder with an IFR rating
An ATPL holder with an IFR rating
A CPL holder with a Flight Instructor Rating
explanation: Ref: CARS section 425.21(9) https://tc.canada.ca/node/32775#425_21_9

question: When training for the initial IFR rating your minimum 100NM cross country must include instrument approaches to minima. How many different locations are legally required?
answers: They can all be conducted at the same airport if they have two or more published approaches, so only one location is required
They must be conducted at 2 different locations with published approaches
They must be conducted at 3 different locations with a final non-precision approach at the point of origin
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements

question: For fixed wing IFR ratings, Group 3 would allow you to fly which of the following?
answers: Only center line thrust multi engine aircraft
explanation: Ref: CARS 421.46 Requirements general

question: What is the validity duration for an IFR licence holder?
explanation: Ref: https://tc.canada.ca/en/aviation/reference-centre/advisory-circulars/advisory-circular-ac-no-401-005

question: The validity period for an IFR licence holder is 24 months, after 12 months following a flight test the 6 month requirement becomes in effect. What does the 6 month requirement involve?
answers: You must have completed at least 6 hours of instrument time and 6 instrument approaches
Within the 6 months you must have completed at least one instrument approach to minima
During the 6 months you must have completed 6 hours of instrument time in the plane and six approaches to minima
You must complete 10 hours of instrument time in the simulator within the 6 months
explanation: Ref: https://tc.canada.ca/en/aviation/reference-centre/advisory-circulars/advisory-circular-ac-no-401-005 Remember 6,6 & 6 Rule

question: In order for a pilot to fly day IFR the minimum equipment list must include the day VFR minimum equipment(CARS 605.16(1)(a) to (h)) plus which of the following? a) AI b) VSI c) OAT (Outside Air Temp gauge) d) Wing Lighting e) Means of preventing malfunction caused by icing for each airspeed indicating system f) A power failure warning device or vacuum indicator that shows the power available to gyroscopic instruments from each power source g) Alternate static source h) 2 way comms i) Radio navigation equipment able to conduct an approach j) Windshied deicing equipment
explanation: Ref: CARS 605.18 Power Driven Aircraft-IFR

question: Aircraft can utilize Airways and Air-Routes, what is the difference between them?
answers: Airways are only used for IFR aircraft and Air-Routes are for VFR aircraft
Airways are in Controlled Low Level Airspace and Air-Routes are in Uncontrolled Low Level Airspace
Airways are only used for commercial traffic to ease congestion in the air and Air-Routes are used for non-commercial aircraft
Airways are in High Level Airspace (above 18,000ft) and Air-Routes are in Low Level Airspace (Below 18,000ft)
explanation: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 & RAC 2.8.7 Class G Airspace

question: Low Level Airways are in Low Level Airspace. What is the base of this type of airspace?
explanation: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Controlled Airspace

question: Low Level Controlled Airspace starts at 2200AGL and extends up to…?
explanation: Ref: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Controlled Airspace

question: High Level Controlled Airspace is classified into 3 areas. What are these areas called?
answers: Low Control, Medium Control and High Control Airspace
Southern, Northern and Mid-Land High Level Airspace
Northern Control Area, Southern Control Area and Arctic Control Area
Continental Control, Arctic Control and Northern Control Area
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.6 High Level Controlled Airspace

question: What flight altitude does the Arctic Control Area Airspace begin at?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.6 High Level Controlled Airspace

question: The vertical dimensions of the Southern Control Area begin above…?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.6 High Level Controlled Airspace

question: Low Level Airways use VHF/UHF for Victor Airways, what are the lateral dimensions of this airway?
explanation: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Controlled Airspace Fig 2.5(a)

question: How many degrees splay does a VHF/UHF Airways or Air-Routes have?
explanation: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Controlled Airspace Fig 2.5(a)

question: Control Area Extensions are based at ……………, unless otherwise specified and extend up to, but not including……………...What are the missing figures?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.7.2 Control Area Extensions

question: How much primary obstacle clearance does a T-Route have?
explanation: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Controlled Airspace Fig 2.7(c) Fixed RNAV Route

question: In addition to the 4NM of primary protection for a Low Level Controlled fixed RNAV route (T-Route), how much secondary protection is provided?
explanation: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Controlled Airspace Fig 2.7(c) Fixed RNAV Route

question: T-Routes airspace and protection areas do not splay, as they do with VHF/UHF and LF/MF Airways or Air-Routes. What is the airspace distance either side of the centerline?
explanation: Ref: AIM - RAC 2.7.1 Low Level Controlled Airspace Fig 2.7(c) Fixed RNAV Route

question: What are the vertical dimensions of the Northern Control Area?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.7.2 Control Area Extensions

question: When operating in Mountainous Regions 1 & 5, how high above the highest obstacle must an aircraft be flown within 5NM of flight?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.12 Mountainous Regions

question: While flying in Mountainous Regions 2,3 & 4 you must apply………… the highest obstacle within 5NM of intended route of flight. What is the missing figure?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.12 Mountainous Regions

question: When flying IFR on Airways or Air-Routes, aircraft can operate at the MOCA or MEA, which ever is applicable. But these are effective whilst in ISA standard regions. If the temperature is much lower than ISA, how much altitude do you add to your MEA or MOCA?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 3.15.6 Item 15: Cruising Speed, Altitude/Level and Route – notes section below

question: The MOCA is the minimum obstacle clearance altitude. When operating in Non-Mountainous Regions, how much altitude is added above the highest obstacle?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 8.5 Minimum Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Altitudes

question: What does the abbreviation MEA mean?
explanation: Ref: FGU pg 115 – Minimum Cruising Altitudes

question: The MEA provides obstacle clearance of 1000ft above the highest obstacle. What else does the MEA also provide for navigation along Airways and Air-Routes?
answers: This altitude ensures satisfactory radio signal along the chosen route
It also provides distance measuring between fixes
The MEA allows the aircraft to receive ACARS
It allows the pilot to deviate from an altitude if they encounter icing
explanation: Ref: FGU pg 115 – Minimum Cruising Altitudes

question: When operating VFR or IFR in Non-Mountainous Regions, how much altitude must you apply to the highest obstacle within the lateral dimensions of an Airway or Air-Route?
answers: No additional altitude is added as its the MOCA and includes 1000ft
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 8.5 Minimum Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Altitudes

question: When looking at an IFR low chart, you notice that there is a MOCA and an MEA figure. Why does the chart have two different numbers?
answers: The MEA is below the MOCA so both are included
The Air Route or Airway is operated in mountainous regions
The MOCA is below the MEA so both are included
There is always a difference between the MOCA & MEA
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 8.5 Minimum Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Altitudes

question: Your aircraft is operating in the Standard Pressure Region. Immediately prior to reaching the cruising flight level, the pilot should set the altimeter to the standard pressure. But your cruising flight level is above FL180. When would you set the altimeter in this case?
answers: You have already set the altimeter to standard pressure before you have reached your cruise. So no need to change the altimeter again.
You would set the altimeter immediately after passing FL180.
You would not change the altimeter setting from the airport elevation you have set.
Commercial flights are required to have an automatic radio altimeter. The changes are completed automatically.
explanation: Ref: CARS 602.36 Standard Pressure Regions

question: Class A airspace is defined as all High Level Controlled Airspace within Southern Domestic Airspace. What are the flight levels included for Class A airspace?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.0 Airspace- Requirements and procedures 2.8.1 Airspace Classification

question: What is the base level of a Transition Area in Low Level Controlled Airspace?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.0 Airspace- Requirements and procedures 2.7.5 Low Level Airspace – Transition Areas

question: What is the base level of a Control Area Extension in Low Level Controlled Airspace?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.0 Airspace- Requirements and procedures 2.7.6 Low Level Airspace – Control Area Extensions

question: What is the general radius of a Control Zone?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.0 Airspace- Requirements and procedures 2.7.3 Low Level Airspace – Control Zones

question: Control Zones extend from the surface up to…?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.0 Airspace- Requirements and procedures 2.7.3 Low Level Airspace – Control Zones

question: You have noticed that, when initiating a climb, the altimeter has not shown any change and you suspect icing. What has happened to the Pitot static system in this case?
answers: The Static port has experienced a complete blockage
The Pitot tube has experienced a complete blockage
explanation: Ref: FGU pg 39 & 40 - Flight Instruments – Pitot Static Instruments Remember all the complete and partial blockages of the Pitot Static System and how they affect every instrument!

question: The Pitot tube has a complete blockage, what indication of this does the airspeed indicator give?
answers: The airspeed indicator will over-read in a climb
The airspeed indicator will under-read in a climb
The airspeed indicator will stay frozen at the last altitude
explanation: Ref: FGU pg 39 & 40 - Flight Instruments – Pitot Static Instruments Remember all the complete and partial blockages of the Pitot Static System and how they affect every instrument!

question: When a controller has said your aircraft is “RADAR IDENTIFIED”, who has responsibility for obstacle clearance?
answers: The controller takes the responsibility for obstacle clearance
The controller only ensures obstacle clearance on radar vectors only
The controller only ensures collision avoidance
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 1.5 Radar Service

question: You are being vectored while en-route for arrival, who has responsibility for obstacle clearance in this case?
answers: The controller takes responsibility for terrain and obstacle clearance while vectoring
The controller only provides traffic separation
The controller never takes responsibility for traffic avoidance and obstacle clearance
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 1.5 Radar Service- 1.5.2 - Procedures

question: The term “CAVOK” is referred to when…?
answers: No clouds below 10,000ft, Vis 6SM or greater and no thunderstorms
Few clouds below 10,000ft, Vis equal to or greater than 6SM and no fog or snow
No clouds below 5,000ft, Vis 6SM or more and no precip, thunderstorms, low fog or drifting snow
CAVOK just means no significant weather predicted within the next 24 hours
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 1.4 Use of the term “CAVOK”

question: Your aircraft is being vectored for an instrument approach. The clearance to start the approach has not been issued yet, so who takes responsibility for collision avoidance and obstacle clearance until the clearance for approach is issued?
answers: The pilot-in-command until the clearance is issued
The ATC provides collision and obstacle clearance until the clearance is issued
Radar vectoring only provides the pilot with obstacle clearance until the clearance is issued
Radar vectoring means the pilot has only collision avoidance assistance until the clearance is issued
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 1.5.5 Obstacle Clearance During Radar Vectors

question: A clearance has been issued and you notice an aircraft converging at your altitude, what actions are you allowed to take?
answers: The clearance has already been issued so you must follow it
You can deviate from your clearance and just continue once the issue in air has been solved
Inform ATC you have to deviate from the clearance issued
You must initiate the avoidance only if TCAS or ACAS RA’s and call for another clearance
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 1.7 Air Traffic Controller (ATC) Clearances, Instructions and Information

question: Transponder codes are used for VFR and IFR flights. Your aircraft is flying IFR above 18,000ft ASL, in this case what is the transponder code?
explanation: Ref: AIM COM 8.0 Transponder Operations – 8.2 Transponder Requirements & CARS 601.03 Low Level IFR Code - 1000 High Level IFR Code - 2000

question: The aircraft is flying IFR in Low Level Controlled Airspace and has not been given a transponder code. What would be the correct transponder code?
answers: You must receive a transponder code as the flight is IFR
explanation: Ref: AIM COM 8.0 Transponder Operations – 8.2 Transponder Requirements & CARS 601.03

question: The aircraft is flying IFR and the heading is 165 degrees magnetic. What could be the flight levels associated with this direction of flight?
explanation: Ref: FGU - Air Rules and Procedures pg 114 – Cruising Altitudes - Canada

question: An IFR flight in Uncontrolled Airspace is flying above FL290, on a heading of 350 degrees magnetic. What would be the first appropriate flight level in this case?
explanation: Ref: FGU - Air Rules and Procedures pg 114 – Cruising Altitudes – Canada – IFR at altitudes above FL290 Cruising altitude for IFR on Easterly headings begin at FL310 and are 4,000ft intervals

question: When flight planning in Northern Domestic Airspace (NDA), you must pick an appropriate cruising altitude. What do you choose for the track or heading for NDA?
explanation: Ref: FGU Aerodromes & Airspace - The Canadian Airspace System pg 99

question: The basic width of an LF/MF Airway is 4.34NM either side of the centre line outwards to a distance of 49.66NM. How many degrees splay does an LF/MF Airway have?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC Low Level Airways – Figure 2.6 LF/MF Airway Dimensions Remember you must memorize VHF/UHF dimensions as well as LF/MF and know which is used for what navigational aid!

question: A Victor Airway has been established for a VOR to VOR Navigational Airway. How many degrees splay and what is the distance from the centre line?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.7 – Low Level Controlled Airspace- 2.7.1 – Low Level Airways

question: A transition area or control zone that does not have an operating control tower is considered to be what type of airspace?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.8.5 - Class E Airspace

question: Your aircraft is operating IFR in Class E Airspace. Does the operating ATC give obstacle clearance and collision avoidance?
answers: Yes, you are radar identified so ATC takes the responsibility of both
Yes, but only if requested as you are in uncontrolled airspace
No, you are only provided with traffic separation assistance
No, you are responsible for obstacle, collision avoidance and traffic separation
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.8.5 - Class E Airspace

question: Low Level Air Routes are in Uncontrolled Class G Airspace. Which statement is correct?
answers: They extend from 2,200ft AGL above the Earths surface
They extend from the Earths surface
They extend from 2,000ft AGL towards FL600
Class G Airspace begins at 3,000ft AGL
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.8.7 Class G Airspace

question: The PIC is allowed to file an IFR flight itinerary instead of a flight plan, when would you file an IFR flight itinerary?
answers: When the flight is no more than 25NM from origin
When the flight is taking place in Northern Domestic Airspace only
When the flight is not taking passengers
When the flight is taken, in part or in whole, outside uncontrolled airspace
explanation: Ref: CARS 602.73 AIM RAC 3.5 Flight Plans and Flight Itineraries – 3.51 When Required

question: When filing an IFR flight plan or itinerary the pilot should preferable file at least …mins before departure time. How many minutes?
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 3.6.2 Filing & CARS 602.75

question: When calculating the fuel requirements for an IFR flight in a propeller driven aircraft, what is the correct statement when an alternate has been filed?
answers: To fly and execute an approach at the destination and continue to fly for 45 mins thereafter

To fly to and execute an approach and a missed approach at the destination, to fly to and land at the alternate, then fly for a period of 45 mins at cruise power plus sufficient fuel for any anticipated delays
To fly to the destination, execute an approach then fly to alternate and land
To fly to the destination and execute an approach, missed approach at the destination and fly to the alternate and land
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 3.12.2 – Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Flight (a) (i)

question: When calculating the fuel requirements for an IFR flight in a propeller driven aircraft, what is the correct statement when an alternate has NOT been filed?
answers: To fly to the destination and execute an approach and missed approach only
To fly to the destination and execute an approach and missed approach and fly at cruise for 30 mins
To fly to the destination and execute an approach and missed approach and fly at cruise for 45 mins
To fly to the destination and execute an approach and missed approach then fly at cruise for 60 mins
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 3.12.2 – Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Flight (a) (ii)

question: When calculating the fuel requirements for an IFR flight in a turbo jet aircraft, what is the correct statement when an alternate has been filed?
answers: To fly and execute an approach at the destination and continue to fly for 30 mins thereafter
To fly to the destination and execute an approach, missed approach at the destination and fly to the alternate and land
To fly to the destination and execute an approach and missed approach then fly at cruise for 60 mins

To fly to and execute an approach and a missed approach at the destination, to fly to and land at the alternate, then fly for a period of 30 mins at cruise power plus sufficient fuel for any anticipated delays
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 3.12.2 – Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Flight (b) (i)

question: When calculating the fuel requirements for an IFR flight in a turbo jet aircraft, what is the correct statement when an alternate has NOT been filed?
answers: To fly to and execute an approach and a missed approach at the destination, then fly for a period of 30 mins at cruise power plus sufficient fuel for any anticipated delays
To fly to the destination and execute an approach, missed approach at the destination and fly to the alternate and land
To fly to the destination and execute an approach and missed approach then fly at cruise for 60 mins
To fly to and execute an approach and a missed approach at the destination, then fly for a period of 45 mins at cruise power plus sufficient fuel for any anticipated delays
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 3.12.2 – Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Flight (b) (ii)

question: You are the PIC of an IFR flight, you must deviate from the ATC clearance due to an ACAS warning. What would you do in order to remain safe in the situation?
answers: Continue as the clearance must be followed in any circumstance and ATC has complete responsibility for obstacle clearance
You must proceed to the missed approach point and hold in a standard pattern until a new clearance has been approved
Immediately take the required action and inform ATC as soon as practicable
You must declare an emergency on 7700 as this is an IFR flight
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 6.0 Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) – General 6.1 Air Traffic Control (ATC) Clearance CARS 602.31

question: You has filed an IFR flight plan and you are not in controlled airspace class A or B. Your aircraft has now entered VMC and is within 25NM of the destination airport. You decide to cancel your IFR flight plan and continue VFR. Which statement is correct in this situation?
answers: You must re-file a VFR flight plan in the air
You will continue on a flight plan but no longer receive ATC traffic separation and must close the flight plan as it is still active
You do not need to change your flight plan but must revise your SARS time
Your Flight plan has been cancelled and you continue as VFR and land
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 6.0 Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) – Flights in Visual Meteorological Conditions (VMC)

question: An aircraft has been delayed in receiving its IFR clearance, the PIC decides to request a VFR departure and it is approved by ATC. What information would ATC give in order for you to receive the IFR clearance and continue IFR as planned?
answers: They would give a time, altitude or location in order to receive the IFR clearance
You continue until you are in IMC and then contact for IFR clearance
You may not continue IFR unless you file a new flight plan in the air
As soon as you depart VFR you must wait 15 minutes until you call ATC for the IFR clearance
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 6.2.2 Visual Flight Rules (VFR) – Release of an Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) Aircraft

question: You are on a IFR flight plan and experience a two-way communications failure. In order to remain safe the pilot must use good judgment. Which of the following would be acceptable and safe in this situation?
answers: Land at a suitable airport if in VMC
Call ATC with your cell phone
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 6.3.2 Two-Way Communications Failure

question: For an aircraft to operate IFR in Class F(R) Airspace, the operator must…?
answers: Have obtained prior permission from the user agency
Or have been cleared for a contact or visual approach
Maintain a listening watch on the appropriate frequency and broadcast intentions when entering or leaving the airspace
explanation: Ref: AIM RAC 2.8.6 Class F Airspace –Advisory Airspace

question: Portable electronic devices are allowed on board but who has the responsibility to make sure they do not interfere with the aircrafts systems?
explanation: Ref: CARS 602.08 & Advisory Circular (AC) No. 700-005 – 4.0 Operational Requirements
